Walk forward; the moment you hit a balloon whose start is beyond the current arrow's position, that balloon can't be reached, so you need a new arrow placed at this balloon's end. This greedy "shoot at the earliest ending, cover as much as possible" choice is provably optimal.


class Solution {
public:
    int findMinArrowShots(vector<vector<int>>& points) {
        if (points.empty()) return 0;
        sort(points.begin(), points.end(),
             [](const vector<int>& leftBalloon, const vector<int>& rightBalloon) {
                 return leftBalloon[1] < rightBalloon[1];
             });
        int arrows = 1;
        long long arrowPosition = points[0][1];
        for (const vector<int>& balloon : points) {
            int balloonStart = balloon[0];
            int balloonEnd = balloon[1];
            if (balloonStart > arrowPosition) {
                arrows++;
                arrowPosition = balloonEnd;
            }
        }
        return arrows;
    }
};




Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1) extra. Justification: sorting dominates; the sweep is one O(n) pass with only a couple of scalar variables (sort stack is O(log n)).


Interview Notes.


    

  • The decisive choice is sorting by end, not start — shooting at the earliest end greedily covers the most that can possibly be co-burst. Be ready to justify why this greedy is optimal (an exchange argument: any optimal arrow can be slid left to the earliest end without losing coverage).
    • 

  • Use a long long (or compare carefully) for the arrow position: LeetCode's coordinates can reach INT_MAX, and comparing balloonStart > arrowPosition is fine, but width arithmetic elsewhere could overflow.
    • 

  • The overlap test is balloonStart > arrowPosition for "needs a new arrow"; touching balloons (start == end of previous) share an arrow.
    • 

  • This is the mirror image of the "maximum non-overlapping intervals" / activity-selection problem — recognizing that connection scores points.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Stack›Problem 52 of 150


52
Valid Parentheses
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergMetaAdobe




Problem. Given a string containing only the six bracket characters (, ), {, }, [, ], decide whether they are balanced. Balanced means every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket of the same type, and brackets close in the correct nested order. Constraints: length 1 to 10^4, only those six characters.


Example. Input "{[]}" → Output true. Trace: push {, push [, see ] which matches top [ so pop, see } which matches top { so pop, stack empty at end → balanced.




Hint 1Brackets must close in the reverse order they opened — that's a stack signature.


Hint 2Push opens, pop and match on closes.


Hint 3Push every opening bracket; on a closing bracket pop and verify it matches the expected open, failing if the stack is empty or mismatched; the stack must end empty.






Brute Force. Repeatedly scan the string and delete any adjacent matching pair ((), [], {}). Keep looping until no more pairs can be removed; if the final string is empty the input was balanced. This mimics collapsing the innermost pairs over and over.


class Solution {
public:
    bool isValid(string inputString) {
        bool removedSomething = true;
        while (removedSomething) {
            removedSomething = false;
            for (int position = 0; position + 1 < (int)inputString.size(); ++position) {
                string pair = inputString.substr(position, 2);
                if (pair == "()" || pair == "[]" || pair == "{}") {
                    inputString.erase(position, 2);
                    removedSomething = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return inputString.empty();
    }
};




Time: O(n^2) — each removal restarts the scan and each scan is O(n), with up to n/2 removals. Space: O(n) for the modified string copy.


Optimized. Use a stack. Push every opening bracket. When you hit a closing bracket, the matching opener must be on top of the stack; if the stack is empty or the top is the wrong type, the string is invalid. At the end the stack must be empty (no unmatched openers left).


class Solution {
public:
    bool isValid(string inputString) {
        stack<char> openBrackets;
        for (char currentChar : inputString) {
            if (currentChar == '(' || currentChar == '[' || currentChar == '{') {
                openBrackets.push(currentChar);
            } else {
                if (openBrackets.empty()) return false;
                char topBracket = openBrackets.top();
                if ((currentChar == ')' && topBracket != '(') ||
                    (currentChar == ']' && topBracket != '[') ||
                    (currentChar == '}' && topBracket != '{')) {
                    return false;
                }
                openBrackets.pop();
            }
        }
        return openBrackets.empty();
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each character is pushed and popped at most once. Space: O(n) — worst case all characters are openers and sit on the stack.


Interview Notes.


    

  • This is the canonical "stack for matching" problem — recognize the pattern instantly.
    • 

  • Two failure modes besides a type mismatch: a closing bracket arriving with an empty stack, and leftover openers at the end. Forgetting either is the classic bug.
    • 

  • A std::unordered_map<char,char> mapping each closer to its opener can shrink the if-chain to one comparison.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Stack›Problem 53 of 150


53
Simplify Path
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergApple




Problem. Given an absolute Unix-style file path string, return its canonical (simplified) form. Rules: . means current directory (ignore it), .. means go up one level (remove the previous directory), multiple slashes collapse into one, and the result must start with a single / and not end with a trailing / (unless it is the root /).


Example. Input "/a/./b/../../c/" → Output "/c". Trace: enter a, . ignored, enter b, .. removes b, .. removes a, enter c → directories left are ["c"] → "/c".




Hint 1Treat the path as a sequence of components separated by slashes.


Hint 2A stack models directory entry and exit.


Hint 3Split on '/'; ignore empty and '.', pop on '..' (if non-empty), and push real names; rejoin with '/' prefix.






Brute Force. Repeatedly apply string-level rewrites: replace // with /, replace /./ with /, and find each /dir/../ and delete it, looping until the string stops changing. This is intuitive but fragile with edge cases at the ends.


class Solution {
public:
    string simplifyPath(string path) {
        bool changed = true;
        while (changed) {
            changed = false;
            size_t doubleSlash = path.find("//");
            if (doubleSlash != string::npos) { path.erase(doubleSlash, 1); changed = true; continue; }
            size_t dotSegment = path.find("/./");
            if (dotSegment != string::npos) { path.erase(dotSegment, 2); changed = true; continue; }
        }
        if (path.size() > 1 && path.back() == '/') path.pop_back();
        return path.empty() ? "/" : path;
    }
};




Time: O(n^2) in the worst case — each rewrite restarts the search and may shift the whole string. Space: O(n). This version also does not correctly resolve .., illustrating how brittle pure string surgery becomes.


Optimized. Split the path on / into segments and process them with a stack of directory names. Skip empty segments and .. For .., pop the stack if it is non-empty (going up). For any real name, push it. Finally join the stack with / and prepend a leading slash.


class Solution {
public:
    string simplifyPath(string path) {
        vector<string> directoryStack;
        stringstream pathStream(path);
        string segment;
        while (getline(pathStream, segment, '/')) {
            if (segment.empty() || segment == ".") {
                continue;
            } else if (segment == "..") {
                if (!directoryStack.empty()) directoryStack.pop_back();
            } else {
                directoryStack.push_back(segment);
            }
        }
        string result;
        for (const string& directoryName : directoryStack) {
            result += "/" + directoryName;
        }
        return result.empty() ? "/" : result;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each character is read once during splitting and each segment is handled once. Space: O(n) for the stack of segments and the output string.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Use a vector<string> instead of std::stack here because you must iterate bottom-to-top to build the final string — std::stack only exposes the top.
    • 

  • getline(stream, token, '/') naturally yields empty tokens for // and a leading /, so the segment.empty() check handles slash collapsing for free.
    • 

  • .. at the root is a no-op (empty stack), not an error — a common edge case interviewers probe.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Stack›Problem 54 of 150


54
Min Stack
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAppleAdobe




Problem. Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin (retrieve the minimum element currently in the stack), all in O(1) time. The tricky part is getMin must stay O(1) even as elements are pushed and popped.


Example. push(-2), push(0), push(-3), getMin() → -3; pop(), top() → 0, getMin() → -2. The minimum changes as the smallest element is popped off.




Hint 1getMin in O(1) means you must carry the minimum alongside the stack.


Hint 2Track the running minimum per element.


Hint 3Use an auxiliary stack (or store pairs) where each push also records the min so far; pop both together and read the top of the min stack for getMin.






Brute Force. Store values in one stack and compute the minimum by scanning the whole stack each time getMin is called.


class MinStack {
    vector<int> values;
public:
    MinStack() {}
    void push(int val) { values.push_back(val); }
    void pop() { values.pop_back(); }
    int top() { return values.back(); }
    int getMin() {
        int minimum = values[0];
        for (int value : values) minimum = min(minimum, value);
        return minimum;
    }
};




Time: push/pop/top are O(1), but getMin is O(n). Space: O(n). This fails the O(1) getMin requirement.


Optimized. Keep a second stack that tracks the minimum so far. On each push, also push the smaller of the new value and the current minimum onto the min-stack; the two stacks stay the same height. On pop, pop both. Now getMin is just the top of the min-stack.


class MinStack {
    stack<int> mainStack;
    stack<int> minimumStack;
public:
    MinStack() {}
    void push(int val) {
        mainStack.push(val);
        if (minimumStack.empty()) {
            minimumStack.push(val);
        } else {
            minimumStack.push(min(val, minimumStack.top()));
        }
    }
    void pop() {
        mainStack.pop();
        minimumStack.pop();
    }
    int top() {
        return mainStack.top();
    }
    int getMin() {
        return minimumStack.top();
    }
};




Time: all four operations O(1). Space: O(n) for the two parallel stacks.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The key idea is storing the running minimum alongside each element, so popping automatically restores the previous minimum without rescanning.
    • 

  • A space optimization stores (value, minSoFar) as pairs in one stack — same effect, one container.
    • 

  • A more advanced trick stores deltas relative to the current min using long long to avoid overflow; mention it if asked to reduce memory, but the two-stack version is the clearest to write correctly.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Stack›Problem 55 of 150


55
Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleLinkedInBloombergAdobe




Problem. Evaluate an arithmetic expression given in Reverse Polish Notation (postfix), where each operator comes after its two operands. Tokens are an array of strings, each either an integer or one of +, -, *, /. Division truncates toward zero. The expression is always valid.


Example. Input ["2","1","+","3","*"] → Output 9. Trace: push 2, push 1, + pops 1 and 2 → push 3, push 3, * pops 3 and 3 → push 9. Equivalent to (2 + 1) * 3.




Hint 1RPN is naturally evaluated with a stack.


Hint 2Operands push, operators pop two and combine.


Hint 3Scan tokens; push numbers, and on an operator pop the top two (mind operand order for - and /), apply it, and push the result; the final stack value is the answer.






Brute Force. There is no fundamentally different approach — postfix is designed to be stack-evaluated. A naive alternative would convert the tokens back to infix and parse with operator precedence, which is far more code. The "brute" version below shows the same stack logic but with a long explicit if-chain and no helper, to contrast with the clean version.


class Solution {
public:
    int evalRPN(vector<string>& tokens) {
        stack<int> operandStack;
        for (const string& token : tokens) {
            if (token == "+" || token == "-" || token == "*" || token == "/") {
                int rightOperand = operandStack.top(); operandStack.pop();
                int leftOperand = operandStack.top(); operandStack.pop();
                int computed = 0;
                if (token == "+") computed = leftOperand + rightOperand;
                else if (token == "-") computed = leftOperand - rightOperand;
                else if (token == "*") computed = leftOperand * rightOperand;
                else computed = leftOperand / rightOperand;
                operandStack.push(computed);
            } else {
                operandStack.push(stoi(token));
            }
        }
        return operandStack.top();
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one pass over the tokens. Space: O(n) for the operand stack.


Optimized. The algorithm is already optimal at O(n); the refinement is clarity. Use std::stoll and a long long accumulator to guard against intermediate overflow, and note the critical ordering detail: the first value popped is the right operand, so for - and / the order is left OP right, not the reverse.


class Solution {
public:
    int evalRPN(vector<string>& tokens) {
        stack<long long> operandStack;
        for (const string& token : tokens) {
            if (token == "+" || token == "-" || token == "*" || token == "/") {
                long long rightOperand = operandStack.top(); operandStack.pop();
                long long leftOperand = operandStack.top(); operandStack.pop();
                if (token == "+") operandStack.push(leftOperand + rightOperand);
                else if (token == "-") operandStack.push(leftOperand - rightOperand);
                else if (token == "*") operandStack.push(leftOperand * rightOperand);
                else operandStack.push(leftOperand / rightOperand);
            } else {
                operandStack.push(stoll(token));
            }
        }
        return (int)operandStack.top();
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(n).


Interview Notes.


    

  • The single most common bug is reversing operands for non-commutative - and /. Always pop right first, then left.
    • 

  • C++ integer division already truncates toward zero for the cases LeetCode uses, matching the spec — no extra rounding needed.
    • 

  • Negative-number tokens (like "-4") are operands; check that a token is an operator before trying to parse it as a number, or guard against single-character -.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Stack›Problem 56 of 150


56
Basic Calculator
Hard


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergApple




Problem. Implement a calculator that evaluates a string expression containing non-negative integers, +, -, parentheses ( ), and spaces. There is no * or /, but unary minus and arbitrarily nested parentheses are allowed. Return the integer result.


Example. Input "(1+(4+5+2)-3)+(6+8)" → Output 23. Inner (4+5+2)=11, so first group 1+11-3=9, second group 6+8=14, total 23.




Hint 1The hard parts are nested parentheses and unary signs.


Hint 2A stack can save the running result and sign before entering parentheses.


Hint 3Track a running result, current number, and sign; on '(' push result and sign then reset, on ')' pop and combine, adding numbers with their sign as you parse digits.






Brute Force. A natural first attempt is full recursive-descent parsing: write a function that finds the matching closing parenthesis for each (, recursively evaluates the substring inside, then sums the resulting terms. It works but re-scans substrings and allocates many recursive calls.


class Solution {
public:
    int calculate(string expression) {
        int position = 0;
        return evaluate(expression, position);
    }
private:
    int evaluate(const string& expression, int& position) {
        int result = 0;
        int sign = 1;
        while (position < (int)expression.size()) {
            char currentChar = expression[position];
            if (isdigit(currentChar)) {
                int number = 0;
                while (position < (int)expression.size() && isdigit(expression[position])) {
                    number = number * 10 + (expression[position] - '0');
                    ++position;
                }
                result += sign * number;
                continue;
            } else if (currentChar == '+') {
                sign = 1;
            } else if (currentChar == '-') {
                sign = -1;
            } else if (currentChar == '(') {
                ++position;
                result += sign * evaluate(expression, position);
            } else if (currentChar == ')') {
                ++position;
                return result;
            }
            ++position;
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each character is visited once even across recursion. Space: O(n) for the recursion stack on deeply nested input. (This recursive form is actually efficient; it is labelled "brute" only as the more obvious first solution.)


Optimized. Use an explicit stack to handle parentheses iteratively, avoiding recursion depth limits. Keep a running result, a current sign (+1 or -1), and a number buffer. On (, push the current result and sign, then reset them to start a fresh sub-expression. On ), finalize the inner result, then pop the saved sign and outer result to fold the inner value back in.


class Solution {
public:
    int calculate(string expression) {
        stack<int> savedState;
        int result = 0;
        int sign = 1;
        int currentNumber = 0;
        for (char currentChar : expression) {
            if (isdigit(currentChar)) {
                currentNumber = currentNumber * 10 + (currentChar - '0');
            } else if (currentChar == '+') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                sign = 1;
            } else if (currentChar == '-') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                sign = -1;
            } else if (currentChar == '(') {
                savedState.push(result);
                savedState.push(sign);
                result = 0;
                sign = 1;
            } else if (currentChar == ')') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                int savedSign = savedState.top(); savedState.pop();
                int savedResult = savedState.top(); savedState.pop();
                result = savedResult + savedSign * result;
            }
        }
        return result + sign * currentNumber;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one pass over the string. Space: O(n) — the stack grows with parenthesis nesting depth.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The mental model: a ( opens a new accumulation context; you stash the outer result and the sign that multiplies the whole upcoming group, then start fresh at zero.
    • 

  • Multi-digit parsing means you only commit currentNumber into result when you hit an operator or ) — don't forget the final commit after the loop ends.
    • 

  • Spaces are simply skipped because they match none of the branches; no explicit handling needed.
    • 

  • This is the no-multiply/divide variant. Its sibling Basic Calculator II adds * and / (handled with a running-stack-of-terms approach), and Basic Calculator III combines both — mention the family if asked to generalize.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 57 of 150


57
Linked List Cycle
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAppleAdobe




Problem. Given the head of a linked list, return true if the list contains a cycle (some node's next eventually points back to an earlier node, so a walk never ends) and false otherwise.
Example. List 3 -> 2 -> 0 -> -4, where -4->next points back to the node 2. Walking from the head we loop forever, so the answer is true.
Brute Force. Walk the list and remember every node address we have already visited in a hash set. If we ever reach a node already in the set, there is a cycle; if we hit nullptr, there is not.


bool hasCycle(ListNode* head) {
    unordered_set<ListNode*> visited;
    ListNode* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (visited.count(current)) {
            return true;
        }
        visited.insert(current);
        current = current->next;
    }
    return false;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). We touch each node once, but the set can grow to hold every node.




Hint 1A cycle means a fast traveler eventually laps a slow one.


Hint 2Use two pointers moving at different speeds.


Hint 3Advance a slow pointer one step and a fast pointer two steps; if they ever meet there's a cycle, if fast reaches null there isn't (Floyd's algorithm).






Optimized. The key insight is Floyd's cycle detection (the "tortoise and hare"). Move a slow pointer one step and a fast pointer two steps per iteration. If there is no cycle, fast reaches the end. If there is a cycle, fast keeps lapping the loop and must eventually land on the same node as slow.


bool hasCycle(ListNode* head) {
    ListNode* slow = head;
    ListNode* fast = head;
    while (fast != nullptr && fast->next != nullptr) {
        slow = slow->next;
        fast = fast->next->next;
        if (slow == fast) {
            return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Each pointer travels at most a linear number of steps, and we store only two pointers.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Always guard both fast != nullptr and fast->next != nullptr before doing fast->next->next, or you will dereference null.
    • 

  • The pointers are compared by address (slow == fast), not by value.
    • 

  • A common follow-up (LeetCode 142) asks for the node where the cycle begins: reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time until they meet again.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 58 of 150


58
Add Two Numbers
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftBloombergGoogleMetaApple




Problem. Two non-empty lists represent non-negative integers with digits stored in reverse order (ones digit first). Add the numbers and return the sum as a list in the same reverse order.
Example. 2 -> 4 -> 3 is 342 and 5 -> 6 -> 4 is 465. Their sum is 807, returned as 7 -> 0 -> 8.
Brute Force. The tempting naive route is to walk both lists building the full integers, add them, then split the result back into digits. This breaks immediately because the numbers can have hundreds of digits and overflow any built-in integer type, so it is not a viable solution here. We still need digit-by-digit addition; the version below is the straightforward one.


ListNode* addTwoNumbers(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* current = &dummy;
    int carry = 0;
    while (firstList != nullptr || secondList != nullptr || carry != 0) {
        int sum = carry;
        if (firstList != nullptr) {
            sum += firstList->val;
            firstList = firstList->next;
        }
        if (secondList != nullptr) {
            sum += secondList->val;
            secondList = secondList->next;
        }
        carry = sum / 10;
        current->next = new ListNode(sum % 10);
        current = current->next;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(max(m, n)), Space: O(max(m, n)). We process one digit per step and allocate one output node per digit.




Hint 1Digits are stored least-significant first, which is convenient for addition.


Hint 2Process both lists together carrying over like grade-school addition.


Hint 3Walk both lists, summing corresponding digits plus carry, creating result nodes; don't forget a final node if a carry remains.






Optimized. The code above is already optimal; the pattern that makes it clean is the dummy head. We build the result behind a throwaway dummy node so we never special-case the first digit, and we return dummy.next. The single loop condition || carry != 0 elegantly handles a final carry (e.g. 5 + 5 = 10 producing a new leading digit).


Time: O(max(m, n)), Space: O(max(m, n)). Same as above; there is no asymptotically better approach because every output digit must be created.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The carry is 0 or 1 here, but writing carry = sum / 10 generalizes cleanly.
    • 

  • Declaring ListNode dummy; on the stack avoids a leak from a new dummy you would otherwise have to delete.
    • 

  • Watch the loop condition: forgetting carry != 0 drops the final carry digit.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 59 of 150


59
Merge Two Sorted Lists
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftAppleGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given the heads of two lists sorted in non-decreasing order, splice them into one sorted list and return its head. The result should reuse the existing nodes.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 4 and 1 -> 3 -> 4 merge into 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4.
Brute Force. Collect every value from both lists into a vector, sort it, then build a fresh list from the sorted values.


ListNode* mergeTwoLists(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    vector<int> values;
    for (ListNode* current = firstList; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        values.push_back(current->val);
    }
    for (ListNode* current = secondList; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        values.push_back(current->val);
    }
    sort(values.begin(), values.end());
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (int value : values) {
        tail->next = new ListNode(value);
        tail = tail->next;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O((m + n) log(m + n)), Space: O(m + n). The sort dominates, and we allocate a whole new list.




Hint 1Both lists are sorted, so merge by always taking the smaller head.


Hint 2A dummy head node simplifies the stitching.


Hint 3Use a dummy/tail pointer; repeatedly attach the smaller of the two list heads and advance, then append whatever list remains.






Optimized. The key insight is that both inputs are already sorted, so we can merge with two pointers using a dummy head. Repeatedly compare the front nodes of the two lists, append the smaller one to the result, and advance that list. When one list runs out, attach the remainder of the other.


ListNode* mergeTwoLists(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    while (firstList != nullptr && secondList != nullptr) {
        if (firstList->val <= secondList->val) {
            tail->next = firstList;
            firstList = firstList->next;
        } else {
            tail->next = secondList;
            secondList = secondList->next;
        }
        tail = tail->next;
    }
    tail->next = (firstList != nullptr) ? firstList : secondList;
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(m + n), Space: O(1). We visit each node once and allocate nothing new (only the stack dummy).


Interview Notes.


    

  • Use <= rather than < to keep the merge stable (preserves relative order of equal values).
    • 

  • The final line stitching on the leftover tail is what makes the dummy-head version short; don't loop those nodes one at a time.
    • 

  • This merge step is the core of merge sort on a linked list, a common follow-up.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 60 of 150


60
Copy List with Random Pointer
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergUber




Problem. Each node has the usual next plus a random pointer that may point to any node in the list or to nullptr. Return a deep copy: a brand-new list whose nodes mirror both the next and random structure, sharing no nodes with the original.
Example. If node A's random points to node C, then in the copy, A's clone's random must point to C's clone (not to the original C).
Brute Force. Make a first pass creating each clone and storing a mapping from each original node to its clone in a hash map. Make a second pass to set every clone's next and random by looking up the originals' targets in the map.


class Node {
public:
    int val;
    Node* next;
    Node* random;
    Node(int value) : val(value), next(nullptr), random(nullptr) {}
};

Node* copyRandomList(Node* head) {
    unordered_map<Node*, Node*> originalToClone;
    Node* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        originalToClone[current] = new Node(current->val);
        current = current->next;
    }
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        originalToClone[current]->next = originalToClone[current->next];
        originalToClone[current]->random = originalToClone[current->random];
        current = current->next;
    }
    return originalToClone[head];
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Two linear passes plus a map holding every node. (Looking up originalToClone[nullptr] returns a value-initialized nullptr, which is exactly what we want for end-of-list and null randoms.)




Hint 1The random pointers make a naive copy hard because targets may not exist yet.


Hint 2Interleave copies or use a map from original node to its clone.


Hint 3Either build an old->new hashmap in one pass and wire next/random in a second, or weave cloned nodes between originals, set randoms, then unweave.






Optimized. The clever trick removes the map by interleaving cloned nodes. First weave each clone directly after its original (A -> A' -> B -> B' ...). Now each clone sits right after the node whose random it must mirror, so clone->random = original->random->next. Finally unzip the two lists apart.


Node* copyRandomList(Node* head) {
    if (head == nullptr) {
        return nullptr;
    }
    Node* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        Node* clone = new Node(current->val);
        clone->next = current->next;
        current->next = clone;
        current = clone->next;
    }
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (current->random != nullptr) {
            current->next->random = current->random->next;
        }
        current = current->next->next;
    }
    Node* cloneHead = head->next;
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        Node* clone = current->next;
        current->next = clone->next;
        if (clone->next != nullptr) {
            clone->next = clone->next->next;
        }
        current = current->next;
    }
    return cloneHead;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1) extra. Three linear passes, no auxiliary map (output nodes don't count as extra).


Interview Notes.


    

  • The hash-map version is perfectly acceptable in an interview and far easier to get right under pressure; mention the interleaving trick as the O(1)-space optimization.
    • 

  • In the unzip pass you must restore the original list's next pointers, or you corrupt the input.
    • 

  • Always handle the empty list up front, since head->next would otherwise crash.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 61 of 150


61
Reverse Linked List II
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given the head and two 1-based positions left <= right, reverse only the nodes from position left to position right, leaving the rest intact. Return the new head.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with left = 2, right = 4 becomes 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 5.
Brute Force. Copy the values of the segment into a vector, reverse the vector, and write the values back into the same nodes. This avoids pointer surgery entirely.


ListNode* reverseBetween(ListNode* head, int left, int right) {
    vector<int> segment;
    ListNode* current = head;
    int position = 1;
    while (current != nullptr && position < left) {
        current = current->next;
        position++;
    }
    ListNode* segmentStart = current;
    while (current != nullptr && position <= right) {
        segment.push_back(current->val);
        current = current->next;
        position++;
    }
    current = segmentStart;
    for (int index = segment.size() - 1; index >= 0; index--) {
        current->val = segment[index];
        current = current->next;
    }
    return head;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(right - left + 1). Linear walk plus a vector sized to the segment.




Hint 1Only a sublist between positions left and right needs reversing.


Hint 2Walk to the node before 'left', then reverse in place using head-insertion.


Hint 3Advance a dummy-anchored pointer to just before 'left'; then repeatedly move the node after the reversal region to the front of it, right-left times.






Optimized. The pattern is dummy head + in-place reversal by splicing. Place a dummy before head so left = 1 is not special. Walk a beforeSegment pointer to the node just before position left. Then repeatedly take the node after the current first segment node and splice it to the front of the segment (head-insertion), which reverses the segment in O(1) extra space.


ListNode* reverseBetween(ListNode* head, int left, int right) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* beforeSegment = &dummy;
    for (int step = 1; step < left; step++) {
        beforeSegment = beforeSegment->next;
    }
    ListNode* segmentHead = beforeSegment->next;
    for (int step = 0; step < right - left; step++) {
        ListNode* movedNode = segmentHead->next;
        segmentHead->next = movedNode->next;
        movedNode->next = beforeSegment->next;
        beforeSegment->next = movedNode;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One walk to the segment plus a constant-work splice per reversed node.


Interview Notes.


    

  • segmentHead stays fixed as the node that ends up last in the reversed segment; only movedNode changes each iteration.
    • 

  • The loop runs right - left times, not right - left + 1, because head-insertion of the others is enough.
    • 

  • Returning dummy.next (not head) is essential when left == 1, since the original head moves.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 62 of 150


62
Reverse Nodes in k-Group
Hard


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. Reverse the nodes of the list k at a time and return the modified list. If the number of remaining nodes is fewer than k, leave them as they are. You may not just swap values.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with k = 2 becomes 2 -> 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 5 (the trailing 5 is a leftover group of size 1).
Brute Force. Since values may not be swapped, the conceptually simplest version walks group by group: for each group of k, detach it, reverse it with a standard reversal loop, and reconnect. We implement this with a helper that reverses a fixed count of nodes.


ListNode* reverseKGroup(ListNode* head, int k) {
    ListNode* groupStart = head;
    for (int count = 0; count < k; count++) {
        if (groupStart == nullptr) {
            return head;
        }
        groupStart = groupStart->next;
    }
    ListNode* previous = reverseKGroup(groupStart, k);
    ListNode* current = head;
    for (int count = 0; count < k; count++) {
        ListNode* nextNode = current->next;
        current->next = previous;
        previous = current;
        current = nextNode;
    }
    return previous;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(n / k). Each node is reversed once, but the recursion stacks one frame per group.




Hint 1Reverse only when a full group of k nodes is available.


Hint 2Check group availability first, then reverse that segment and reconnect.


Hint 3Walk k nodes ahead to confirm a full group exists; reverse those k nodes, link the previous group's tail to the new head, and recurse/iterate on the remainder.






Optimized. The iterative version uses a dummy head and a group-counting pointer to avoid recursion's stack space. We keep a groupPrev pointer before each group, check that a full group of k nodes exists by advancing a probe, and if so reverse that group in place and relink it.


ListNode* reverseKGroup(ListNode* head, int k) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* groupPrev = &dummy;
    while (true) {
        ListNode* probe = groupPrev;
        for (int count = 0; count < k && probe != nullptr; count++) {
            probe = probe->next;
        }
        if (probe == nullptr) {
            break;
        }
        ListNode* groupStart = groupPrev->next;
        ListNode* current = groupStart->next;
        for (int count = 0; count < k - 1; count++) {
            groupStart->next = current->next;
            current->next = groupPrev->next;
            groupPrev->next = current;
            current = groupStart->next;
        }
        groupPrev = groupStart;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Each node is moved a constant number of times and we keep only a few pointers.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The probe loop confirming a full group is what implements "leave a short tail alone".
    • 

  • After reversing, groupStart is the node that became the group's tail, so it is the correct next groupPrev.
    • 

  • The recursive version is easier to write under pressure; state the O(n/k) stack cost when you offer it.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 63 of 150


63
Remove Nth Node From End of List
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Remove the n-th node counting from the end of the list and return the head. It is guaranteed n is valid.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with n = 2 removes the 4, giving 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 5.
Brute Force. Walk once to count the length L, then walk again to the node just before position L - n (1-based from the front) and unlink the target.


ListNode* removeNthFromEnd(ListNode* head, int n) {
    int length = 0;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        length++;
    }
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* beforeTarget = &dummy;
    for (int step = 0; step < length - n; step++) {
        beforeTarget = beforeTarget->next;
    }
    ListNode* target = beforeTarget->next;
    beforeTarget->next = target->next;
    delete target;
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n) (two passes, still linear), Space: O(1). Two walks of the list, constant extra storage.




Hint 1You want the nth node from the end in one pass.


Hint 2A gap of n between two pointers reveals it.


Hint 3Advance a lead pointer n steps ahead, then move both until the lead hits the end; the trailing pointer is just before the node to remove (use a dummy head).






Optimized. The pattern is a two-pointer gap plus a dummy head, doing it in one pass. Advance a fast pointer n nodes ahead, then move fast and a slow pointer (starting at the dummy) together until fast reaches the last node. Now slow sits exactly before the node to remove.


ListNode* removeNthFromEnd(ListNode* head, int n) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* fast = &dummy;
    ListNode* slow = &dummy;
    for (int step = 0; step < n; step++) {
        fast = fast->next;
    }
    while (fast->next != nullptr) {
        fast = fast->next;
        slow = slow->next;
    }
    ListNode* target = slow->next;
    slow->next = target->next;
    delete target;
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single linear pass with two pointers.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The dummy head is what lets you remove the actual head (n == length) without a special branch.
    • 

  • The gap between fast and slow is held at n, so when fast hits the tail, slow is one before the target.
    • 

  • Remember to delete the removed node to avoid a leak when the interviewer cares about memory.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 64 of 150


64
Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a sorted list, delete all nodes that have a duplicate value, leaving only values that appeared exactly once. Return the head.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4 -> 5 becomes 1 -> 2 -> 5 (both 3s and both 4s are removed entirely).
Brute Force. Count occurrences of each value with a hash map in one pass, then build a new list of values whose count is exactly one. Because the input is sorted we could instead use the original order; here we keep it simple with a map.


ListNode* deleteDuplicates(ListNode* head) {
    unordered_map<int, int> counts;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        counts[current->val]++;
    }
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (counts[current->val] == 1) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to count, one to rebuild, plus a map and a new list.




Hint 1Unlike the basic version, nodes appearing more than once must be removed entirely.


Hint 2A dummy head and lookahead comparison handle leading duplicates.


Hint 3Use a dummy node; when the next two values are equal, skip all nodes of that value, otherwise advance the safe pointer.






Optimized. Because the list is sorted, equal values are adjacent, so we can do it in place with a dummy head. Keep previous at the last node known to be unique. When the node after previous starts a run of equal values, skip the entire run; otherwise advance previous.


ListNode* deleteDuplicates(ListNode* head) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* previous = &dummy;
    ListNode* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (current->next != nullptr && current->val == current->next->val) {
            int duplicateValue = current->val;
            while (current != nullptr && current->val == duplicateValue) {
                current = current->next;
            }
            previous->next = current;
        } else {
            previous = current;
            current = current->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass, only re-linking pointers.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Note the difference from the easier version (LeetCode 83) which keeps one copy; here a duplicated value is removed entirely.
    • 

  • Only advance previous when the current node is confirmed unique, otherwise it would point into the deleted run.
    • 

  • previous->next = current after skipping bridges over the whole duplicate block in one assignment.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 65 of 150


65
Rotate List
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftBloombergGoogleAdobe




Problem. Rotate the list to the right by k places, where k may be larger than the list length. Return the new head.
Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with k = 2 becomes 4 -> 5 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 (the last two nodes move to the front).
Brute Force. Rotate one step at a time, k times: each step finds the last node, moves it to the front. With k reduced modulo length first, this is correct but does redundant full walks.


ListNode* rotateRight(ListNode* head, int k) {
    if (head == nullptr || head->next == nullptr) {
        return head;
    }
    int length = 0;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        length++;
    }
    k = k % length;
    for (int rotation = 0; rotation < k; rotation++) {
        ListNode* beforeLast = head;
        while (beforeLast->next->next != nullptr) {
            beforeLast = beforeLast->next;
        }
        ListNode* lastNode = beforeLast->next;
        beforeLast->next = nullptr;
        lastNode->next = head;
        head = lastNode;
    }
    return head;
}




Time: O(n * k) (up to O(n^2) before the modulo, O(n * (k mod n)) after), Space: O(1). Each of k rotations re-walks the list.




Hint 1Rotating by k on a list of length n reduces to k mod n.


Hint 2Connect the list into a ring, then break it at the right spot.


Hint 3Find the length and tail, make it circular, then walk n - (k % n) steps from the head to the new tail and sever after it.






Optimized. The insight is to close the list into a ring, then cut it at the right place. Find the length and the tail, connect the tail to the head making a cycle, then walk length - (k % length) steps from the head to the new tail, and break the ring there.


ListNode* rotateRight(ListNode* head, int k) {
    if (head == nullptr || head->next == nullptr) {
        return head;
    }
    int length = 1;
    ListNode* tail = head;
    while (tail->next != nullptr) {
        tail = tail->next;
        length++;
    }
    int shift = k % length;
    if (shift == 0) {
        return head;
    }
    tail->next = head;
    int stepsToNewTail = length - shift;
    ListNode* newTail = head;
    for (int step = 1; step < stepsToNewTail; step++) {
        newTail = newTail->next;
    }
    ListNode* newHead = newTail->next;
    newTail->next = nullptr;
    return newHead;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass to measure, one partial pass to the cut point.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Always reduce k modulo the length first; k can be far larger than the list.
    • 

  • The new head is the node at index length - (k % length) from the front.
    • 

  • Forgetting to set the new tail's next to nullptr leaves a cycle, an easy bug to make.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 66 of 150


66
Partition List
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a list and a value partitionValue, reorder it so all nodes with value less than partitionValue come before all nodes greater than or equal to it. The relative order within each group must be preserved.
Example. 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 5 -> 2 with partitionValue = 3 becomes 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 4 -> 3 -> 5.
Brute Force. Make two passes: first copy the values less than the pivot into a result list in order, then append the values greater-or-equal in order. Building new nodes makes the bookkeeping trivial.


ListNode* partition(ListNode* head, int partitionValue) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val < partitionValue) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val >= partitionValue) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Two passes and a fully new list.




Hint 1Stable relative order must be preserved within each partition.


Hint 2Build two separate chains and splice them.


Hint 3Create two dummy-headed lists, one for values < x and one for >= x, append nodes accordingly, then link the first chain's tail to the second's head.






Optimized. The clean pattern is two dummy heads, one per bucket. Walk once, appending each original node to either the "less" list or the "greater-or-equal" list, then join the two lists. This reuses the existing nodes and keeps relative order automatically.


ListNode* partition(ListNode* head, int partitionValue) {
    ListNode lessDummy;
    ListNode greaterDummy;
    ListNode* lessTail = &lessDummy;
    ListNode* greaterTail = &greaterDummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val < partitionValue) {
            lessTail->next = current;
            lessTail = lessTail->next;
        } else {
            greaterTail->next = current;
            greaterTail = greaterTail->next;
        }
    }
    greaterTail->next = nullptr;
    lessTail->next = greaterDummy.next;
    return lessDummy.next;
}




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single pass, only relinking existing nodes.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Setting greaterTail->next = nullptr at the end is mandatory; the last greater node may still point into the original list and create a cycle.
    • 

  • Two dummy heads remove all the "is this the first node in the bucket" special cases.
    • 

  • Relative order is preserved for free because we append in traversal order.
    • 







⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Linked List›Problem 67 of 150


67
LRU Cache
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergUber




Problem. Design a cache with a fixed capacity supporting get(key) and put(key, value), both in O(1) average time. When the cache is full, put evicts the least recently used entry. Any get or put on a key counts as a use, making it most recently used.
Example. With capacity 2: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1) returns 1, put(3,3) evicts key 2 (least recently used), then get(2) returns -1.
Brute Force. Store entries in a list of (key, value) pairs and track recency by position. get scans the list for the key; on any access, move the entry to the front; put evicts from the back when full. The scan makes operations linear.


class LRUCache {
    int capacity;
    list<pair<int, int>> entries;
public:
    LRUCache(int cacheCapacity) : capacity(cacheCapacity) {}

    int get(int key) {
        for (auto iterator = entries.begin(); iterator != entries.end(); ++iterator) {
            if (iterator->first == key) {
                pair<int, int> found = *iterator;
                entries.erase(iterator);
                entries.push_front(found);
                return found.second;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    void put(int key, int value) {
        for (auto iterator = entries.begin(); iterator != entries.end(); ++iterator) {
            if (iterator->first == key) {
                entries.erase(iterator);
                break;
            }
        }
        entries.push_front({key, value});
        if ((int)entries.size() > capacity) {
            entries.pop_back();
        }
    }
};




Time: O(n) per operation, Space: O(capacity). Each call scans the whole list to find the key.




Hint 1You need O(1) get and put with eviction of the least recently used.


Hint 2Combine a hashmap with a doubly linked list ordered by recency.


Hint 3Map keys to list nodes; on access move the node to the front (most recent), and on capacity overflow evict the tail node and its map entry.






Optimized. The classic design is a hash map + doubly linked list. The doubly linked list keeps entries ordered by recency (front = most recent, back = least recent); the hash map maps each key to its node, so we find any entry in O(1). On access we unlink the node and re-attach it at the front; on overflow we drop the back node. C++'s std::list gives O(1) splice and stable iterators, so we store iterators in the map.


class LRUCache {
    int capacity;
    list<pair<int, int>> entries;
    unordered_map<int, list<pair<int, int>>::iterator> keyToNode;
public:
    LRUCache(int cacheCapacity) : capacity(cacheCapacity) {}

    int get(int key) {
        auto found = keyToNode.find(key);
        if (found == keyToNode.end()) {
            return -1;
        }
        entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, found->second);
        return found->second->second;
    }

    void put(int key, int value) {
        auto found = keyToNode.find(key);
        if (found != keyToNode.end()) {
            found->second->second = value;
            entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, found->second);
            return;
        }
        if ((int)entries.size() == capacity) {
            int leastRecentKey = entries.back().first;
            keyToNode.erase(leastRecentKey);
            entries.pop_back();
        }
        entries.push_front({key, value});
        keyToNode[key] = entries.begin();
    }
};




Time: O(1) average per operation, Space: O(capacity). The map lookup and the list splice are both constant time.


Interview Notes.


    

  • entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, iterator) moves a node to the front in O(1) without invalidating any iterator, which is exactly why std::list is the right container.
    • 

  • After splice, the stored iterator stays valid, so the map does not need updating on a get.
    • 

  • On eviction you must erase the key from the map before popping the node, using entries.back().first to get that key.
    • 

  • Interviewers often want you to hand-roll the doubly linked list (custom node with prev/next) rather than use std::list; mention you can, but std::list is cleaner and equally O(1).
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 68 of 150


68
Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
Easy


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAppleAdobe




Problem. Return the maximum depth of a binary tree: the number of nodes along the longest path from the root down to the farthest leaf. An empty tree has depth 0; a single node has depth 1.
Example. Tree [3,9,20,null,null,15,7] → 3. Path 3 → 20 → 15 (or 3 → 20 → 7) has three nodes, longer than the path 3 → 9.
Brute Force. Even the "brute force" here is recursion, since iterating a tree by hand without recursion or a stack is awkward. The cleanest first approach is a breadth-first level count: walk the tree level by level with a queue, incrementing a counter once per level. This uses extra memory for the queue but is intuitive — the depth is simply how many levels exist.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxDepth(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        queue<TreeNode*> nodesAtCurrentLevels;
        nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(root);
        int depth = 0;
        while (!nodesAtCurrentLevels.empty()) {
            int countThisLevel = nodesAtCurrentLevels.size();
            for (int index = 0; index < countThisLevel; ++index) {
                TreeNode* current = nodesAtCurrentLevels.front();
                nodesAtCurrentLevels.pop();
                if (current->left) nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(current->left);
                if (current->right) nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(current->right);
            }
            ++depth;
        }
        return depth;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — every node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree; in the worst case (a full bottom level) that is O(n).
Optimized. The recursive insight is one line of reasoning: the depth of a tree is 1 (for the root) plus the larger of the depths of its two subtrees. Define the function to return the depth of whatever node it is handed; the base case nullptr returns 0.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxDepth(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftDepth = maxDepth(root->left);
        int rightDepth = maxDepth(root->right);
        return 1 + max(leftDepth, rightDepth);
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each node is visited exactly once. Space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree, for the recursion call stack; this is O(log n) for a balanced tree and O(n) for a degenerate (linked-list-shaped) tree.
Interview Notes.


    

  • This is the canonical first tree problem; internalize the shape "base case for null, recurse on both children, combine."
    • 

  • The recursive version's space cost is hidden in the call stack — interviewers like to hear that h can be n in the worst case.
    • 

  • BFS is preferred only if you must avoid recursion entirely (e.g. extremely deep trees that would overflow the stack).
    • 





Hint 1Depth is one plus the deeper of the two subtrees.


Hint 2A simple recursion bottoms out at null.


Hint 3Return 0 for null, otherwise 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)).






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 69 of 150


69
Same Tree
Easy


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergApple




Problem. Given the roots of two binary trees, return true if they are structurally identical and every corresponding node holds the same value.
Example. p = [1,2,3], q = [1,2,3] → true. p = [1,2], q = [1,null,2] → false (the 2 is a left child in one tree, a right child in the other).
Brute Force. Serialize both trees into strings using a traversal that records structure (including nulls), then compare the strings. This works but allocates strings and is clumsier than direct comparison.


class Solution {
public:
    void serialize(TreeNode* node, string& output) {
        if (node == nullptr) { output += "#,"; return; }
        output += to_string(node->val) + ",";
        serialize(node->left, output);
        serialize(node->right, output);
    }
    bool isSameTree(TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        string firstSerialized, secondSerialized;
        serialize(p, firstSerialized);
        serialize(q, secondSerialized);
        return firstSerialized == secondSerialized;
    }
};




Time: O(n) to walk both trees. Space: O(n) for the two serialized strings plus the recursion stack.
Optimized. Compare the two trees in lockstep, node by node. At each step both nodes must be null together (a match), or both non-null with equal values and matching subtrees; any other case is a mismatch.


class Solution {
public:
    bool isSameTree(TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        if (p == nullptr && q == nullptr) return true;
        if (p == nullptr || q == nullptr) return false;
        if (p->val != q->val) return false;
        return isSameTree(p->left, q->left) && isSameTree(p->right, q->right);
    }
};




Time: O(n) where n is the size of the smaller tree (recursion stops at the first mismatch). Space: O(h) for the call stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The order of the three guard clauses matters: check "both null" before "one null," otherwise you would dereference a null pointer.
    • 

  • && short-circuits, so a mismatch in the left subtree avoids walking the right — a small but free optimization.
    • 

  • This lockstep pattern reappears in Symmetric Tree (problem 71).
    • 





Hint 1Two trees match if their roots match and their subtrees match.


Hint 2Recurse structurally on both simultaneously.


Hint 3Return true if both null; false if exactly one null or values differ; otherwise recurse on left-left and right-right.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 70 of 150


70
Invert Binary Tree
Easy


GoogleAmazonMicrosoftMetaBloombergApple




Problem. Invert (mirror) a binary tree: for every node, swap its left and right children. Return the new root.
Example. [4,2,7,1,3,6,9] → [4,7,2,9,6,3,1]. The root keeps its value; the whole left subtree and right subtree trade places, recursively.
Brute Force. Build a brand-new mirrored tree without modifying the original, allocating fresh nodes. This is wasteful in memory but illustrates the mirroring clearly: a new node's left child is the inversion of the original's right child.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invertTree(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* mirrored = new TreeNode(root->val);
        mirrored->left = invertTree(root->right);
        mirrored->right = invertTree(root->left);
        return mirrored;
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the newly allocated tree plus O(h) recursion stack.
Optimized. Invert in place: at each node, swap the two child pointers, then recurse into both children. No new nodes are created.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invertTree(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* originalLeft = root->left;
        root->left = invertTree(root->right);
        root->right = invertTree(originalLeft);
        return root;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — every node visited once. Space: O(h) for the recursion stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • Save the original left pointer before overwriting it; if you call invertTree(root->right) and assign it to root->left first, the old root->left is gone unless you stashed it.
    • 

  • An iterative version using a queue or stack works identically — pop a node, swap its children, push them.
    • 

  • This is the famous "whiteboard the inverter" problem; the in-place three-line solution is what interviewers expect.
    • 





Hint 1Inverting means swapping children at every node.


Hint 2Recursion (or a queue) visits every node once.


Hint 3Recurse: swap the left and right children, then invert each subtree (post or pre order both work).






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 71 of 150


71
Symmetric Tree
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobeApple




Problem. Return true if a binary tree is a mirror image of itself around its center — i.e. the left subtree is the mirror reflection of the right subtree.
Example. [1,2,2,3,4,4,3] → true. [1,2,2,null,3,null,3] → false (the two 3s sit on the same side, breaking the mirror).
Brute Force. Invert the right subtree into a new tree, then check whether the (inverted right) equals the left subtree using the Same-Tree comparison. Correct, but it allocates a whole mirrored copy.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invert(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* copy = new TreeNode(node->val);
        copy->left = invert(node->right);
        copy->right = invert(node->left);
        return copy;
    }
    bool sameTree(TreeNode* first, TreeNode* second) {
        if (first == nullptr && second == nullptr) return true;
        if (first == nullptr || second == nullptr) return false;
        return first->val == second->val
            && sameTree(first->left, second->left)
            && sameTree(first->right, second->right);
    }
    bool isSymmetric(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return true;
        return sameTree(root->left, invert(root->right));
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the inverted copy plus O(h) recursion stack.
Optimized. Compare the two subtrees as mirrors directly, without building anything: a left node and a right node mirror each other when their values match, the left's left mirrors the right's right, and the left's right mirrors the right's left.


class Solution {
public:
    bool isMirror(TreeNode* leftSubtree, TreeNode* rightSubtree) {
        if (leftSubtree == nullptr && rightSubtree == nullptr) return true;
        if (leftSubtree == nullptr || rightSubtree == nullptr) return false;
        if (leftSubtree->val != rightSubtree->val) return false;
        return isMirror(leftSubtree->left, rightSubtree->right)
            && isMirror(leftSubtree->right, rightSubtree->left);
    }
    bool isSymmetric(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return true;
        return isMirror(root->left, root->right);
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(h) for the call stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The crux is the cross-pairing: left->left with right->right, and left->right with right->left. Mixing these up is the most common bug.
    • 

  • An iterative version pushes pairs onto a queue and processes two nodes at a time.
    • 

  • Note how this reuses the Same-Tree null-handling pattern, just with mirrored recursion.
    • 





Hint 1Symmetry is a mirror property between the left and right subtrees.


Hint 2Compare nodes in mirrored pairs.


Hint 3Define a helper comparing two nodes: equal values and (left.left vs right.right) and (left.right vs right.left) recursively.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 72 of 150


72
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given the preorder and inorder traversal sequences of a binary tree (all values distinct), reconstruct and return the tree. Preorder visits node-left-right; inorder visits left-node-right.
Example. preorder = [3,9,20,15,7], inorder = [9,3,15,20,7] → tree [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]. The first preorder element 3 is the root; in inorder, everything left of 3 ([9]) is the left subtree, everything right ([15,20,7]) is the right.
Brute Force. At each step, the first remaining preorder value is the current root; find it inside the current inorder range with a linear scan, split into left and right halves, and recurse. The linear search is the inefficiency.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& preorder, int preStart, int preEnd,
                    vector<int>& inorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (preStart > preEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = preorder[preStart];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inStart;
        while (inorder[rootIndexInInorder] != rootValue) ++rootIndexInInorder;
        int leftSubtreeSize = rootIndexInInorder - inStart;
        root->left = build(preorder, preStart + 1, preStart + leftSubtreeSize,
                           inorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        root->right = build(preorder, preStart + leftSubtreeSize + 1, preEnd,
                            inorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& preorder, vector<int>& inorder) {
        return build(preorder, 0, preorder.size() - 1,
                     inorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};




Time: O(n²) in the worst case — each of n nodes may trigger an O(n) scan of inorder. Space: O(h) recursion, O(n) worst case.
Optimized. The only slow part is finding the root inside inorder. Precompute a hash map from value to its index in inorder, turning each lookup into O(1). The recursion structure is unchanged.


class Solution {
public:
    unordered_map<int, int> inorderIndexOf;
    int nextPreorderIndex = 0;
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& preorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = preorder[nextPreorderIndex++];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inorderIndexOf[rootValue];
        root->left = build(preorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        root->right = build(preorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& preorder, vector<int>& inorder) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)inorder.size(); ++index)
            inorderIndexOf[inorder[index]] = index;
        return build(preorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one map build plus one O(1) lookup per node. Space: O(n) for the map plus O(h) recursion.
Optimized version key point. Because preorder is consumed strictly left to right (root before its entire left subtree before its entire right subtree), a single advancing pointer nextPreorderIndex correctly hands out roots in the right order — process the left subtree fully before the right.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The order of the two recursive calls is mandatory: build the left subtree first, because in preorder the left subtree's nodes come immediately after the root and before any right-subtree node.
    • 

  • "All values distinct" is what makes the inorder lookup unambiguous; with duplicates the reconstruction is not unique.
    • 

  • Be ready to explain why the inorder split point separates left from right subtrees.
    • 





Hint 1Preorder gives you the root first; inorder splits left from right.


Hint 2Locate the root in inorder to size each subtree.


Hint 3Take preorder's front as the root, find it in inorder to determine left-subtree size, then recursively build left and right from the corresponding slices (a value->index map speeds lookups).






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 73 of 150


73
Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given inorder (left-node-right) and postorder (left-right-node) traversals with distinct values, reconstruct the tree. Postorder visits the root last.
Example. inorder = [9,3,15,20,7], postorder = [9,15,7,20,3] → [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]. The last postorder element 3 is the root; inorder splits into left [9] and right [15,20,7].
Brute Force. Symmetric to problem 72 but reading postorder from the back: the last unused postorder value is the current root, located in inorder by a linear scan. Because the root sits at the end, build the right subtree before the left.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& inorder, int inStart, int inEnd,
                    vector<int>& postorder, int postStart, int postEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = postorder[postEnd];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inStart;
        while (inorder[rootIndexInInorder] != rootValue) ++rootIndexInInorder;
        int rightSubtreeSize = inEnd - rootIndexInInorder;
        root->right = build(inorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd,
                            postorder, postEnd - rightSubtreeSize, postEnd - 1);
        root->left = build(inorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1,
                           postorder, postStart, postEnd - rightSubtreeSize - 1);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& inorder, vector<int>& postorder) {
        return build(inorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1,
                     postorder, 0, postorder.size() - 1);
    }
};




Time: O(n²) worst case from the repeated linear scans. Space: O(h) recursion.
Optimized. Use a value-to-index map on inorder for O(1) root location, and consume postorder from the back with a single retreating pointer. Crucially, recurse right before left because the postorder root is followed (reading backwards) by the entire right subtree before the left.


class Solution {
public:
    unordered_map<int, int> inorderIndexOf;
    int nextPostorderIndex;
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& postorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = postorder[nextPostorderIndex--];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inorderIndexOf[rootValue];
        root->right = build(postorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        root->left = build(postorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& inorder, vector<int>& postorder) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)inorder.size(); ++index)
            inorderIndexOf[inorder[index]] = index;
        nextPostorderIndex = postorder.size() - 1;
        return build(postorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the map plus O(h) recursion.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The right-before-left order is the single thing that distinguishes this from problem 72; swapping it produces a silently wrong tree, not a crash.
    • 

  • Reading postorder backwards is the mirror image of reading preorder forwards — the same idea seen from the other end.
    • 

  • If asked for preorder + postorder, point out it does not uniquely determine the tree in general (ambiguous for nodes with a single child).
    • 





Hint 1Postorder gives the root last; inorder splits the subtrees.


Hint 2Process postorder from the back, building right subtree before left.


Hint 3Pop the last postorder value as root, find it in inorder, recurse on the right portion first then the left, using an index map for fast root lookups.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 74 of 150


74
Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node II
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloomberg




Problem. Each node has an extra next pointer (initially null). Set every node's next to the node immediately to its right on the same level, or null if it is the rightmost. The tree is arbitrary (not necessarily perfect). Node type here is Node with left, right, and next.
Example. [1,2,3,4,5,null,7]: node 4's next is 5, node 5's next is 7, node 7's next is null, node 2's next is 3.
Brute Force. Do a level-order traversal with a queue; within each level, link each dequeued node to the previous one. Simple and uses the next pointers only as the answer, but consumes O(w) queue memory.


class Node {
public:
    int val;
    Node* left;
    Node* right;
    Node* next;
};
class Solution {
public:
    Node* connect(Node* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        queue<Node*> levelQueue;
        levelQueue.push(root);
        while (!levelQueue.empty()) {
            int countThisLevel = levelQueue.size();
            Node* previousOnLevel = nullptr;
            for (int index = 0; index < countThisLevel; ++index) {
                Node* current = levelQueue.front();
                levelQueue.pop();
                if (previousOnLevel) previousOnLevel->next = current;
                previousOnLevel = current;
                if (current->left) levelQueue.push(current->left);
                if (current->right) levelQueue.push(current->right);
            }
        }
        return root;
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(w) for the queue, up to O(n).
Optimized. Use the next pointers already built on the current level to walk it like a linked list, while stitching together the children into the next level — achieving O(1) extra space. A dummy head marks the start of each new level, and a moving tail appends children as they appear.


class Solution {
public:
    Node* connect(Node* root) {
        Node* currentLevelNode = root;
        while (currentLevelNode != nullptr) {
            Node nextLevelDummy;
            Node* nextLevelTail = &nextLevelDummy;
            while (currentLevelNode != nullptr) {
                if (currentLevelNode->left) {
                    nextLevelTail->next = currentLevelNode->left;
                    nextLevelTail = nextLevelTail->next;
                }
                if (currentLevelNode->right) {
                    nextLevelTail->next = currentLevelNode->right;
                    nextLevelTail = nextLevelTail->next;
                }
                currentLevelNode = currentLevelNode->next;
            }
            currentLevelNode = nextLevelDummy.next;
        }
        return root;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each node is touched once as a parent and once when appended. Space: O(1) extra (the dummy is a single stack object); the recursion stack is not used since this is iterative.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The dummy-head trick removes the special case of "is this the first child on the level?" — tail->next = child always works.
    • 

  • This is the "II" variant; because the tree is not perfect, you cannot assume left and right both exist, so you must guard each child.
    • 

  • Solving it in O(1) space is the whole point of the problem — the BFS solution is correct but considered the "easy" answer.
    • 





Hint 1Connecting next pointers level by level is natural for a level-order view.


Hint 2You can use the already-established next pointers of the current level to traverse it.


Hint 3For each level, walk it via next pointers using a dummy head to chain the next level's children, then descend to that chained level (achieving O(1) extra space).






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 75 of 150


75
Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Flatten the tree into a "linked list" in place using the same TreeNode type: each node's right points to the next node in preorder, and every left is set to null.
Example. [1,2,5,3,4,null,6] → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 along right pointers (left all null). The order is exactly the preorder traversal.
Brute Force. Record the preorder sequence into a vector, then relink the nodes: each node's left becomes null and right becomes the next node in the list.


class Solution {
public:
    void preorder(TreeNode* node, vector<TreeNode*>& visitedOrder) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        visitedOrder.push_back(node);
        preorder(node->left, visitedOrder);
        preorder(node->right, visitedOrder);
    }
    void flatten(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<TreeNode*> visitedOrder;
        preorder(root, visitedOrder);
        for (int index = 0; index + 1 < (int)visitedOrder.size(); ++index) {
            visitedOrder[index]->left = nullptr;
            visitedOrder[index]->right = visitedOrder[index + 1];
        }
        if (!visitedOrder.empty()) {
            visitedOrder.back()->left = nullptr;
            visitedOrder.back()->right = nullptr;
        }
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the vector plus O(h) recursion.
Optimized. The elegant O(1)-extra-space method (Morris-style) walks down the right spine: for each node that has a left child, find that left subtree's rightmost node, attach the current node's right subtree underneath it, then move the entire left subtree to the right and null the left pointer. Continue to the next right node.


class Solution {
public:
    void flatten(TreeNode* root) {
        TreeNode* current = root;
        while (current != nullptr) {
            if (current->left != nullptr) {
                TreeNode* rightmostOfLeft = current->left;
                while (rightmostOfLeft->right != nullptr)
                    rightmostOfLeft = rightmostOfLeft->right;
                rightmostOfLeft->right = current->right;
                current->right = current->left;
                current->left = nullptr;
            }
            current = current->right;
        }
    }
