};




Time: O(n) — although it walks down left subtrees to find predecessors, each edge is traversed a constant number of times. Space: O(1) extra.
Interview Notes.


    

  • This is a Morris-traversal-style rewiring: the predecessor (rightmost of the left subtree) gets the orphaned right subtree grafted on, so nothing is lost.
    • 

  • A clean recursive alternative flattens right subtree first, then left, prepending each into a member previousNode pointer — that costs O(h) stack.
    • 

  • Make sure the rightmost-search loop uses the updated tree only after attaching; here we search before any rewiring, so it is safe.
    • 





Hint 1The flattened order matches a preorder traversal.


Hint 2You can rewire pointers so left is null and right is the next preorder node.


Hint 3For each node with a left child, find the rightmost node of the left subtree, attach the current right subtree there, move left to right, and null out left; advance to the next right node.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 76 of 150


76
Path Sum
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. Given a tree and a target sum, return true if there is a root-to-leaf path whose node values add up exactly to the target. A leaf is a node with no children.
Example. [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2,null,null,null,1], target 22 → true via 5 → 4 → 11 → 2.
Brute Force. Enumerate every root-to-leaf path explicitly into a list, then check each path's sum. Correct but stores whole paths needlessly.


class Solution {
public:
    void collectPathSums(TreeNode* node, int runningSum, vector<int>& leafSums) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        runningSum += node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr) {
            leafSums.push_back(runningSum);
            return;
        }
        collectPathSums(node->left, runningSum, leafSums);
        collectPathSums(node->right, runningSum, leafSums);
    }
    bool hasPathSum(TreeNode* root, int targetSum) {
        vector<int> leafSums;
        collectPathSums(root, 0, leafSums);
        for (int sum : leafSums) if (sum == targetSum) return true;
        return false;
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(number of leaves) for the list plus O(h) recursion.
Optimized. Carry the remaining target down the tree: subtract the current value, and at a leaf check whether the remainder hit zero. No storage of paths; short-circuit as soon as a match is found.


class Solution {
public:
    bool hasPathSum(TreeNode* root, int targetSum) {
        if (root == nullptr) return false;
        int remaining = targetSum - root->val;
        if (root->left == nullptr && root->right == nullptr)
            return remaining == 0;
        return hasPathSum(root->left, remaining)
            || hasPathSum(root->right, remaining);
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The leaf test left == nullptr && right == nullptr is essential — you may not stop at an internal node even if the running sum already matches.
    • 

  • Returning false for a null root handles the empty tree and prevents a single-child internal node from being mistaken for a path end.
    • 

  • Values can be negative, so you cannot prune a branch just because the running sum overshot the target.
    • 





Hint 1You're checking existence of a root-to-leaf path with a target sum.


Hint 2Subtract as you descend and check at leaves.


Hint 3Recurse subtracting the node's value from the target; at a leaf return whether the remaining target equals the node's value; OR the results of the two subtrees.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 77 of 150


77
Sum Root to Leaf Numbers
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergFacebook




Problem. Each root-to-leaf path spells a number by concatenating digits (each node holds a digit 0–9). Return the sum of all such numbers.
Example. [1,2,3] → paths 1→2 = 12 and 1→3 = 13, sum 25. [4,9,0,5,1] → 495 + 491 + 40 = 1026.
Brute Force. Collect each root-to-leaf path as its full integer into a list, then sum the list. Same idea as Path Sum's brute force, building each number by running * 10 + digit.


class Solution {
public:
    void collectNumbers(TreeNode* node, int runningNumber, vector<int>& leafNumbers) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        runningNumber = runningNumber * 10 + node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr) {
            leafNumbers.push_back(runningNumber);
            return;
        }
        collectNumbers(node->left, runningNumber, leafNumbers);
        collectNumbers(node->right, runningNumber, leafNumbers);
    }
    int sumNumbers(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<int> leafNumbers;
        collectNumbers(root, 0, leafNumbers);
        int total = 0;
        for (int number : leafNumbers) total += number;
        return total;
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(number of leaves) plus O(h) recursion.
Optimized. Fold the addition into the recursion: pass the number built so far down, and have each call return the sum of all leaf-numbers beneath it. At a leaf, return the completed number; otherwise return the sum from both children.


class Solution {
public:
    int sumFrom(TreeNode* node, int runningNumber) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        runningNumber = runningNumber * 10 + node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr)
            return runningNumber;
        return sumFrom(node->left, runningNumber)
             + sumFrom(node->right, runningNumber);
    }
    int sumNumbers(TreeNode* root) {
        return sumFrom(root, 0);
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The trick runningNumber * 10 + digit is the standard way to grow a number digit by digit, left to right.
    • 

  • A null child returns 0, so internal nodes with one child correctly forward only the existing branch's contribution.
    • 

  • Constraints guarantee the sum fits in a 32-bit int on this problem; mention you would widen to long if depths were larger.
    • 





Hint 1Each path builds a number digit by digit as you descend.


Hint 2Carry the accumulated number into the recursion.


Hint 3Recurse passing current = current*10 + node.val; at leaves add current to a running total, summing contributions from both subtrees.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 78 of 150


78
Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
Hard


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. A path is any sequence of connected nodes (it need not pass through the root and need not reach a leaf), and each node appears at most once. Return the maximum possible sum of node values along any such path.
Example. [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7] → 42, the path 15 → 20 → 7. Note it does not include the root.
Brute Force. For every node, treat it as the highest point ("turning point") of a path and compute the best downward path on each side by a separate recursive height-style function; combine left + node + right and track the global best. Recomputing the per-side best for every node from scratch is the inefficiency.


class Solution {
public:
    int bestDownward(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->left));
        int rightGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->right));
        return node->val + max(leftGain, rightGain);
    }
    int maxPathSum(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int throughThisNode = root->val
            + max(0, bestDownward(root->left))
            + max(0, bestDownward(root->right));
        int answer = throughThisNode;
        if (root->left) answer = max(answer, maxPathSum(root->left));
        if (root->right) answer = max(answer, maxPathSum(root->right));
        return answer;
    }
};




Time: O(n²) — for each node we call bestDownward which itself walks a subtree. Space: O(h) recursion.
Optimized. A single postorder pass does both jobs. The helper returns the best downward path starting at a node (so it can be extended by the parent), while a member variable records the best complete path seen anywhere (which may turn at the current node, using both sides). Negative downward gains are clamped to zero — better to drop a branch than to subtract from the sum.


class Solution {
public:
    int bestPathSum = INT_MIN;
    int bestDownward(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->left));
        int rightGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->right));
        int pathTurningHere = node->val + leftGain + rightGain;
        bestPathSum = max(bestPathSum, pathTurningHere);
        return node->val + max(leftGain, rightGain);
    }
    int maxPathSum(TreeNode* root) {
        bestDownward(root);
        return bestPathSum;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one visit per node. Space: O(h) recursion stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The key distinction: a node returns a path that goes down only one side (so the parent can hang it below itself), but updates the answer with a path that may use both sides (which cannot be extended upward).
    • 

  • Clamp each child's gain with max(0, ...) so a negative subtree is simply excluded.
    • 

  • Initialize the global best to INT_MIN, not 0 — the answer can be negative when all values are negative.
    • 





Hint 1A path may dip into a subtree and come back, so a subtree contributes at most one upward arm.


Hint 2Distinguish 'best downward path through this node' from 'best path that can extend upward'.


Hint 3Post-order recurse returning the max downward gain (clamp negatives to 0); at each node update a global best with node.val + leftGain + rightGain, but return node.val + max(leftGain, rightGain) upward.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 79 of 150


79
Binary Search Tree Iterator
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergMetaUber




Problem. Implement an iterator over a BST that returns values in ascending order. next() returns the next-smallest value; hasNext() reports whether more remain. Aim for average O(1) per next() and O(h) memory.
Example. BST [7,3,15,null,null,9,20]: next()→3, next()→7, hasNext()→true, next()→9, next()→15, hasNext()→true, next()→20, hasNext()→false.
Brute Force. In the constructor, do a full inorder traversal into a vector (which is automatically sorted for a BST), then next() just walks an index. Simple, but it uses O(n) memory up front and does all the work before the first next().


class BSTIterator {
    vector<int> sortedValues;
    int position = 0;
    void inorder(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        inorder(node->left);
        sortedValues.push_back(node->val);
        inorder(node->right);
    }
public:
    BSTIterator(TreeNode* root) { inorder(root); }
    int next() { return sortedValues[position++]; }
    bool hasNext() { return position < (int)sortedValues.size(); }
};




Time: O(n) construction, O(1) per next(). Space: O(n) for the stored values.
Optimized. Simulate the inorder traversal lazily with an explicit stack holding only the path of left-descendants. Push all left children initially; on next(), pop a node (the current smallest), then push the left spine of its right child. The stack never exceeds the tree height.


class BSTIterator {
    stack<TreeNode*> leftSpine;
    void pushLeftSpine(TreeNode* node) {
        while (node != nullptr) {
            leftSpine.push(node);
            node = node->left;
        }
    }
public:
    BSTIterator(TreeNode* root) { pushLeftSpine(root); }
    int next() {
        TreeNode* smallest = leftSpine.top();
        leftSpine.pop();
        pushLeftSpine(smallest->right);
        return smallest->val;
    }
    bool hasNext() { return !leftSpine.empty(); }
};




Time: O(1) amortized per next() — each node is pushed and popped exactly once across the whole iteration. Space: O(h) for the stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • "Amortized O(1)" is the precise claim: a single next() might push a long left spine, but those pushes are paid back over later cheap calls.
    • 

  • The O(h) stack is the win over the O(n) precomputed vector; mention this directly when asked about memory.
    • 

  • This is just an inorder traversal turned inside-out — recognizing that connection is what the interviewer is testing.
    • 





Hint 1An in-order traversal of a BST yields sorted values; the iterator should produce them lazily.


Hint 2A stack can simulate the recursion incrementally.


Hint 3Push all left nodes onto a stack initially; next() pops a node, then pushes the left spine of its right child; hasNext checks the stack.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 80 of 150


80
Count Complete Tree Nodes
Easy


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. Count the nodes in a complete binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, which is filled left to right). A plain O(n) count works, but the structure allows faster.
Example. [1,2,3,4,5,6] → 6. Levels are [1], [2,3], [4,5,6]; the last is left-filled.
Brute Force. Ignore the completeness property and count every node with an ordinary traversal.


class Solution {
public:
    int countNodes(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        return 1 + countNodes(root->left) + countNodes(root->right);
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion.
Optimized. Exploit completeness. Measure the leftmost depth and rightmost depth: if they are equal, the tree is perfect and has exactly 2^depth - 1 nodes — no traversal needed. Otherwise recurse on both children; only the subtrees straddling the incomplete last level do real work.


class Solution {
public:
    int leftHeight(TreeNode* node) {
        int height = 0;
        while (node != nullptr) { ++height; node = node->left; }
        return height;
    }
    int rightHeight(TreeNode* node) {
        int height = 0;
        while (node != nullptr) { ++height; node = node->right; }
        return height;
    }
    int countNodes(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftmost = leftHeight(root);
        int rightmost = rightHeight(root);
        if (leftmost == rightmost) return (1 << leftmost) - 1;
        return 1 + countNodes(root->left) + countNodes(root->right);
    }
};




Time: O(log² n) — at most O(log n) levels of recursion, each doing an O(log n) height measurement. Space: O(log n) recursion (the tree is complete, so its height is logarithmic).
Interview Notes.


    

  • 1 << depth is 2^depth; for a perfect tree of height h the node count is 2^h - 1.
    • 

  • The recursion only branches along the single path where the last level is incomplete; all-perfect subtrees are counted by formula in O(log n).
    • 

  • If the tree were not guaranteed complete, this optimization would be invalid and you would fall back to the O(n) count.
    • 





Hint 1A complete tree has a special shape you can exploit instead of counting every node.


Hint 2Compare the left-spine and right-spine heights.


Hint 3If left and right heights match, the subtree is perfect with 2^h - 1 nodes; otherwise recurse into both halves, giving O(log^2 n).






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 81 of 150


81
Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
Medium


AmazonMetaMicrosoftGoogleBloombergLinkedIn




Problem. Given two nodes p and q known to exist in the tree, return their lowest common ancestor: the deepest node that has both as descendants (a node can be a descendant of itself). This is a general binary tree, not a BST.
Example. [3,5,1,6,2,0,8,null,null,7,4], p=5, q=1 → 3. With p=5, q=4 → 5, since 5 is an ancestor of 4 and of itself.
Brute Force. Find the root-to-node path for p and for q as two lists, then walk both lists from the top in parallel; the last node they share is the LCA. Clear but stores two full paths.


class Solution {
public:
    bool findPath(TreeNode* node, TreeNode* target, vector<TreeNode*>& path) {
        if (node == nullptr) return false;
        path.push_back(node);
        if (node == target) return true;
        if (findPath(node->left, target, path)) return true;
        if (findPath(node->right, target, path)) return true;
        path.pop_back();
        return false;
    }
    TreeNode* lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode* root, TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        vector<TreeNode*> pathToP, pathToQ;
        findPath(root, p, pathToP);
        findPath(root, q, pathToQ);
        TreeNode* ancestor = nullptr;
        int index = 0;
        while (index < (int)pathToP.size() && index < (int)pathToQ.size()
               && pathToP[index] == pathToQ[index]) {
            ancestor = pathToP[index];
            ++index;
        }
        return ancestor;
    }
};




Time: O(n). Space: O(h) for the two paths plus recursion.
Optimized. A single postorder recursion. Ask each subtree "do you contain p or q?" If one of the targets is found in the left subtree and the other in the right, the current node is the LCA. If the current node is itself p or q, it counts as found too.


class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode* root, TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        if (root == nullptr || root == p || root == q) return root;
        TreeNode* foundInLeft = lowestCommonAncestor(root->left, p, q);
        TreeNode* foundInRight = lowestCommonAncestor(root->right, p, q);
        if (foundInLeft != nullptr && foundInRight != nullptr) return root;
        return foundInLeft != nullptr ? foundInLeft : foundInRight;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — each node visited once. Space: O(h) recursion stack.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The return value does double duty: at the LCA it returns the answer; below the LCA it returns "where I found a target," which bubbles up.
    • 

  • The base case root == p || root == q handles the ancestor-of-itself case cleanly — if q is below p, the call at p returns p and the other side returns null, so p propagates up as the answer.
    • 

  • This works because both nodes are guaranteed present; if they might be absent, you would add a count to confirm both were actually seen before returning.
    • 





Hint 1The LCA is where the two target nodes' paths diverge.


Hint 2Recurse and report which side(s) contain a target.


Hint 3Recurse: if a node is one of the targets return it; if both subtrees return non-null this node is the LCA, otherwise bubble up whichever side found a target.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 82 of 150


82
Binary Tree Right Side View
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Imagine standing to the right of a binary tree and looking left. Return the values of the nodes you can see, ordered from top to bottom. For each depth level, that visible node is the rightmost node on that level. The tree can be empty (return an empty list).


Example. Tree: root 1 with left child 2 and right child 3; node 2 has a right child 5; node 3 has a right child 4.


    1
   / \
  2   3
   \   \
    5   4




Level 0 rightmost is 1, level 1 rightmost is 3, level 2 rightmost is 4. Output: [1, 3, 4].




Hint 1The right side view is the last node seen at each level.


Hint 2Level-order traversal naturally exposes the last node per level.


Hint 3BFS level by level and record the final node of each level; alternatively DFS visiting right before left and recording the first node seen at each new depth.






Brute Force. Do a depth-first traversal while tracking the current depth. For each level keep the latest value seen, but force the traversal to visit the left subtree before the right subtree so that the right side overwrites the left — the last value written for each depth ends up being the rightmost. Store one entry per depth in a map keyed by depth, then read it out in order.


class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth, std::map<int, int>& rightmostByDepth) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        rightmostByDepth[depth] = node->val;
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, rightmostByDepth);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, rightmostByDepth);
    }

    std::vector<int> rightSideView(TreeNode* root) {
        std::map<int, int> rightmostByDepth;
        traverse(root, 0, rightmostByDepth);
        std::vector<int> result;
        for (const auto& depthValuePair : rightmostByDepth) {
            result.push_back(depthValuePair.second);
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Justification: every one of the n nodes is visited once, but each std::map write costs O(log n) because the map is a balanced tree, giving O(n log n). The map plus recursion stack hold up to O(n) entries.


Optimized. Run an ordinary BFS level by level. At each level the last node you pop (the one at index levelSize - 1) is the rightmost, so append its value to the answer. No depth bookkeeping or map is needed — the level-size loop gives you the boundary for free.


class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<int> rightSideView(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<int> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                if (positionInLevel == levelSize - 1) {
                    result.push_back(currentNode->val);
                }
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: each node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once with O(1) work, so O(n) total. The queue holds at most one full level, which can be up to O(n) wide for a near-complete tree.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The rightmost node is not always a right child — node 5 could be visible if 3 had no right child, so don't shortcut by only following right pointers.
    • 

  • Push children left-then-right so the queue stays in left-to-right order within each level; the rightmost is then simply the last popped.
    • 

  • A DFS variant that visits right-before-left and records the first value seen per depth also works in O(n); mention it if asked for the recursive form.
    • 

  • Empty tree must return [], not crash — guard the null root.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 83 of 150


83
Average of Levels in Binary Tree
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleFacebookBloomberg




Problem. Given the root of a binary tree, return a list where the i-th element is the average value of all nodes at depth i. The tree is non-empty. Because values can sum large, accumulate the per-level sum in a wide integer type to avoid overflow, then divide as a double.


Example. Tree: root 3, left child 9, right child 20; node 20 has children 15 and 7.


    3
   / \
  9   20
     /  \
    15   7




Level 0 average is 3, level 1 average is (9 + 20) / 2 = 14.5, level 2 average is (15 + 7) / 2 = 11. Output: [3.0, 14.5, 11.0].




Hint 1This is a straightforward per-level aggregation.


Hint 2Level-order traversal collects each level's values.


Hint 3BFS by levels, summing each level's node values and dividing by the level's node count to get the average.






Brute Force. DFS while tracking depth, building two parallel maps: one summing the values at each depth and one counting how many nodes that depth has. After the traversal, walk the depths in order and divide sum by count.


class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth,
                  std::map<int, long long>& sumByDepth,
                  std::map<int, int>& countByDepth) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        sumByDepth[depth] += node->val;
        countByDepth[depth] += 1;
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
    }

    std::vector<double> averageOfLevels(TreeNode* root) {
        std::map<int, long long> sumByDepth;
        std::map<int, int> countByDepth;
        traverse(root, 0, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
        std::vector<double> result;
        for (const auto& depthSumPair : sumByDepth) {
            int depth = depthSumPair.first;
            result.push_back(static_cast<double>(depthSumPair.second) / countByDepth[depth]);
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Justification: n node visits, each doing O(log n) map operations. The two maps plus recursion stack are O(n).


Optimized. BFS with the level-size loop. For each level, sum the values of exactly levelSize popped nodes, then push sum / levelSize as a double. The level size is known up front, so no counting map is needed.


class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<double> averageOfLevels(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<double> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            long long levelSum = 0;
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                levelSum += currentNode->val;
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
            result.push_back(static_cast<double>(levelSum) / levelSize);
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: one enqueue and one dequeue per node with O(1) work each. The queue holds at most a full level, up to O(n).


Interview Notes.


    

  • Use long long for levelSum: a level full of values near INT_MAX overflows a plain int before you divide.
    • 

  • Cast to double before dividing, otherwise integer division throws away the fractional part (e.g. 29 / 2 would give 14, not 14.5).
    • 

  • This is the cleanest "BFS template" problem — if you can write this, the rest of the section is the same loop with a different per-level action.
    • 

  • levelSize is guaranteed non-zero inside the loop, so the division is always safe.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 84 of 150


84
Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergLinkedIn




Problem. Return the node values grouped by level: a list of lists, where the i-th inner list holds every value at depth i, in left-to-right order. Empty tree returns an empty outer list.


Example. Tree: root 3, left 9, right 20; node 20 has children 15 and 7.


    3
   / \
  9   20
     /  \
    15   7




Output: [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]].




Hint 1You need nodes grouped by depth.


Hint 2A queue processed one full level at a time gives the grouping.


Hint 3BFS using the queue size at the start of each iteration as the level boundary; collect that many nodes' values into a sublist.






Brute Force. DFS with depth tracking. Maintain a vector of vectors; whenever the current depth equals the current number of inner lists, append a fresh empty inner list, then push the node's value into the inner list at index depth. Visiting left before right keeps each inner list in left-to-right order.


class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth, std::vector<std::vector<int>>& levels) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        if (depth == static_cast<int>(levels.size())) {
            levels.push_back(std::vector<int>());
        }
        levels[depth].push_back(node->val);
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, levels);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, levels);
    }

    std::vector<std::vector<int>> levelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> levels;
        traverse(root, 0, levels);
        return levels;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: each node is visited once and appended in amortized O(1). The output stores all n values and the recursion stack is O(h) where h is tree height, both within O(n).


Optimized. BFS with the level-size loop. For each level, create one fresh inner vector, fill it with the levelSize popped values, then attach it to the result. This produces the grouping directly without any depth comparison.


class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<std::vector<int>> levelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            std::vector<int> currentLevelValues;
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                currentLevelValues.push_back(currentNode->val);
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
            result.push_back(currentLevelValues);
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: every node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once. The output holds all n values and the queue holds at most one level, both O(n).


Interview Notes.


    

  • This is the canonical BFS problem; the other three in this section are this loop with the per-level result transformed.
    • 

  • Capturing levelSize before the inner loop is essential — nodeQueue.size() changes as you push children, so reading it inside the loop would be wrong.
    • 

  • The BFS version is usually preferred in interviews because it mirrors the problem's "by level" framing one-to-one.
    • 

  • Push left child before right child to preserve left-to-right order within each inner list.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 85 of 150


85
Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergAdobe




Problem. Return values grouped by level like the previous problem, but alternate direction: level 0 left-to-right, level 1 right-to-left, level 2 left-to-right, and so on (zigzag / boustrophedon order). Empty tree returns an empty outer list.


Example. Tree: root 3, left 9, right 20; node 20 has children 15 and 7.


    3
   / \
  9   20
     /  \
    15   7




Level 0 left-to-right [3], level 1 right-to-left [20, 9], level 2 left-to-right [15, 7]. Output: [[3], [20, 9], [15, 7]].




Hint 1Same level grouping as standard level order, but alternate the output direction.


Hint 2Track a left-to-right toggle per level.


Hint 3Do level-order BFS, and for every other level reverse the collected values (or push to the front) before adding the level to the result.






Brute Force. Do an ordinary left-to-right level-order traversal exactly as in problem 84 (here via DFS with depth tracking), then post-process: reverse every inner list at an odd index to flip its direction.


class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth, std::vector<std::vector<int>>& levels) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        if (depth == static_cast<int>(levels.size())) {
            levels.push_back(std::vector<int>());
        }
        levels[depth].push_back(node->val);
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, levels);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, levels);
    }

    std::vector<std::vector<int>> zigzagLevelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> levels;
        traverse(root, 0, levels);
        for (int depth = 0; depth < static_cast<int>(levels.size()); ++depth) {
            if (depth % 2 == 1) {
                std::reverse(levels[depth].begin(), levels[depth].end());
            }
        }
        return levels;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: the traversal is O(n); reversing the odd levels touches each value at most once more, still O(n) overall. The output and recursion stack are O(n).


Optimized. Use the same BFS loop but avoid a separate reversal pass by writing values directly into their final slots. For each level, size the inner vector to levelSize; when the level should go right-to-left, fill it back-to-front by computing the insertion index as levelSize - 1 - positionInLevel. A boolean toggled each level decides the direction.


class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<std::vector<int>> zigzagLevelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        bool leftToRight = true;
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            std::vector<int> currentLevelValues(levelSize);
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                int insertionIndex = leftToRight ? positionInLevel : (levelSize - 1 - positionInLevel);
                currentLevelValues[insertionIndex] = currentNode->val;
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
            result.push_back(currentLevelValues);
            leftToRight = !leftToRight;
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: each node is enqueued and dequeued once, and writing into a pre-sized vector by index is O(1). The output and queue are O(n).


Interview Notes.


    

  • The traversal itself never changes direction — you always BFS left-to-right and only the output ordering zigzags. Pushing children in flipped order is a common bug; keep children left-then-right always.
    • 

  • Pre-sizing the inner vector with std::vector<int> currentLevelValues(levelSize) lets you assign by index; the brute-force std::reverse approach is equally O(n) and often clearer to explain.
    • 

  • Toggle the leftToRight flag once per level, after the inner loop completes — not per node.
    • 

  • A std::deque (push front vs back) is another clean way to express the alternation if the interviewer prefers it.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search Tree›Problem 86 of 150


86
Minimum Absolute Difference in BST
Easy


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergApple




Problem. Given the root of a BST, return the minimum absolute difference between the values of any two different nodes in the tree. Values are integers and the tree has at least two nodes.


Example. Tree with values {1, 3, 6, 4} arranged as a BST so in-order gives [1, 3, 4, 6]. The closest pair is 3 and 4, so the answer is 1. Trace: differences between consecutive sorted values are 3-1=2, 4-3=1, 6-4=2; the smallest is 1.




Hint 1In a BST the closest values to any node are its in-order neighbors.


Hint 2An in-order traversal yields sorted values, so adjacent differences are candidates.


Hint 3Traverse in-order tracking the previous value; the minimum gap between consecutive in-order values is the answer.






Brute Force. Collect every node value into a list, then compare every pair and track the smallest difference. This ignores the BST structure entirely and just brute-forces all pairs.


class Solution {
public:
    void collectValues(TreeNode* node, vector<int>& values) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        values.push_back(node->val);
        collectValues(node->left, values);
        collectValues(node->right, values);
    }

    int getMinimumDifference(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<int> values;
        collectValues(root, values);
        int minimumDifference = INT_MAX;
        for (int firstIndex = 0; firstIndex < values.size(); firstIndex++) {
            for (int secondIndex = firstIndex + 1; secondIndex < values.size(); secondIndex++) {
                int difference = abs(values[firstIndex] - values[secondIndex]);
                minimumDifference = min(minimumDifference, difference);
            }
        }
        return minimumDifference;
    }
};




Time: O(n²), Space: O(n). We compare every pair of the n collected values, giving the quadratic time; the vector holding all values is the linear space.


Optimized. Key insight: the minimum difference in any sorted sequence always occurs between two adjacent values, never between values that are far apart. Since an in-order traversal of a BST produces sorted values, we only need to compare each value with the one visited just before it. We carry along the previously seen value and update the answer as we go, never storing the whole list.


class Solution {
public:
    void inOrder(TreeNode* node, int& previousValue, int& minimumDifference) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        inOrder(node->left, previousValue, minimumDifference);
        if (previousValue != INT_MIN) {
            minimumDifference = min(minimumDifference, node->val - previousValue);
        }
        previousValue = node->val;
        inOrder(node->right, previousValue, minimumDifference);
    }

    int getMinimumDifference(TreeNode* root) {
        int previousValue = INT_MIN;
        int minimumDifference = INT_MAX;
        inOrder(root, previousValue, minimumDifference);
        return minimumDifference;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(h). Each node is visited once, so time is linear. The space is the recursion stack depth, which equals the tree height h (O(log n) for a balanced tree, O(n) in the worst case of a degenerate, list-like tree).


Interview Notes.


    

  • Because in-order yields sorted values, node->val - previousValue is always non-negative, so you can drop the abs().
    • 

  • The INT_MIN sentinel marks "no previous value yet" so the very first node visited is skipped in the comparison.
    • 

  • Passing previousValue and minimumDifference by reference (int&) lets the recursion share and update one running state across all calls.
    • 

  • This adjacent-only insight reappears constantly — sorted data means the answer lives between neighbors.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search Tree›Problem 87 of 150


87
Kth Smallest Element in a BST
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergUber




Problem. Given the root of a BST and an integer k, return the value of the k-th smallest element (1-indexed, so k = 1 is the minimum).


Example. BST with in-order order [1, 2, 3, 4] and k = 2. The 2nd smallest is 2. Trace: in-order visits 1 (count 1), then 2 (count 2 == k) → return 2.




Hint 1In-order traversal of a BST gives ascending order.


Hint 2You only need to count to the kth in-order node.


Hint 3Do an in-order traversal (recursive or stack-based) and return the value when you've visited k nodes.






Brute Force. Do a full in-order traversal, store every value in a sorted-by-construction list, then index into position k - 1.


class Solution {
public:
    void inOrder(TreeNode* node, vector<int>& sortedValues) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        inOrder(node->left, sortedValues);
        sortedValues.push_back(node->val);
        inOrder(node->right, sortedValues);
    }

    int kthSmallest(TreeNode* root, int k) {
        vector<int> sortedValues;
        inOrder(root, sortedValues);
        return sortedValues[k - 1];
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). We always walk the whole tree even when k is small, and we store all n values in the vector.


Optimized. Key insight: in-order visits values in ascending order, so the k-th node we encounter during the traversal is the k-th smallest. We can stop the moment we reach it instead of finishing the whole tree, and we never need to store the values — just a running count.


class Solution {
public:
    void inOrder(TreeNode* node, int& k, int& result) {
        if (node == nullptr || k == 0) return;
        inOrder(node->left, k, result);
        if (k == 0) return;
        k--;
        if (k == 0) {
            result = node->val;
            return;
        }
        inOrder(node->right, k, result);
    }

    int kthSmallest(TreeNode* root, int k) {
        int result = 0;
        inOrder(root, k, result);
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(h + k), Space: O(h). We descend to the smallest element (cost h) then visit k nodes, stopping early. The space is the recursion stack bounded by the tree height h.


Interview Notes.


    

  • We decrement k as a countdown; when it hits 0 the current node is the answer.
    • 

  • The early if (k == 0) return; checks prune the rest of the traversal once the answer is found, which is the whole point of the optimization.
    • 

  • A common follow-up: "what if the tree is modified often and you must answer many k-th queries?" Augment each node with a subtree-size count so each query becomes O(h) without re-traversing.
    • 

  • An iterative version using an explicit stack avoids recursion depth limits and lets you pause and resume the traversal cleanly.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search Tree›Problem 88 of 150


88
Validate Binary Search Tree
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given the root of a binary tree, determine whether it is a valid BST. Validity requires that every node's left subtree contains only smaller values and its right subtree only larger values — not just its immediate children. Assume no duplicate values.


Example. Tree with root 5, left child 1, right child 4, and 4 having children 3 and 6. In-order gives [1, 5, 3, 4, 6], which is not sorted (5 then 3), so the answer is false. The trap: 4 < 5 so it looks fine locally, but 4 is in the right subtree of 5 and must exceed 5.




Hint 1Each node must respect bounds inherited from its ancestors, not just its parent.


Hint 2Pass down a valid (low, high) range.


Hint 3Recurse with an open interval: every node must lie strictly within (low, high); descend left tightening the upper bound to node.val and right tightening the lower bound.






Brute Force. Do an in-order traversal collecting all values, then check that the resulting list is strictly increasing. This directly applies the sorted-order property.


class Solution {
public:
    void inOrder(TreeNode* node, vector<int>& values) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        inOrder(node->left, values);
        values.push_back(node->val);
        inOrder(node->right, values);
    }

    bool isValidBST(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<int> values;
        inOrder(root, values);
        for (int index = 1; index < values.size(); index++) {
            if (values[index] <= values[index - 1]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to collect and one pass to check, both linear; the vector storing all values is the linear space.


Optimized. Key insight: instead of materializing the list, pass each node a valid range (lowerBound, upperBound) it must fall strictly inside. The root may be anything; descending left tightens the upper bound to the parent's value, and descending right tightens the lower bound. This catches "deep" violations like a small value buried in a right subtree, which a naive parent-vs-child check would miss.


class Solution {
public:
    bool validate(TreeNode* node, long lowerBound, long upperBound) {
        if (node == nullptr) return true;
        if (node->val <= lowerBound || node->val >= upperBound) {
            return false;
        }
        return validate(node->left, lowerBound, node->val) &&
               validate(node->right, node->val, upperBound);
    }

    bool isValidBST(TreeNode* root) {
        return validate(root, LONG_MIN, LONG_MAX);
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(h). Every node is checked once against its bounds. The space is the recursion stack, bounded by the tree height h.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Use long for the bounds so that a node value equal to INT_MIN or INT_MAX does not falsely fail the comparison against an int sentinel.
    • 

  • The classic bug is checking only node->val > node->left->val and node->val < node->right->val — that validates parent-child pairs but misses ancestors further up, so it wrongly accepts invalid trees.
    • 

  • An equivalent approach: in-order traverse while tracking only the previous value, returning false the instant a value is not strictly greater than its predecessor — same O(n) time, O(h) space, no vector.
    • 

  • Bounds start at the widest possible range so the root is unconstrained, and each recursive step narrows exactly one side.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 89 of 150


89
Number of Islands
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergUber




Problem. You are given a 2D grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water). An island is a group of '1' cells connected horizontally or vertically (not diagonally). Count how many distinct islands there are. Constraints: grid is up to ~300×300; cells are only '1' or '0'.


Example. Grid [["1","1","0"],["1","0","0"],["0","0","1"]] → output 2. Trace: the top-left block of three connected 1s is one island; the lone 1 in the bottom-right corner is the second.




Hint 1Each unvisited land cell starts a connected region.


Hint 2Flood fill marks an entire island so you don't recount it.


Hint 3Scan the grid; on each unvisited '1', increment the island count and DFS/BFS-flood all connected land cells to '0' (or a visited marker).






Brute Force. There is no meaningfully different "brute force" here — counting islands fundamentally requires visiting connected land. A naive version is to repeatedly scan for an unvisited land cell and erase its whole connected blob, which is exactly the optimized approach below. So conceptually the brute force is flood fill; a slower-but-correct variant uses union-find to merge adjacent land cells, then counts distinct roots.


class Solution {
public:
    int numIslands(vector<vector<char>>& grid) {
        int rows = grid.size();
        if (rows == 0) return 0;
        int cols = grid[0].size();
        vector<int> parent(rows * cols);
        for (int index = 0; index < rows * cols; ++index) parent[index] = index;
        function<int(int)> findRoot = [&](int node) {
            while (parent[node] != node) {
                parent[node] = parent[parent[node]];
                node = parent[node];
            }
            return node;
        };
        auto unite = [&](int first, int second) {
            parent[findRoot(first)] = findRoot(second);
        };
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row) {
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
                if (grid[row][col] == '1') {
                    if (row + 1 < rows && grid[row + 1][col] == '1')
                        unite(row * cols + col, (row + 1) * cols + col);
                    if (col + 1 < cols && grid[row][col + 1] == '1')
                        unite(row * cols + col, row * cols + col + 1);
                }
            }
        }
        int islandCount = 0;
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row)
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col)
                if (grid[row][col] == '1' && findRoot(row * cols + col) == row * cols + col)
                    ++islandCount;
        return islandCount;
    }
};




Time: O(rows × cols × α), Space: O(rows × cols). Justification: every cell is united with its right/down neighbor in near-constant time (α is the inverse-Ackermann function, effectively constant), and the parent array holds one entry per cell.


Optimized. The key insight is flood fill via DFS: scan the grid, and each time we hit an unvisited '1', increment the island counter and then "sink" the entire connected island by DFS-marking every reachable land cell as water. Because each island is fully erased the first time it is touched, the outer scan only triggers a new count once per island.


class Solution {
public:
    int numIslands(vector<vector<char>>& grid) {
        int rows = grid.size();
        if (rows == 0) return 0;
        int cols = grid[0].size();
        int islandCount = 0;
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row) {
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
                if (grid[row][col] == '1') {
                    ++islandCount;
                    sinkIsland(grid, row, col, rows, cols);
                }
            }
        }
        return islandCount;
    }
private:
    void sinkIsland(vector<vector<char>>& grid, int row, int col, int rows, int cols) {
        if (row < 0 || row >= rows || col < 0 || col >= cols || grid[row][col] != '1')
            return;
        grid[row][col] = '0';
        sinkIsland(grid, row + 1, col, rows, cols);
        sinkIsland(grid, row - 1, col, rows, cols);
        sinkIsland(grid, row, col + 1, rows, cols);
        sinkIsland(grid, row, col - 1, rows, cols);
    }
};




Time: O(V + E) = O(rows × cols), Space: O(rows × cols) worst-case recursion stack. Justification: treat each cell as a vertex and each adjacency between two land cells as an edge; every cell is visited at most once, and a snake-shaped island can recurse as deep as the number of cells.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Marking the grid in place avoids a separate visited array, saving space — mention this trade-off (it mutates the input).
    • 

  • If interviewers forbid mutating the input, use a vector<vector<bool>> visited instead.
    • 

  • BFS with a queue avoids deep-recursion stack overflow on huge single islands; offer it if asked about robustness.
    • 

  • Connectivity is 4-directional here; clarify whether diagonals count before coding.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 90 of 150


90
Surrounded Regions
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a board of 'X' and 'O', capture all regions that are completely surrounded by 'X' by flipping their 'O's to 'X'. An 'O' region is not captured if any of its cells touches the border of the board. Constraints: board up to ~200×200.


Example. Board [["X","X","X"],["X","O","X"],["X","X","X"]] → the center O is fully surrounded, so it becomes X. But in [["X","O"],["X","X"]], the O is on the border, so it stays O.




Hint 1Only regions touching the border escape capture.


Hint 2Mark border-connected 'O's as safe first.


Hint 3Flood-fill from every 'O' on the boundary marking them safe; then flip all remaining 'O's to 'X' and restore the safe ones.






Brute Force. For each 'O' cell, run a flood fill to determine whether its region reaches the border; if not, flip the whole region. This re-explores regions repeatedly because nothing marks a region as "already decided," so overlapping work blows up.


class Solution {
public:
    void solve(vector<vector<char>>& board) {
        int rows = board.size();
        if (rows == 0) return;
        int cols = board[0].size();
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row) {
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
                if (board[row][col] == 'O') {
                    vector<pair<int,int>> region;
                    bool touchesBorder = false;
                    vector<vector<bool>> visited(rows, vector<bool>(cols, false));
                    collect(board, row, col, rows, cols, visited, region, touchesBorder);
                    if (!touchesBorder)
                        for (auto& cell : region)
                            board[cell.first][cell.second] = 'X';
                }
            }
        }
    }
private:
    void collect(vector<vector<char>>& board, int row, int col, int rows, int cols,
                 vector<vector<bool>>& visited, vector<pair<int,int>>& region, bool& touchesBorder) {
        if (row < 0 || row >= rows || col < 0 || col >= cols) return;
        if (visited[row][col] || board[row][col] != 'O') return;
        visited[row][col] = true;
        if (row == 0 || row == rows - 1 || col == 0 || col == cols - 1) touchesBorder = true;
        region.push_back({row, col});
        collect(board, row + 1, col, rows, cols, visited, region, touchesBorder);
        collect(board, row - 1, col, rows, cols, visited, region, touchesBorder);
        collect(board, row, col + 1, rows, cols, visited, region, touchesBorder);
        collect(board, row, col - 1, rows, cols, visited, region, touchesBorder);
    }
};




Time: O((rows × cols)²) worst case, Space: O(rows × cols). Justification: allocating a fresh visited array and exploring per starting cell can revisit the board for many 'O' cells.


Optimized. Flip the logic: instead of finding surrounded regions, find the safe ones. Any 'O' connected to a border 'O' is safe. So DFS inward from every border 'O', marking safe cells with a temporary sentinel like '#'. After that pass, every remaining 'O' must be surrounded → flip it to 'X'; restore each '#' back to 'O'.


class Solution {
public:
    void solve(vector<vector<char>>& board) {
        int rows = board.size();
        if (rows == 0) return;
        int cols = board[0].size();
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row) {
            markSafe(board, row, 0, rows, cols);
            markSafe(board, row, cols - 1, rows, cols);
        }
        for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
            markSafe(board, 0, col, rows, cols);
            markSafe(board, rows - 1, col, rows, cols);
        }
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row) {
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
                if (board[row][col] == 'O') board[row][col] = 'X';
                else if (board[row][col] == '#') board[row][col] = 'O';
            }
        }
    }
private:
    void markSafe(vector<vector<char>>& board, int row, int col, int rows, int cols) {
        if (row < 0 || row >= rows || col < 0 || col >= cols || board[row][col] != 'O')
            return;
        board[row][col] = '#';
        markSafe(board, row + 1, col, rows, cols);
        markSafe(board, row - 1, col, rows, cols);
        markSafe(board, row, col + 1, rows, cols);
        markSafe(board, row, col - 1, rows, cols);
    }
};




Time: O(V + E) = O(rows × cols), Space: O(rows × cols) recursion stack. Justification: each cell is marked at most once during the border DFS, and the final sweep is a single pass.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The "work from the boundary inward" inversion is the whole trick — say it out loud; interviewers love it.
    • 

  • A temporary sentinel character avoids a separate visited array but requires the cleanup pass; don't forget to restore '#'.
    • 

  • This is the same border-anchored idea used in Pacific Atlantic Water Flow — recognizing the family helps.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 91 of 150


91
Clone Graph
Medium


AmazonGoogleMetaMicrosoftBloombergUber




Problem. Given a reference to a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone): new node objects with the same val and the same neighbor structure, sharing nothing with the original. Each node holds an int val and a vector<Node*> neighbors. Constraints: up to ~100 nodes, values are unique 1..100.


Example. A 4-node square 1—2—3—4—1 returns a brand-new square with the same connections; modifying a clone's neighbors must not affect the original.




Hint 1You must copy nodes and edges without infinitely revisiting in cycles.


Hint 2A map from original node to its clone prevents re-cloning.


Hint 3Traverse (DFS or BFS); when first seeing a node create its clone in the map, then for each neighbor recurse/enqueue and link the cloned neighbor.






Brute Force. There isn't a slower-yet-correct alternative worth showing — any correct clone must visit every node and every edge exactly via a traversal plus a map from original→copy to handle cycles. Skipping the map causes infinite recursion on cycles, which is incorrect rather than merely slow. So the optimized DFS below is the canonical solution.


Optimized. Maintain an unordered_map<Node*, Node*> from each original node to its clone. DFS from the start node: if the original is already in the map, return its clone (this both stops cycles and reuses copies); otherwise create the clone, record it before recursing, then recurse into each neighbor and wire up the cloned neighbors.


class Solution {
public:
    Node* cloneGraph(Node* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return nullptr;
        unordered_map<Node*, Node*> originalToClone;
        return dfsClone(node, originalToClone);
    }
private:
    Node* dfsClone(Node* original, unordered_map<Node*, Node*>& originalToClone) {
        if (originalToClone.count(original))
            return originalToClone[original];
        Node* clone = new Node(original->val);
        originalToClone[original] = clone;
        for (Node* neighbor : original->neighbors)
            clone->neighbors.push_back(dfsClone(neighbor, originalToClone));
        return clone;
    }
};




Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V). Justification: every vertex is created once and every edge is traversed once; the map and recursion stack each hold up to V entries.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Inserting originalToClone[original] = clone before the recursive loop is essential — it breaks cycles. Forgetting it causes infinite recursion.
    • 

  • A BFS version with a queue works identically; create the clone when first discovered, then connect neighbors as you dequeue.
    • 

  • Clarify whether the graph can be empty (nullptr input) — handle it up front.
    •

Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 92 of 150


92
Evaluate Division
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergUber




Problem. You are given equations like a / b = 2.0 and b / c = 3.0, plus a list of queries like a / c = ?. Return each query's value, or -1.0 if it cannot be determined. Constraints: variables are strings; queries may involve unknown variables.


Example. Equations a/b = 2.0, b/c = 3.0. Query a/c → 2.0 * 3.0 = 6.0. Query x/x → -1.0 because x never appears. Query a/a → 1.0.




Hint 1Equations define a weighted directed graph where edge weights are ratios.


Hint 2A query is a path product between two variables.


Hint 3Build a graph with a/b = k as edges of weight k and b/a = 1/k; answer each query by DFS/BFS multiplying edge weights along a path, returning -1 if disconnected or a variable is unknown.






Brute Force. Model each variable as a graph vertex and each equation x/y = value as two directed weighted edges: x→y with weight value and y→x with weight 1/value. The answer to x/y is the product of edge weights along any path from x to y. The brute idea is the same DFS as the optimized one — there is no fundamentally cheaper correct method for these small inputs, so this is the standard solution.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<double> calcEquation(vector<vector<string>>& equations,
                                vector<double>& values,
                                vector<vector<string>>& queries) {
        unordered_map<string, vector<pair<string, double>>> adjacency;
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)equations.size(); ++index) {
            string numerator = equations[index][0];
            string denominator = equations[index][1];
            adjacency[numerator].push_back({denominator, values[index]});
            adjacency[denominator].push_back({numerator, 1.0 / values[index]});
        }
        vector<double> answers;
        for (auto& query : queries) {
            string source = query[0];
            string target = query[1];
            if (!adjacency.count(source) || !adjacency.count(target)) {
                answers.push_back(-1.0);
                continue;
            }
            unordered_set<string> visited;
            double result = dfsRatio(source, target, 1.0, adjacency, visited);
            answers.push_back(result);
        }
        return answers;
    }
private:
    double dfsRatio(const string& current, const string& target, double accumulated,
                    unordered_map<string, vector<pair<string, double>>>& adjacency,
                    unordered_set<string>& visited) {
        if (current == target) return accumulated;
        visited.insert(current);
        for (auto& edge : adjacency[current]) {
            const string& neighbor = edge.first;
            double weight = edge.second;
            if (!visited.count(neighbor)) {
                double result = dfsRatio(neighbor, target, accumulated * weight, adjacency, visited);
                if (result != -1.0) return result;
            }
        }
        return -1.0;
    }
};




Time: O(queries × (V + E)), Space: O(V + E). Justification: each query runs one DFS that touches every vertex and edge at most once; the adjacency map stores two edges per equation.


Optimized. The DFS above is already the typical interview answer. If asked to beat it for many repeated queries, use union-find with weights (each node stores a ratio to its set root), answering each query in near-constant time after a one-time build; or precompute all-pairs ratios with Floyd–Warshall.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<double> calcEquation(vector<vector<string>>& equations,
                                vector<double>& values,
                                vector<vector<string>>& queries) {
        unordered_map<string, string> parent;
        unordered_map<string, double> ratioToParent;
        function<void(const string&)> ensure = [&](const string& variable) {
            if (!parent.count(variable)) {
                parent[variable] = variable;
                ratioToParent[variable] = 1.0;
            }
        };
        function<pair<string,double>(string)> findRoot = [&](string variable) {
            double accumulated = 1.0;
            while (parent[variable] != variable) {
                accumulated *= ratioToParent[variable];
                variable = parent[variable];
            }
            return make_pair(variable, accumulated);
        };
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)equations.size(); ++index) {
            string numerator = equations[index][0];
            string denominator = equations[index][1];
            ensure(numerator);
            ensure(denominator);
            auto rootNumerator = findRoot(numerator);
            auto rootDenominator = findRoot(denominator);
            parent[rootNumerator.first] = rootDenominator.first;
            ratioToParent[rootNumerator.first] =
                values[index] * rootDenominator.second / rootNumerator.second;
        }
        vector<double> answers;
        for (auto& query : queries) {
            string source = query[0];
            string target = query[1];
            if (!parent.count(source) || !parent.count(target)) {
                answers.push_back(-1.0);
                continue;
            }
            auto rootSource = findRoot(source);
            auto rootTarget = findRoot(target);
            if (rootSource.first != rootTarget.first)
                answers.push_back(-1.0);
            else
                answers.push_back(rootSource.second / rootTarget.second);
        }
        return answers;
    }
};




Time: O((equations + queries) × α) ≈ near-linear, Space: O(V). Justification: union-find operations are amortized near-constant; we store one parent and one ratio per variable. Two variables are comparable only if they share a root, and the ratio between them is the quotient of their ratios to that shared root.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The graph framing — edges as multiplicative weights, query as path product — is the insight to articulate first.
    • 

  • The DFS solution is usually accepted; reach for weighted union-find only if asked to optimize repeated queries.
    • 

  • Always handle unknown variables (return -1.0) and the x/x case where x is unknown (still -1.0, not 1.0).
    • 

  • A variable divided by itself when it is known should give 1.0 — the union-find naturally returns this.
    •

Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 93 of 150


93
Course Schedule
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergApple




Problem. There are numCourses courses labeled 0..numCourses-1. prerequisites[i] = [course, requiredFirst] means you must take requiredFirst before course. Return true if you can finish all courses, false otherwise. This is exactly asking whether the directed graph has no cycle (a cycle = circular dependency = impossible).


Example. numCourses = 2, prerequisites = [[1,0]] → true (take 0, then 1). prerequisites = [[1,0],[0,1]] → false (0 needs 1 and 1 needs 0 — a cycle).




Hint 1A valid schedule exists iff the prerequisite graph has no cycle.


Hint 2Cycle detection on a directed graph is the core.


Hint 3Either do DFS tracking nodes in the current recursion stack to spot a back edge, or run Kahn's topological sort and check that all nodes get processed.






Brute Force. Repeatedly scan for a course with no remaining prerequisites, "take" it, and remove it from others' prerequisite lists; repeat until stuck. If you take all courses, return true; if you get stuck with courses remaining, there is a cycle. Done naively with repeated full scans, this is the slow version of Kahn's algorithm below.


class Solution {
public:
    bool canFinish(int numCourses, vector<vector<int>>& prerequisites) {
        vector<vector<int>> remaining(numCourses);
        for (auto& pair : prerequisites)
            remaining[pair[0]].push_back(pair[1]);
        vector<bool> taken(numCourses, false);
        int takenCount = 0;
        bool progress = true;
        while (progress) {
            progress = false;
            for (int course = 0; course < numCourses; ++course) {
                if (taken[course]) continue;
                bool ready = true;
                for (int requiredFirst : remaining[course])
                    if (!taken[requiredFirst]) { ready = false; break; }
                if (ready) {
                    taken[course] = true;
                    ++takenCount;
                    progress = true;
                }
            }
        }
        return takenCount == numCourses;
    }
};




Time: O(numCourses² + numCourses × E) worst case, Space: O(V + E). Justification: each outer pass takes at least one course, and each pass rescans all courses and their prerequisite lists.


Optimized. Use topological sort via Kahn's algorithm (BFS). Compute each course's indegree (how many prerequisites point into it). Start a queue with all indegree-0 courses (no prerequisites). Pop a course, "finish" it, and decrement the indegree of every course depending on it; when any drops to 0, enqueue it. If we finish exactly numCourses, there was no cycle.


class Solution {
public:
    bool canFinish(int numCourses, vector<vector<int>>& prerequisites) {
        vector<vector<int>> adjacency(numCourses);
        vector<int> indegree(numCourses, 0);
        for (auto& pair : prerequisites) {
            int course = pair[0];
            int requiredFirst = pair[1];
            adjacency[requiredFirst].push_back(course);
            ++indegree[course];
        }
        queue<int> ready;
        for (int course = 0; course < numCourses; ++course)
            if (indegree[course] == 0) ready.push(course);
        int finishedCount = 0;
        while (!ready.empty()) {
            int course = ready.front();
            ready.pop();
            ++finishedCount;
            for (int dependent : adjacency[course]) {
                --indegree[dependent];
                if (indegree[dependent] == 0) ready.push(dependent);
            }
        }
        return finishedCount == numCourses;
    }
};




Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V + E). Justification: each course is enqueued and dequeued once (V) and each edge is processed once when its source is finished (E).


Interview Notes.


    

  • "Can finish all courses" = "is this directed graph acyclic." Say that translation explicitly.
    • 

  • Kahn's BFS and DFS-with-colors (white/gray/black) both work; BFS indegree is easier to explain and less error-prone.
    • 

  • If finishedCount < numCourses, the leftover courses form a cycle — useful framing for the follow-up.
    •

Patterns›Graph (General)›Problem 94 of 150


94
Course Schedule II
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergUber




Problem. Same setup as #93, but now return an actual valid order to take all courses (any one is fine). If no valid order exists (a cycle), return an empty array. Constraints identical to Course Schedule.


Example. numCourses = 4, prerequisites = [[1,0],[2,0],[3,1],[3,2]] → [0,1,2,3] or [0,2,1,3] are both valid; [0,1] with [[0,1],[1,0]] → [] because of the cycle.




Hint 1This is topological ordering of the prerequisite graph.


Hint 2Process courses with no remaining prerequisites first.


Hint 3Kahn's algorithm: compute in-degrees, enqueue zero-in-degree courses, repeatedly pop and decrement neighbors' in-degrees; if you output all n courses that order is valid, otherwise there's a cycle.






Brute Force. As in #93, repeatedly pick any course whose prerequisites are all already taken and append it to the order, rescanning each round. If the order ends up shorter than numCourses, return empty. Correct but quadratic due to repeated scans.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> findOrder(int numCourses, vector<vector<int>>& prerequisites) {
        vector<vector<int>> remaining(numCourses);
        for (auto& pair : prerequisites)
            remaining[pair[0]].push_back(pair[1]);
        vector<bool> taken(numCourses, false);
        vector<int> order;
        bool progress = true;
        while (progress) {
            progress = false;
            for (int course = 0; course < numCourses; ++course) {
                if (taken[course]) continue;
                bool ready = true;
                for (int requiredFirst : remaining[course])
                    if (!taken[requiredFirst]) { ready = false; break; }
                if (ready) {
                    taken[course] = true;
                    order.push_back(course);
                    progress = true;
                }
            }
        }
        if ((int)order.size() != numCourses) return {};
        return order;
    }
};




Time: O(numCourses² + numCourses × E), Space: O(V + E). Justification: same repeated-scan structure as the #93 brute force, now also recording the order.


Optimized. Run Kahn's topological sort exactly as in #93, but record each finished course into an order vector as it is dequeued — the dequeue sequence is a valid topological order. At the end, if order.size() == numCourses, return it; otherwise a cycle exists, so return an empty vector.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> findOrder(int numCourses, vector<vector<int>>& prerequisites) {
        vector<vector<int>> adjacency(numCourses);
        vector<int> indegree(numCourses, 0);
        for (auto& pair : prerequisites) {
            int course = pair[0];
            int requiredFirst = pair[1];
            adjacency[requiredFirst].push_back(course);
            ++indegree[course];
        }
        queue<int> ready;
        for (int course = 0; course < numCourses; ++course)
            if (indegree[course] == 0) ready.push(course);
        vector<int> order;
        while (!ready.empty()) {
            int course = ready.front();
            ready.pop();
            order.push_back(course);
            for (int dependent : adjacency[course]) {
                --indegree[dependent];
                if (indegree[dependent] == 0) ready.push(dependent);
            }
        }
        if ((int)order.size() != numCourses) return {};
        return order;
    }
};




Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V + E). Justification: identical to #93 — every vertex is processed once, every edge once; the order vector adds O(V) space.


Interview Notes.


    

  • This is #93 plus "record the dequeue order" — point out it's the same algorithm with one extra line.
    • 

  • Edge direction matters: build requiredFirst → course so indegree-0 means "no prerequisites left," and the output reads in dependency order.
    • 

  • A DFS approach pushes courses onto a stack as recursion returns, then reverses it — mention it as the alternative, but Kahn's is cleaner here because cycle detection falls out for free.
    • 

  • Any valid topological order is accepted, so don't worry if yours differs from the expected sample.
    •

Patterns›Graph (BFS)›Problem 95 of 150


95
Snakes and Ladders
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloomberg




Problem. You have an n x n board numbered 1 to n*n in boustrophedon order (row by row from the bottom, alternating left-to-right and right-to-left). From square s you roll a die and may move to any of s+1 ... s+6. If a destination has a snake or ladder (board[r][c] != -1), you are immediately transported to that target square. Return the minimum number of die rolls to reach square n*n, or -1 if impossible.


Example. Board where square 2 has a ladder to 15 and square 17 has a snake to 13. Starting at 1, roll to reach 2, take ladder to 15; from 15 roll to reach n*n. Output 2 rolls. Trace: BFS level 1 reaches squares {2→15, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}; level 2 expands from those and hits the goal.




Hint 1This is a shortest-path-in-moves problem on a board graph.


Hint 2Each square connects to the next 1..6 squares (with snakes/ladders redirecting).


Hint 3BFS over board positions: from each square enqueue destinations of dice rolls 1-6 (applying any snake/ladder), tracking visited; the first time you reach the last square gives the minimum moves.






Brute Force. A naive recursive search tries every die roll sequence with backtracking and no memoization. It re-explores the same square through countless roll paths, producing exponential blowup. This is impractical and also gives no shortest-path guarantee without extra work, so it is shown only conceptually — the real challenge is the coordinate conversion, which BFS handles cleanly. We skip a runnable exponential version because it adds nothing over the optimized BFS; the brute mindset here is "DFS every path," which is strictly worse.


Time: O(6^(nn)) for unmemoized roll-sequence DFS, Space: O(nn) recursion depth — exponential and never used in practice.


Optimized. Model each square as a node; squares s+1..s+6 (after applying any snake/ladder) are its neighbors. BFS from square 1 gives the minimum rolls because every edge counts as one roll (unweighted). The only subtlety is converting a 1-based square number to a (row, col) index in the boustrophedon layout, since rows alternate direction and numbering starts from the bottom row.


class Solution {
public:
    int snakesAndLadders(vector<vector<int>>& board) {
        int boardSize = board.size();
        int finalSquare = boardSize * boardSize;

        auto squareToCoordinates = [&](int squareNumber) {
            int zeroBased = squareNumber - 1;
            int rowFromBottom = zeroBased / boardSize;
            int columnInRow = zeroBased % boardSize;
            int rowIndex = boardSize - 1 - rowFromBottom;
            int columnIndex = (rowFromBottom % 2 == 0)
                                  ? columnInRow
                                  : boardSize - 1 - columnInRow;
            return make_pair(rowIndex, columnIndex);
        };

        vector<int> rollsToReach(finalSquare + 1, -1);
        queue<int> frontier;
        frontier.push(1);
        rollsToReach[1] = 0;

        while (!frontier.empty()) {
            int currentSquare = frontier.front();
            frontier.pop();
            if (currentSquare == finalSquare) {
                return rollsToReach[currentSquare];
            }
            for (int dieFace = 1; dieFace <= 6; ++dieFace) {
                int nextSquare = currentSquare + dieFace;
                if (nextSquare > finalSquare) {
                    break;
                }
                auto [rowIndex, columnIndex] = squareToCoordinates(nextSquare);
                int destination = board[rowIndex][columnIndex];
                if (destination != -1) {
                    nextSquare = destination;
                }
                if (rollsToReach[nextSquare] == -1) {
                    rollsToReach[nextSquare] = rollsToReach[currentSquare] + 1;
                    frontier.push(nextSquare);
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};




Time: O(n^2), Space: O(n^2). There are n*n squares (nodes), each enqueued at most once, and each pops with at most 6 outgoing edges, so the work is linear in the number of squares.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The single biggest bug source is the boustrophedon conversion — practice it cold. Off-by-one (1-based vs 0-based) and the row-parity flip are the classic mistakes.
    • 

  • BFS is correct here precisely because every move costs one roll; if rolls had varying costs you would need Dijkstra.
    • 

  • Follow-up: "What if a square can hold both a snake and a ladder, or chained transports?" The problem guarantees you do not chain (you stop after one transport), so do not re-apply the board to destination — a subtle trap interviewers probe.
    • 

  • Follow-up: "Reconstruct the actual sequence of rolls." Store a parent pointer per square and backtrack from the goal.
    •
