Time: O(n^2), Space: O(1). The two nested loops examine every (start, end) pair, giving roughly n^2/2 subarrays; we only keep a few scalar variables so space is constant.


Optimized. Apply Kadane directly. Keep currentSum, the best sum of a subarray ending at the current index, and maxSum, the best over all indices. At each element, either extend the previous subarray or restart at this element — that is max(currentElement, currentSum + currentElement). Update maxSum after each step. Initializing both to the first element handles the all-negative case cleanly, since the answer is then the single largest (least negative) element.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubArray(vector<int>& nums) {
        int currentSum = nums[0];
        int maxSum = nums[0];
        for (int index = 1; index < (int)nums.size(); ++index) {
            currentSum = max(nums[index], currentSum + nums[index]);
            maxSum = max(maxSum, currentSum);
        }
        return maxSum;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single pass touches each element once and we store only two scalars regardless of input size.


Interview Notes.


    

  • All-negative edge case: do not initialize maxSum to 0, or you will wrongly return 0 for an array like [-3, -1, -2]; initialize to nums[0] (or INT_MIN) so the least-negative element wins.
    • 

  • Follow-up "return the subarray indices": track a tentative tempStart that you reset whenever you restart currentSum at the current element, and commit bestStart/bestEnd whenever currentSum exceeds maxSum.
    • 

  • This is the canonical 1D DP collapse — interviewers love hearing you name the recurrence dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1] + nums[i]) and then explain why only the previous state matters.
    • 

  • A common follow-up is the divide-and-conquer solution (O(n log n)) — know it exists, but Kadane is strictly better and is the expected answer.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Kadane's Algorithm›Problem 113 of 150


113
Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a circular integer array nums, find the maximum possible sum of a non-empty subarray, where the subarray may wrap around the end and continue from the beginning. Each element may be used at most once (no element appears twice in the chosen subarray). Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 10^4, values are 32-bit ints.


Example. Input nums = [5, -3, 5] → Output 10. Trace: the best subarray wraps around — take the last 5 and the first 5 (indices 2 then 0), giving 5 + 5 = 10, which beats the non-wrapping best of 5.




Hint 1The answer is either a normal subarray or one that wraps around the ends.


Hint 2A wrapping max equals total minus the minimum subarray.


Hint 3Compute the standard max subarray (Kadane); also compute total minus the minimum subarray; return the larger, but if all numbers are negative return the plain Kadane max.






Brute Force. The answer is either a normal (non-wrapping) subarray or a wrapping one. Simulate wrapping by checking subarrays that start at each index and extend up to n elements using modular indexing, accumulating the sum and tracking the maximum.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubarraySumCircular(vector<int>& nums) {
        int arraySize = nums.size();
        int maxSum = nums[0];
        for (int start = 0; start < arraySize; ++start) {
            int currentSum = 0;
            for (int length = 0; length < arraySize; ++length) {
                currentSum += nums[(start + length) % arraySize];
                maxSum = max(maxSum, currentSum);
            }
        }
        return maxSum;
    }
};




Time: O(n^2), Space: O(1). The outer loop fixes a start, the inner loop extends up to n elements with wraparound, and we keep only scalar accumulators.


Optimized. Key insight: a wrapping subarray is exactly the total array sum minus a contiguous non-wrapping subarray in the middle. So the best wrapping sum is totalSum - minSubarraySum, where minSubarraySum is the smallest contiguous sum found by running Kadane with min instead of max. The overall answer is max(maxStraight, totalSum - minSubarray). The critical edge case: if every element is negative, then totalSum - minSubarray equals 0 (you'd be "removing" the entire array, leaving an empty subarray, which is not allowed), so when maxStraight < 0 you must return maxStraight alone.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubarraySumCircular(vector<int>& nums) {
        int totalSum = 0;
        int currentMax = nums[0];
        int bestStraight = nums[0];
        int currentMin = nums[0];
        int bestWrappedComplement = nums[0];
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); ++index) {
            int value = nums[index];
            totalSum += value;
            if (index == 0) continue;
            currentMax = max(value, currentMax + value);
            bestStraight = max(bestStraight, currentMax);
            currentMin = min(value, currentMin + value);
            bestWrappedComplement = min(bestWrappedComplement, currentMin);
        }
        if (bestStraight < 0) {
            return bestStraight;
        }
        return max(bestStraight, totalSum - bestWrappedComplement);
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). We run two Kadane passes (max and min) fused into one loop and keep only a handful of scalars.


Interview Notes.


    

  • All-negative edge case is the trap here: totalSum - minSubarray collapses to an empty subarray, so guard with if (bestStraight < 0) return bestStraight; before considering the wrap.
    • 

  • Explain the decomposition out loud: the maximum wrapping subarray = total minus the minimum internal subarray, because keeping the prefix+suffix is the same as discarding the middle.
    • 

  • "Return the indices" follow-up is harder when the answer wraps — you track start/end for both the max-Kadane and min-Kadane passes, and for the wrap case the chosen subarray is the complement of the min-subarray (a suffix joined to a prefix).
    • 

  • Mention you fold both passes into a single loop for clarity and cache-friendliness; doing two separate scans is also fine and arguably easier to read under pressure.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 114 of 150


114
Search Insert Position
Easy✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. Given a sorted array of distinct integers and a target value, return the index where the target is found. If it is not present, return the index where it would be inserted to keep the array sorted. Constraints: the array is sorted ascending, sizes up to 10^4, values fit in int.


Example. nums = [1, 3, 5, 6], target = 5 → output 2 (found at index 2). For target = 2 → output 1 (would slot between 1 and 3). Trace for target 2: window shrinks until low lands on the first element >= 2, which is index 1.




Hint 1The array is sorted, so binary search applies.


Hint 2You want the leftmost position where the target could go.


Hint 3Binary search for target; return the index if found, otherwise the low pointer where the search converges (the insertion point).






Brute Force. Walk left to right and return the first index whose value is greater than or equal to the target; if none qualifies, the target belongs at the end.


class Solution {
public:
    int searchInsert(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] >= target) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return nums.size();
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). We may scan the entire array once in the worst case, using only a loop counter.


Optimized. This is the classic "lower bound" search: find the leftmost index whose value is >= target. Use the low < high template where high starts at nums.size() (one past the end) so the answer can legitimately be the insertion-at-end position. Whenever nums[mid] >= target, that index is a candidate, so move high to mid; otherwise the answer is strictly to the right, so move low to mid + 1.


class Solution {
public:
    int searchInsert(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size();
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] >= target) {
                high = mid;
            } else {
                low = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Each iteration halves the window, and we keep only a few index variables.


Interview Notes.


    

  • This is the std::lower_bound pattern — mentioning the STL equivalent (lower_bound(nums.begin(), nums.end(), target) - nums.begin()) signals fluency.
    • 

  • Note why high = nums.size() not nums.size() - 1: the valid output range is [0, n], and an exclusive upper bound captures the insert-at-end case cleanly.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "Now the array has duplicates — return the first occurrence" is the same lower-bound code unchanged.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 115 of 150


115
Search a 2D Matrix
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobeApple




Problem. Given an m x n matrix where each row is sorted ascending and the first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row, determine whether a target value exists. Constraints: the whole matrix is effectively one sorted sequence read row by row.


Example. matrix = [[1,3,5,7],[10,11,16,20],[23,30,34,60]], target = 3 → true. Treating it as the flat sorted list [1,3,5,7,10,...], index 1 holds 3.




Hint 1The matrix is sorted as if it were one long sorted array.


Hint 2Treat the 2D grid as a flattened sorted sequence.


Hint 3Binary search over indices 0..m*n-1, converting mid to (mid / cols, mid % cols) to read the value, narrowing toward the target.






Brute Force. Inspect every cell and compare against the target.


class Solution {
public:
    bool searchMatrix(vector<vector<int>>& matrix, int target) {
        for (const vector<int>& row : matrix) {
            for (int value : row) {
                if (value == target) {
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};




Time: O(m·n), Space: O(1). We may touch every one of the m·n cells.


Optimized. The key insight is the strong ordering guarantee: reading the matrix row by row produces one fully sorted sequence, so we can binary-search the virtual flat array of length m·n. Map a flat index back to a 2D cell with row = mid / columnCount and column = mid % columnCount.


class Solution {
public:
    bool searchMatrix(vector<vector<int>>& matrix, int target) {
        int rowCount = matrix.size();
        int columnCount = matrix[0].size();
        int low = 0;
        int high = rowCount * columnCount - 1;
        while (low <= high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            int value = matrix[mid / columnCount][mid % columnCount];
            if (value == target) {
                return true;
            } else if (value < target) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};




Time: O(log(m·n)), Space: O(1). One binary search over m·n virtual elements.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The index mapping mid / columnCount and mid % columnCount is the whole trick — practice it until it is automatic.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: the related problem "Search a 2D Matrix II" weakens the guarantee (rows and columns sorted, but rows are not chained). There the flat-array trick fails; the answer is staircase search from the top-right corner in O(m + n). Knowing the difference between the two problems is a frequent gotcha.
    • 

  • Here low <= high with an inclusive high = m*n - 1 is the natural fit since we want exact-match detection.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 116 of 150


116
Find Peak Element
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergApple




Problem. A peak element is one strictly greater than its neighbors. Given an array where nums[i] != nums[i+1] for all valid i, return the index of any peak. Imagine nums[-1] and nums[n] as negative infinity, so the ends can be peaks. Constraints: must run in O(log n).


Example. nums = [1, 2, 3, 1] → 2 (value 3 is greater than both neighbors). For nums = [1, 2, 1, 3, 5, 6, 4], either index 1 or index 5 is a valid answer.




Hint 1You only need any peak, and a peak must exist toward the larger neighbor.


Hint 2Binary search on the slope direction.


Hint 3If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1] a peak lies to the right, so move low up; otherwise a peak is at mid or left, so move high down, until they converge.






Brute Force. Scan and return the first index larger than its right neighbor (the boundaries are handled by the imagined infinities).


class Solution {
public:
    int findPeakElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        for (int index = 0; index + 1 < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] > nums[index + 1]) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return nums.size() - 1;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single linear pass.


Optimized. The monotonic insight is subtle because the array is not sorted: compare nums[mid] with nums[mid + 1]. If nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1], the slope is rising, so a peak must exist to the right — move low to mid + 1. Otherwise the slope is falling (or mid itself is a peak), so a peak exists at mid or to its left — move high to mid. Using low < high guarantees mid + 1 is always a valid index and the window converges to a single peak.


class Solution {
public:
    int findPeakElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1]) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). We discard half the array each step even without global sorting.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The eye-opener for interviewers: binary search needs a monotonic decision, not a sorted array. "Which half is guaranteed to contain a peak?" is the predicate here.
    • 

  • Why low < high and not <=: with <= and high = mid you risk an infinite loop when low == high == mid. Converging on a single survivor avoids that.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "find a peak in a 2D grid" extends the same divide-and-conquer idea along a column/row and is a known hard variant.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 117 of 150


117
Search in Rotated Sorted Array
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftMetaGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. A sorted array of distinct values was rotated at some unknown pivot (e.g. [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] became [4,5,6,7,0,1,2]). Given the rotated array and a target, return its index or -1. Constraints: must run in O(log n).


Example. nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], target = 0 → 4. Trace: at mid = 3 (value 7) the left half [4,5,6,7] is sorted but 0 is not inside it, so search right.




Hint 1Rotation means one half of any range is always sorted.


Hint 2Binary search deciding which half is sorted and whether the target lies in it.


Hint 3At each mid, determine the sorted half; if the target falls within that sorted half's range search there, otherwise search the other half.






Brute Force. Linear scan for the target.


class Solution {
public:
    int search(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] == target) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Every element may be visited.


Optimized. The insight: at any mid, at least one of the two halves is properly sorted. Detect which one by comparing nums[low] with nums[mid]. If the left half is sorted and the target lies within its value range, search left; otherwise search right. Symmetrically for a sorted right half. This keeps each step O(log n) despite the rotation.


class Solution {
public:
    int search(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low <= high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] == target) {
                return mid;
            }
            if (nums[low] <= nums[mid]) {
                if (nums[low] <= target && target < nums[mid]) {
                    high = mid - 1;
                } else {
                    low = mid + 1;
                }
            } else {
                if (nums[mid] < target && target <= nums[high]) {
                    low = mid + 1;
                } else {
                    high = mid - 1;
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). One half is always discarded.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The nums[low] <= nums[mid] check (using <=, not <) handles the two-element window correctly — a classic off-by-one trap.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "what if duplicates are allowed?" (LeetCode 81) breaks the sorted-half detection when nums[low] == nums[mid] == nums[high]; you fall back to shrinking both ends by one, degrading worst case to O(n).
    • 

  • Be ready to explain why exactly one half is sorted — the rotation point can sit in only one of them.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 118 of 150


118
Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a sorted array, return the starting and ending indices of a target value as [first, last]. If the target is absent, return [-1, -1]. Constraints: must run in O(log n); values may repeat.


Example. nums = [5,7,7,8,8,10], target = 8 → [3, 4]. For target = 6 → [-1, -1].




Hint 1You need two boundaries, so run binary search twice.


Hint 2Bias the search left for the first occurrence and right for the last.


Hint 3Binary search for the leftmost index >= target (first) and the rightmost index <= target (last); if the target isn't present return [-1,-1].






Brute Force. Scan once to record the first match and keep updating the last match.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> searchRange(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int first = -1;
        int last = -1;
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] == target) {
                if (first == -1) {
                    first = index;
                }
                last = index;
            }
        }
        return {first, last};
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One full pass; the output vector is constant size.


Optimized. Run two boundary binary searches. The first finds the leftmost index >= target (lower bound); the second finds the leftmost index > target, whose predecessor is the last occurrence. After computing the lower bound, confirm it actually points at the target before trusting it.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> searchRange(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int first = lowerBound(nums, target);
        if (first == (int)nums.size() || nums[first] != target) {
            return {-1, -1};
        }
        int last = lowerBound(nums, target + 1) - 1;
        return {first, last};
    }
private:
    int lowerBound(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size();
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] >= target) {
                high = mid;
            } else {
                low = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Two logarithmic searches, only index variables stored.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Reusing one lowerBound helper twice (target and target + 1) is cleaner than writing separate first/last searches and avoids subtle boundary bugs.
    • 

  • Watch the overflow edge: if target is INT_MAX, target + 1 overflows. Mention guarding it or searching for the upper bound directly in a strict interview.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "count occurrences of the target" is just last - first + 1, a one-line extension once you have both boundaries.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 119 of 150


119
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. A sorted array of distinct values was rotated at an unknown pivot. Return the minimum element. Constraints: must run in O(log n).


Example. nums = [3,4,5,1,2] → 1. For nums = [11,13,15,17] (rotated zero times) → 11.




Hint 1The minimum is the only place where order 'breaks' in a rotated sorted array.


Hint 2Binary search comparing mid to the rightmost element.


Hint 3If nums[mid] > nums[high], the min is to the right (low = mid+1), else it's at mid or left (high = mid); converge on the minimum.






Brute Force. Scan and track the smallest value.


class Solution {
public:
    int findMin(vector<int>& nums) {
        int minimum = nums[0];
        for (int value : nums) {
            if (value < minimum) {
                minimum = value;
            }
        }
        return minimum;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A linear pass over all values.


Optimized. The minimum is the single "inflection point" where the rotation wraps. Compare nums[mid] with nums[high]: if nums[mid] > nums[high], the minimum must lie strictly to the right of mid, so move low to mid + 1; otherwise the minimum is at mid or to its left, so move high to mid. Comparing against high (not low) is what makes the non-rotated case work correctly.


class Solution {
public:
    int findMin(vector<int>& nums) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] > nums[high]) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid;
            }
        }
        return nums[low];
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Half the window is dropped each iteration.


Interview Notes.


    

  • A common mistake is comparing nums[mid] to nums[low]; that fails on an already-sorted (zero-rotation) array. Anchor on high instead.
    • 

  • The low < high template is deliberate: the loop converges to the single minimum without ever reading an out-of-bounds index.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "with duplicates" (LeetCode 154) — when nums[mid] == nums[high] you cannot tell which side holds the min, so decrement high, pushing the worst case to O(n).
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 120 of 150


120
Median of Two Sorted Arrays
Hard


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftAppleAdobeBloomberg




Problem. Given two sorted arrays, return the median of the combined set of all elements, without actually merging them. Constraints: the official requirement is O(log(m + n)) time; total length can be even or odd.


Example. nums1 = [1, 3], nums2 = [2] → 2.0 (merged [1,2,3], middle element). nums1 = [1, 2], nums2 = [3, 4] → 2.5 (average of the two middles of [1,2,3,4]).




Hint 1Logarithmic time means binary searching the partition, not merging.


Hint 2Partition both arrays so the left halves hold the smaller half of all elements.


Hint 3Binary search the smaller array's cut so that maxLeftA <= minRightB and maxLeftB <= minRightA; the median comes from the boundary values depending on parity of the total length.






Brute Force. Merge the two arrays in sorted order, then pick the middle element(s).


class Solution {
public:
    double findMedianSortedArrays(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2) {
        vector<int> merged;
        merged.reserve(nums1.size() + nums2.size());
        int firstIndex = 0;
        int secondIndex = 0;
        while (firstIndex < (int)nums1.size() && secondIndex < (int)nums2.size()) {
            if (nums1[firstIndex] <= nums2[secondIndex]) {
                merged.push_back(nums1[firstIndex++]);
            } else {
                merged.push_back(nums2[secondIndex++]);
            }
        }
        while (firstIndex < (int)nums1.size()) {
            merged.push_back(nums1[firstIndex++]);
        }
        while (secondIndex < (int)nums2.size()) {
            merged.push_back(nums2[secondIndex++]);
        }
        int total = merged.size();
        if (total % 2 == 1) {
            return merged[total / 2];
        }
        return (merged[total / 2 - 1] + merged[total / 2]) / 2.0;
    }
};




Time: O(m + n), Space: O(m + n). We build the full merged array.


Optimized. The O(log(m + n)) approach is partition-based binary search. Choose a cut in the smaller array; the cut in the larger array is then forced so that exactly half of all elements sit on the left side. A partition is valid when every element on the left is <= every element on the right, checked via maxLeftFirst <= minRightSecond and maxLeftSecond <= minRightFirst. Binary-search the cut position in the smaller array, using sentinels (INT_MIN/INT_MAX) for empty sides. Always binary-search the smaller array to keep the cut range valid.


class Solution {
public:
    double findMedianSortedArrays(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2) {
        if (nums1.size() > nums2.size()) {
            return findMedianSortedArrays(nums2, nums1);
        }
        int firstSize = nums1.size();
        int secondSize = nums2.size();
        int low = 0;
        int high = firstSize;
        int halfLength = (firstSize + secondSize + 1) / 2;
        while (low <= high) {
            int cutFirst = low + (high - low) / 2;
            int cutSecond = halfLength - cutFirst;
            int maxLeftFirst = (cutFirst == 0) ? INT_MIN : nums1[cutFirst - 1];
            int minRightFirst = (cutFirst == firstSize) ? INT_MAX : nums1[cutFirst];
            int maxLeftSecond = (cutSecond == 0) ? INT_MIN : nums2[cutSecond - 1];
            int minRightSecond = (cutSecond == secondSize) ? INT_MAX : nums2[cutSecond];
            if (maxLeftFirst <= minRightSecond && maxLeftSecond <= minRightFirst) {
                if ((firstSize + secondSize) % 2 == 1) {
                    return max(maxLeftFirst, maxLeftSecond);
                }
                return (max(maxLeftFirst, maxLeftSecond) + min(minRightFirst, minRightSecond)) / 2.0;
            } else if (maxLeftFirst > minRightSecond) {
                high = cutFirst - 1;
            } else {
                low = cutFirst + 1;
            }
        }
        return 0.0;
    }
};




Time: O(log(min(m, n))), Space: O(1). We binary-search only the smaller array's cut positions and store a handful of boundary values.


Interview Notes.


    

  • This is one of the hardest "Top 150" problems; many interviewers accept the clean O(log(m + n)) merge-walk (advance through (m+n)/2 elements with two pointers) if you cannot finish the partition version under pressure — but the partition method is the gold standard.
    • 

  • The forced relationship cutSecond = halfLength - cutFirst is the heart of it; halfLength = (m + n + 1) / 2 makes the odd-length case put the median on the left side automatically.
    • 

  • Always recurse to search the smaller array first; otherwise cutSecond can go negative and the sentinels break.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "find the k-th smallest of two sorted arrays" generalizes this and is a frequent harder variant.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 121 of 150


121
Kth Largest Element in an Array
Medium


AmazonMetaMicrosoftGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k-th largest element in sorted order (not the k-th distinct element). Constraints: 1 <= k <= nums.size() <= 10^5, values can be negative.


Example. nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2 → 5. Sorted descending is [6,5,4,3,2,1]; the 2nd element is 5.




Hint 1You don't need to fully sort to find the kth largest.


Hint 2A min-heap of size k, or quickselect, gives it efficiently.


Hint 3Maintain a size-k min-heap (its root is the kth largest), or use quickselect partitioning around a pivot until the pivot lands at index n-k.






Brute Force. Sort the whole array in descending order and index into position k - 1. Simple and correct, but it does more work than needed — we sort all n elements just to read one.


class Solution {
public:
    int findKthLargest(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        sort(nums.begin(), nums.end(), greater<int>());
        return nums[k - 1];
    }
};




Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1). Sorting dominates; the comparison-based sort visits all n elements log n times. No extra storage beyond the sort's internal stack.


Optimized. Maintain a min-heap of size k. Push elements in; whenever the heap exceeds size k, pop the smallest. After processing everything, the heap holds the k largest elements and its top (the smallest of those) is exactly the k-th largest. We never store more than k items, and each push/pop is O(log k).


class Solution {
public:
    int findKthLargest(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> minHeap;
        for (int currentNumber : nums) {
            minHeap.push(currentNumber);
            if (minHeap.size() > static_cast<size_t>(k)) {
                minHeap.pop();
            }
        }
        return minHeap.top();
    }
};




Time: O(n log k), Space: O(k). Each of the n elements triggers an O(log k) heap operation; the heap is capped at k elements.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The optimal-on-average solution is Quickselect (partition like quicksort but recurse into only one side): O(n) average time, O(n^2) worst case, O(1) extra space. Interviewers often want you to mention it.
    • 

  • If asked to guarantee worst-case O(n), reference the median-of-medians pivot strategy.
    • 

  • Clarify whether duplicates count separately — here they do (k-th largest by position, not distinct value).
    • 

  • The heap solution is preferred when data streams in and you can't hold all of it; Quickselect needs the full array in memory.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 122 of 150


122
IPO
Hard


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergGoldman Sachs




Problem. You can complete at most k projects. Each project i has a profits[i] and a capital[i] requirement. You start with w capital; finishing a project adds its profit to your capital. You can only start a project if your current capital is at least its requirement, and each project is done at most once. Maximize final capital. Constraints: up to 10^5 projects.


Example. k = 2, w = 0, profits = [1,2,3], capital = [0,1,1] → 4. With capital 0 only project 0 is affordable (profit 1, capital → 1). Now projects 1 and 2 are affordable; pick the bigger profit 3 (capital → 4). Done with 2 projects.




Hint 1You can do at most k projects, each time picking the best affordable one.


Hint 2A max-heap of profits among currently affordable projects drives the greedy choice.


Hint 3Sort projects by capital; with each of the k rounds, push all now-affordable projects' profits into a max-heap and take the top, adding it to your capital.






Brute Force. Repeat k times: scan every not-yet-done project, find the affordable one with the highest profit, take it, add its profit. Each of the k rounds rescans all n projects.


class Solution {
public:
    int findMaximizedCapital(int k, int w, vector<int>& profits, vector<int>& capital) {
        int projectCount = profits.size();
        vector<bool> alreadyDone(projectCount, false);
        for (int round = 0; round < k; ++round) {
            int bestIndex = -1;
            for (int projectIndex = 0; projectIndex < projectCount; ++projectIndex) {
                if (!alreadyDone[projectIndex] && capital[projectIndex] <= w) {
                    if (bestIndex == -1 || profits[projectIndex] > profits[bestIndex]) {
                        bestIndex = projectIndex;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (bestIndex == -1) break;
            w += profits[bestIndex];
            alreadyDone[bestIndex] = true;
        }
        return w;
    }
};




Time: O(k·n), Space: O(n). Each round is a full O(n) scan; the boolean array tracks completed projects.


Optimized. This is a greedy problem: at every step take the affordable project with the largest profit. Sort projects by capital requirement so we can cheaply unlock them as our capital grows. Keep a max-heap of profits of all currently affordable projects. Each round, move every newly-affordable project (capital ≤ w) into the heap, then pop the heap's top — the biggest reachable profit.


class Solution {
public:
    int findMaximizedCapital(int k, int w, vector<int>& profits, vector<int>& capital) {
        int projectCount = profits.size();
        vector<pair<int, int>> projectsByCapital;
        for (int projectIndex = 0; projectIndex < projectCount; ++projectIndex) {
            projectsByCapital.push_back({capital[projectIndex], profits[projectIndex]});
        }
        sort(projectsByCapital.begin(), projectsByCapital.end());

        priority_queue<int> affordableProfits;
        int nextProject = 0;
        for (int round = 0; round < k; ++round) {
            while (nextProject < projectCount && projectsByCapital[nextProject].first <= w) {
                affordableProfits.push(projectsByCapital[nextProject].second);
                ++nextProject;
            }
            if (affordableProfits.empty()) break;
            w += affordableProfits.top();
            affordableProfits.pop();
        }
        return w;
    }
};




Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Sorting is O(n log n); each project enters and leaves the heap at most once, costing O(n log n) total over all rounds.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The key realization is that doing a project never reduces capital, so the set of affordable projects only grows — that is what makes pure greedy correct here.
    • 

  • Sorting by capital lets the nextProject pointer advance monotonically; you never re-scan unlocked projects.
    • 

  • A common follow-up: what if profits could be negative? Then greedy breaks and you'd need DP — interviewers like probing whether you understand why greedy works.
    • 

  • Watch the early exit: if no project is affordable, no future project will be either, so break immediately.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 123 of 150


123
Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftLinkedInBloomberg




Problem. Given two sorted integer arrays nums1 and nums2 and an integer k, find the k pairs (u, v) with u from nums1 and v from nums2 that have the smallest sums. Return them as a list of pairs. Constraints: arrays can each be up to 10^5 long, so the total pair count m·n can be huge.


Example. nums1 = [1,7,11], nums2 = [2,4,6], k = 3 → [[1,2],[1,4],[1,6]]. Their sums 3, 5, 7 are the three smallest among all nine pairs.




Hint 1The smallest sums come from pairing small elements of both arrays.


Hint 2A min-heap can expand candidate pairs in increasing-sum order.


Hint 3Seed the heap with pairs (nums1[i], nums2[0]); pop the smallest, record it, and push the neighbor (i, j+1) to grow the frontier until you have k pairs.






Brute Force. Generate all m·n pairs, sort them by sum, take the first k. Correct but builds and sorts a potentially enormous list.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> kSmallestPairs(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2, int k) {
        vector<vector<int>> allPairs;
        for (int firstValue : nums1) {
            for (int secondValue : nums2) {
                allPairs.push_back({firstValue, secondValue});
            }
        }
        sort(allPairs.begin(), allPairs.end(), [](const vector<int>& leftPair, const vector<int>& rightPair) {
            return leftPair[0] + leftPair[1] < rightPair[0] + rightPair[1];
        });
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        for (int pairIndex = 0; pairIndex < k && pairIndex < static_cast<int>(allPairs.size()); ++pairIndex) {
            result.push_back(allPairs[pairIndex]);
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(m·n log(m·n)), Space: O(m·n). We materialize every pair and sort the lot.


Optimized. Use a min-heap of candidate pairs ordered by sum. Because both arrays are sorted, the smallest pair must be (nums1[0], nums2[0]). Seed the heap with pairs (nums1[i], nums2[0]) for each i. Each time we pop the smallest pair (i, j), we add its sum to the result and push the next candidate in that row, (i, j+1). This explores only the frontier of promising pairs rather than all of them.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> kSmallestPairs(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2, int k) {
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        if (nums1.empty() || nums2.empty()) return result;

        priority_queue<tuple<int, int, int>, vector<tuple<int, int, int>>, greater<tuple<int, int, int>>> minHeap;
        for (int firstIndex = 0; firstIndex < static_cast<int>(nums1.size()) && firstIndex < k; ++firstIndex) {
            minHeap.push({nums1[firstIndex] + nums2[0], firstIndex, 0});
        }

        while (!minHeap.empty() && static_cast<int>(result.size()) < k) {
            auto [currentSum, firstIndex, secondIndex] = minHeap.top();
            minHeap.pop();
            result.push_back({nums1[firstIndex], nums2[secondIndex]});
            if (secondIndex + 1 < static_cast<int>(nums2.size())) {
                minHeap.push({nums1[firstIndex] + nums2[secondIndex + 1], firstIndex, secondIndex + 1});
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(k log k), Space: O(k). The heap holds at most about k entries, and we pop k times, each pop/push costing O(log k).


Interview Notes.


    

  • Storing the sum as the first element of the tuple lets the default tuple comparison sort by sum automatically — a clean trick worth explaining.
    • 

  • We seed at most k rows (firstIndex < k) because we can never need more than k pairs, which keeps the heap small even when nums1 is enormous.
    • 

  • This is the same pattern as "merge k sorted lists" — each row i of nums1 paired with nums2 is a sorted sequence, and we are merging them.
    • 

  • Edge cases interviewers check: empty arrays, and k larger than m·n (return all pairs).
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 124 of 150


124
Find Median from Data Stream
Hard


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergApple




Problem. Design a data structure that supports addNum(int num) to ingest numbers one at a time from a stream, and findMedian() to return the median of all numbers seen so far. The median is the middle value when the count is odd, or the average of the two middle values when even. Constraints: up to 5·10^4 calls, so each operation must be fast.


Example. Add 1, add 2 → median 1.5 (average of 1 and 2). Add 3 → median 2 (middle of 1, 2, 3).




Hint 1You need the running median, which depends on the middle one or two values.


Hint 2Two heaps split the data into a lower and upper half.


Hint 3Maintain a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half, kept balanced in size; the median is a heap top or the average of both tops.






Brute Force. Keep all numbers in a vector. On each findMedian, sort the vector and read the middle. Easy to reason about but every query re-sorts.


class MedianFinder {
public:
    vector<int> allNumbers;

    MedianFinder() {}

    void addNum(int num) {
        allNumbers.push_back(num);
    }

    double findMedian() {
        sort(allNumbers.begin(), allNumbers.end());
        int totalCount = allNumbers.size();
        if (totalCount % 2 == 1) {
            return allNumbers[totalCount / 2];
        }
        return (allNumbers[totalCount / 2 - 1] + allNumbers[totalCount / 2]) / 2.0;
    }
};




Time: O(1) add, O(n log n) per findMedian, Space: O(n). Each query pays a full sort.


Optimized. Use the two-heap technique. Keep a max-heap (lowerHalf) for the smaller half of the numbers and a min-heap (upperHalf) for the larger half. Maintain the invariant that lowerHalf has the same number of elements as upperHalf or exactly one more. The median is then either the top of lowerHalf (odd total) or the average of the two tops (even total). On each insert, push into lowerHalf, hand its top over to upperHalf, and rebalance if upperHalf grew larger — this keeps both halves correctly partitioned.


class MedianFinder {
public:
    priority_queue<int> lowerHalf;
    priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> upperHalf;

    MedianFinder() {}

    void addNum(int num) {
        lowerHalf.push(num);
        upperHalf.push(lowerHalf.top());
        lowerHalf.pop();
        if (upperHalf.size() > lowerHalf.size()) {
            lowerHalf.push(upperHalf.top());
            upperHalf.pop();
        }
    }

    double findMedian() {
        if (lowerHalf.size() > upperHalf.size()) {
            return lowerHalf.top();
        }
        return (lowerHalf.top() + upperHalf.top()) / 2.0;
    }
};




Time: O(log n) per addNum, O(1) per findMedian, Space: O(n). Inserts do a constant number of O(log n) heap operations; the median is read directly from the tops.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The "push to lowerHalf, move its max to upperHalf, rebalance" dance is the cleanest way to keep both halves sorted relative to each other without comparing values yourself.
    • 

  • The invariant lowerHalf.size() ∈ {upperHalf.size(), upperHalf.size() + 1} is what makes findMedian O(1) — state it explicitly in an interview.
    • 

  • Common follow-up: if all numbers are in a small fixed range (e.g., 0–100), a counting/bucket approach gives O(1) add; if 99% of values fall in a range, mention a hybrid of buckets plus heaps for the tails.
    • 

  • Another follow-up is a sliding-window median, which needs removals — heaps don't delete arbitrary elements cheaply, so you'd reach for a balanced BST or an ordered multiset (std::multiset) instead.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 125 of 150


125
Add Binary
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergFacebook




Problem. Given two non-empty binary strings a and b, return their sum as a binary string. Inputs contain only '0' and '1', have no leading zeros (except the string "0" itself), and can be up to 10^4 characters long — far longer than any built-in integer can hold, so you must add them as strings.
Example. a = "11", b = "1" → "100". Trace from the right: 1+1 = 10 (write 0, carry 1); next 1+0+carry 1 = 10 (write 0, carry 1); no digits left but carry 1 (write 1). Reading the written bits bottom-to-top gives 100.
Brute Force. Convert both strings to integers, add, convert back. This is conceptually simple but breaks immediately: a 10^4-bit number overflows long long (which holds ~63 bits), so this approach is wrong for the stated constraints, not merely slow. We include it only to show why string addition is necessary.


class Solution {
public:
    string addBinary(string a, string b) {
        unsigned long long left = stoull(a, nullptr, 2);
        unsigned long long right = stoull(b, nullptr, 2);
        unsigned long long total = left + right;
        if (total == 0) return "0";
        string result;
        while (total > 0) {
            result.push_back(char('0' + (total & 1)));
            total >>= 1;
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n) — but only valid when both numbers fit in 64 bits; for the real constraints it produces a wrong answer.
Optimized. Walk both strings from the least-significant end (the rightmost character), summing matching digits plus a running carry. At each position the column sum is 0..3; the output bit is sum & 1 (sum modulo 2) and the next carry is sum >> 1 (sum divided by 2). Continue while either string has digits left or a carry remains, then reverse the collected bits.


class Solution {
public:
    string addBinary(string a, string b) {
        string result;
        int i = a.size() - 1;
        int j = b.size() - 1;
        int carry = 0;
        while (i >= 0 || j >= 0 || carry) {
            int sum = carry;
            if (i >= 0) sum += a[i--] - '0';
            if (j >= 0) sum += b[j--] - '0';
            result.push_back(char('0' + (sum & 1)));
            carry = sum >> 1;
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(max(n, m)), Space: O(max(n, m)) — one pass over the longer string; the result string is the only extra storage and its length is at most one more than the longer input.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The whole problem is just elementary-school column addition in base 2; the only bit tricks are sum & 1 for the digit and sum >> 1 for the carry.
    • 

  • Building the result back-to-front and reversing once at the end is O(n); repeatedly prepending to the front would be O(n^2).
    • 

  • The same skeleton solves "Add Strings" (base 10) — swap & 1/>> 1 for % 10// 10.
    • 

  • Mention that you deliberately avoided integer conversion because of overflow; interviewers like hearing you anticipate the long-input constraint.
    • 





Hint 1Add like grade school, but in base 2 and from the right.


Hint 2Track a carry while walking both strings backward.


Hint 3Iterate from the ends summing bits plus carry, append sum%2, carry sum/2; prepend a leading 1 if carry remains, then reverse.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 126 of 150


126
Reverse Bits
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftAppleGoogleBloomberg




Problem. Given a 32-bit unsigned integer, return the integer formed by reversing the order of its 32 bits (bit 0 swaps with bit 31, bit 1 with bit 30, and so on). The input is always treated as exactly 32 bits wide.
Example. n = 43261596 = 00000010100101000001111010011100. Reversing the 32-bit string gives 00111001011110000010100101000000 = 964176192.
Brute Force. Read the 32 bits into a string from the input, reverse the string, parse it back to an integer. It works but allocates a heap string and does character parsing for a pure-arithmetic task.


class Solution {
public:
    uint32_t reverseBits(uint32_t n) {
        string bits;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            bits.push_back(char('0' + (n & 1)));
            n >>= 1;
        }
        return static_cast<uint32_t>(stoul(bits, nullptr, 2));
    }
};




Time: O(32) = O(1), Space: O(32) = O(1) — fixed 32 iterations and a fixed-length string; constant, but with unnecessary allocation.
Optimized. Build the answer bit by bit without any string. For each of the 32 positions, shift result left to open a new low slot, drop in the current lowest bit of n via n & 1, then shift n right to expose the next bit. Pulling bits off the low end of n while pushing them onto the low end of a left-shifting result reverses their order naturally.


class Solution {
public:
    uint32_t reverseBits(uint32_t n) {
        uint32_t result = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            result = (result << 1) | (n & 1);
            n >>= 1;
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(1), Space: O(1) — exactly 32 iterations of constant work and a single integer of storage.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The type is uint32_t for a reason: right-shifting a signed negative int is implementation-defined and may sign-extend (copy the sign bit in), corrupting the reversal. Always reverse on an unsigned type.
    • 

  • Follow-up: "If this is called millions of times, how do you speed it up?" Precompute a 256-entry lookup table that reverses one byte, then reverse the four bytes and reorder them — O(1) with a tiny constant.
    • 

  • A divide-and-conquer variant swaps halves, then quarters, then bytes, then nibbles, then pairs with five masked shifts; mention it as the lookup-free fast path.
    • 





Hint 1You're mirroring the 32 bits end to end.


Hint 2Shift bits out of the input and into the result in reverse positions.


Hint 3For 32 iterations, shift the result left, OR in the input's least significant bit, then shift the input right.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 127 of 150


127
Number of 1 Bits
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleAppleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given an unsigned integer, return how many bits are set to 1 in its binary representation (this count is called the Hamming weight or popcount).
Example. n = 11 = 1011. The set bits are at positions 0, 1, and 3 → answer 3.
Brute Force. Inspect each of the 32 bit positions: test the lowest bit with n & 1, add it to the count, and shift right. Simple and correct, but it always runs 32 iterations regardless of how few bits are set.


class Solution {
public:
    int hammingWeight(uint32_t n) {
        int bitCount = 0;
        while (n) {
            bitCount += n & 1;
            n >>= 1;
        }
        return bitCount;
    }
};




Time: O(32) = O(1), Space: O(1) — bounded by the integer width.
Optimized — Brian Kernighan's trick. The expression n & (n - 1) clears exactly the lowest set bit of n. Subtracting one turns that lowest 1 into 0 and turns every 0 below it into 1; AND-ing with the original then wipes that bit and all the borrowed lower ones. So each iteration removes one set bit, and the loop runs only as many times as there are ones — much faster on sparse numbers.


class Solution {
public:
    int hammingWeight(uint32_t n) {
        int bitCount = 0;
        while (n) {
            n &= n - 1;
            ++bitCount;
        }
        return bitCount;
    }
};




Time: O(k) where k is the number of set bits (at most 32, so O(1) worst case), Space: O(1) — one counter.
Interview Notes.


    

  • Walk the interviewer through why n & (n - 1) clears the lowest set bit with a concrete example like 1100 - 1 = 1011, AND = 1000; this is a classic "explain the trick" moment.
    • 

  • In production you'd use the hardware instruction via __builtin_popcount(n) (GCC/Clang) or std::popcount (C++20), but interviewers usually want the manual version.
    • 

  • Relatedly, n & (n - 1) == 0 (for n > 0) tests whether n is a power of two — exactly one set bit.
    • 





Hint 1Counting set bits is the goal.


Hint 2A trick clears the lowest set bit each step.


Hint 3Repeatedly do n &= (n - 1), which removes the lowest set bit, counting iterations until n is zero.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 128 of 150


128
Single Number
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAppleAdobe




Problem. Every element in a non-empty array appears exactly twice except for one element that appears once. Return that single element. The required follow-up is to do it in linear time with O(1) extra space.
Example. nums = [4, 1, 2, 1, 2] → 4. XOR running total: 0^4 = 4, 4^1 = 5, 5^2 = 7, 7^1 = 6, 6^2 = 4. The pairs cancel and 4 survives.
Brute Force. Count occurrences with a hash map, then return the key whose count is one. Correct and obvious, but it uses O(n) extra space — exactly what the follow-up forbids.


class Solution {
public:
    int singleNumber(vector<int>& nums) {
        unordered_map<int, int> counts;
        for (int value : nums) ++counts[value];
        for (auto& entry : counts) {
            if (entry.second == 1) return entry.first;
        }
        return 0;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n) — the map can hold up to n/2 + 1 distinct keys.
Optimized — XOR. XOR is associative, commutative, self-cancelling (x ^ x == 0), and has 0 as identity (x ^ 0 == x). XOR-ing the entire array therefore annihilates every value that appears twice, no matter the order, and leaves only the element that appears once. One pass, a single accumulator, zero extra structures.


class Solution {
public:
    int singleNumber(vector<int>& nums) {
        int result = 0;
        for (int value : nums) result ^= value;
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1) — one pass and one integer.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The key sentence to say out loud: "XOR cancels pairs and order doesn't matter, so everything paired disappears."
    • 

  • This works because each duplicate appears an even number of times (twice); XOR generalizes to "any element appearing an even number of times cancels."
    • 

  • It does not extend to elements appearing three times — that needs the bit-counting approach in the next problem.
    • 





Hint 1XOR has a self-cancelling property.


Hint 2XOR-ing all numbers leaves only the unique one.


Hint 3Fold the array with XOR; pairs cancel to 0 and the lone element remains.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 129 of 150


129
Single Number II
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Every element appears exactly three times except for one element that appears once. Return the single element, ideally in linear time and constant extra space. Values can be negative.
Example. nums = [2, 2, 3, 2] → 3. Across all numbers, bit 1 (value 2) is set three times (sum 3 → 3 mod 3 = 0) while bit 0 and bit 1 of the 3 contribute the leftover; reconstructing only the bits whose set-count isn't a multiple of 3 yields 3.
Brute Force — count bits per position. For each of the 32 bit positions, sum how many array elements have that bit set. If the total is not a multiple of three, the single number must own that bit, so set it in the answer. Tripled elements contribute 0 or 3 to each position (both ≡ 0 mod 3) and drop out automatically.


class Solution {
public:
    int singleNumber(vector<int>& nums) {
        int result = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            int bitSum = 0;
            for (int value : nums) {
                bitSum += (value >> i) & 1;
            }
            if (bitSum % 3 != 0) {
                result |= (1 << i);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(32 n) = O(n), Space: O(1) — constant counters, but 32 passes over the data.
Optimized — two-bit state machine. Track two masks, ones and twos, that together record each bit's running count modulo 3 (a bit can be "seen 0, 1, or 2 times so far"). When a bit arrives, fold it into ones only if it isn't already accounted for in twos, then symmetrically update twos from the new ones; the & ~ terms reset a bit's count to 0 once it reaches three. After processing all numbers, ones holds the bits that appeared a number of times ≡ 1 mod 3 — exactly the single element.


class Solution {
public:
    int singleNumber(vector<int>& nums) {
        int ones = 0;
        int twos = 0;
        for (int value : nums) {
            ones = (ones ^ value) & ~twos;
            twos = (twos ^ value) & ~ones;
        }
        return ones;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1) — single pass, two integer registers.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The generalized follow-up — "every element appears k times except one appearing once" — is solved by the brute-force bit-count method with % k instead of % 3; that is the safe, explainable answer when the state-machine version slips your mind.
    • 

  • The ones/twos automaton is the slick answer but easy to get wrong under pressure; be ready to derive or at least justify it, and have the bit-count method as your reliable fallback.
    • 

  • Negative numbers are fine because the bitwise operations act on the two's-complement representation; the sign bit (bit 31) is just another counted position.
    • 





Hint 1Every bit position should sum to a multiple of 3 except where the unique number contributes.


Hint 2Count bits per position mod 3, or use two-state bitmask logic.


Hint 3For each of the 32 bit positions count occurrences and take mod 3 to recover that bit of the answer; the classic O(1)-space version uses 'ones' and 'twos' accumulators.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 130 of 150


130
Bitwise AND of Numbers Range
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloomberg




Problem. Given two integers left and right with left <= right, return the bitwise AND of every integer in the inclusive range [left, right]. The range can be enormous, so iterating each value can time out.
Example. left = 5 (101), right = 7 (111). 5 & 6 & 7 = 101 & 110 & 111 = 100 = 4. Note the answer equals the common binary prefix of 5 and 7 with the differing lower bits zeroed.
Brute Force. Literally AND every integer from left to right. Correct, but when right - left is in the billions this loops far too many times and times out.


class Solution {
public:
    int rangeBitwiseAnd(int left, int right) {
        int result = left;
        for (int value = left + 1; value <= right; ++value) {
            result &= value;
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(right - left), Space: O(1) — proportional to the range size, which can be ~2^31.
Optimized — common binary prefix. A bit in the result is 1 only if it is 1 in every number of the range. Any bit position that flips between a 0 and a 1 somewhere in [left, right] gets ANDed down to 0. The bits that never flip are exactly the shared high-order prefix of left and right; all lower bits become zero. To find that prefix, right-shift both numbers in lockstep until they're equal (stripping off the differing low bits), counting the shifts, then shift the common value back left by the same amount to restore the zeros.


class Solution {
public:
    int rangeBitwiseAnd(int left, int right) {
        int shiftCount = 0;
        while (left < right) {
            left >>= 1;
            right >>= 1;
            ++shiftCount;
        }
        return left << shiftCount;
    }
};




Time: O(log(max value)) = O(32) = O(1), Space: O(1) — at most 32 shifts.
Interview Notes.


    

  • The crisp insight to state: "The answer is the common left-most prefix of left and right; everything below the first differing bit is forced to zero because some value in between flips it."
    • 

  • An equivalent solution uses Brian Kernighan: repeatedly do right &= right - 1 until right <= left, clearing low set bits of right until it can no longer exceed left.
    • 

  • Watch the edge case left == right (loop runs zero times, returns left) and large ranges spanning a power-of-two boundary, where the answer is 0 because the high bit itself flips.
    • 





Hint 1The result keeps only the common high-order prefix of left and right.


Hint 2Differences in lower bits get zeroed out across the range.


Hint 3Right-shift both bounds until they're equal (counting shifts), then left-shift that common prefix back by the same count.






⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 131 of 150


131
Palindrome Number
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleAdobeBloombergApple




Problem. Given an integer inputNumber, return true if it reads the same forwards and backwards. Negative numbers are never palindromes (the - sign only appears on the left). Constraints: -2^31 <= inputNumber <= 2^31 - 1.


Example. Input 121 → true (121 reversed is 121). Input -121 → false (reversed it would read 121-). Input 10 → false (reversed is 01).




Hint 1Negative numbers aren't palindromes; you can avoid converting to a string.


Hint 2Reverse only the second half of the number and compare.


Hint 3Pop digits to build a reversed half while the original shrinks; stop when the reversed half is >= the remaining, then compare for even/odd length.






Brute Force. Convert the number to a string and compare it against its reverse. Simple and correct, but uses extra memory and sidesteps the numeric insight the interviewer wants.


class Solution {
public:
    bool isPalindrome(int inputNumber) {
        if (inputNumber < 0) return false;
        string digits = to_string(inputNumber);
        string reversed(digits.rbegin(), digits.rend());
        return digits == reversed;
    }
};




Time: O(d) where d is the number of digits (d <= 10), Space: O(d) for the two strings. Each digit is touched a constant number of times.


Optimized. Reverse only the second half of the number and compare it to the first half, so we never build a full reversed value that could overflow. Pop digits off the right end with % 10 and / 10, building reversedHalf. Stop when reversedHalf becomes greater than or equal to the shrinking remaining — at that point we've consumed half the digits. For an even digit count the two halves must match exactly; for an odd count, the middle digit sits alone in reversedHalf, so we drop it with reversedHalf / 10. Any number ending in 0 (except 0 itself) cannot be a palindrome because no number starts with 0.


class Solution {
public:
    bool isPalindrome(int inputNumber) {
        if (inputNumber < 0 || (inputNumber % 10 == 0 && inputNumber != 0))
            return false;
        int remaining = inputNumber;
        int reversedHalf = 0;
        while (remaining > reversedHalf) {
            reversedHalf = reversedHalf * 10 + remaining % 10;
            remaining /= 10;
        }
        return remaining == reversedHalf || remaining == reversedHalf / 10;
    }
};




Time: O(d), Space: O(1). We process half the digits and use only two scalar variables, no allocation.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Reversing only half avoids overflow entirely — reversing the whole number could exceed INT_MAX, which is precisely why this approach beats a naive full reversal.
    • 

  • State the two edge cases out loud: negatives, and trailing-zero numbers like 10 or 1200.
    • 

  • Common FAANG follow-up: "What if you can't convert to a string?" — the half-reversal answer above is exactly what they're fishing for.
    • 

  • A clean fallback if asked for the simplest possible version: reverse the whole number into a long long and compare; mention the overflow trade-off.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 132 of 150


132
Plus One
Easy


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. You're given a large non-negative integer represented as an array digits, most-significant digit first, with no leading zeros. Add one to it and return the resulting digit array. The number can be larger than any built-in integer type, which is the whole point.


Example. Input [1,2,3] → [1,2,4] (123 + 1 = 124). Input [9,9] → [1,0,0] (99 + 1 = 100, carry ripples and grows the array).




Hint 1Adding one only propagates a carry through trailing 9s.


Hint 2Work from the least significant digit.


Hint 3From the end, if a digit is 9 set it to 0 and continue, otherwise increment it and stop; if all were 9 prepend a leading 1.






Brute Force. Convert the array to an actual integer, add one, convert back. This fails the moment the number exceeds long long, so it isn't a real solution for the stated constraints — but it's worth naming to show you understand why it's wrong.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> plusOne(vector<int>& digits) {
        long long value = 0;
        for (int digit : digits) value = value * 10 + digit;
        value += 1;
        vector<int> result;
        for (string character : {to_string(value)})
            for (char singleChar : character)
                result.push_back(singleChar - '0');
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(n). It's linear, but it silently breaks for inputs longer than ~18 digits — unacceptable given the constraints.


Optimized. Simulate grade-school addition right to left. Walk from the last digit: if a digit is less than 9, increment it and return immediately — there's no carry to propagate. If it's 9, set it to 0 and continue left. If you fall off the front, every digit was 9, so the result is a 1 followed by all zeros (e.g. 999 -> 1000); prepend the leading 1.


class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> plusOne(vector<int>& digits) {
        for (int index = digits.size() - 1; index >= 0; --index) {
            if (digits[index] < 9) {
                digits[index] += 1;
                return digits;
            }
            digits[index] = 0;
        }
        digits.insert(digits.begin(), 1);
        return digits;
    }
};




Time: O(n), Space: O(1) extra in the common case (we mutate in place); the all-nines case adds one element. We touch each digit at most once.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The key insight is early return: the moment a digit absorbs the carry without becoming 10, you're done — no need to scan the rest.
    • 

  • The only array-growth case is all nines; call it out explicitly.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "Now add two such arrays" or "subtract one" — same right-to-left carry/borrow machinery generalizes.
    • 

  • Mention that this is really arbitrary-precision arithmetic, which is why we can't just use int.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 133 of 150


133
Factorial Trailing Zeroes
Medium


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given an integer targetNumber, return the number of trailing zeros in targetNumber! (factorial). For example 5! = 120 has one trailing zero. Constraints: 0 <= targetNumber <= 10^4.


Example. Input 5 → 1 (120 ends in one zero). Input 10 → 2 (10! = 3628800, two trailing zeros). Input 25 → 6.




Hint 1Trailing zeros come from factors of 10, i.e., pairs of 2 and 5, and 5s are scarcer.


Hint 2Count how many times 5 divides into the factorial's terms.


Hint 3Sum n/5 + n/25 + n/125 + ... until the term is zero; that total is the count of trailing zeros.






Brute Force. Compute the factorial, then strip and count trailing zeros by repeated % 10. This is hopeless: 10000! has tens of thousands of digits and overflows every numeric type almost instantly, so you'd need a big-integer library just to get a wrong-for-the-interview answer.


class Solution {
public:
    int trailingZeroes(int targetNumber) {
        unsigned long long factorial = 1;
        for (int multiplier = 2; multiplier <= targetNumber; ++multiplier)
            factorial *= multiplier;
        int zeroCount = 0;
        while (factorial > 0 && factorial % 10 == 0) {
            ++zeroCount;
            factorial /= 10;
        }
        return zeroCount;
    }
};




Time: O(n) multiplications, Space: O(1) — but it overflows for any targetNumber beyond ~20, so it is fundamentally broken here.


Optimized. A trailing zero comes from a factor of 10 = 2 * 5. In a factorial there are always more factors of 2 than of 5, so the number of trailing zeros equals the number of factors of 5 in the product. Count multiples of 5 (each contributes one), plus multiples of 25 (contribute an extra), plus 125, and so on. That's floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + .... Implement it by repeatedly dividing the running value by 5 and summing.


class Solution {
public:
    int trailingZeroes(int targetNumber) {
        int zeroCount = 0;
        for (long long powerOfFive = 5; targetNumber / powerOfFive >= 1; powerOfFive *= 5)
            zeroCount += targetNumber / powerOfFive;
        return zeroCount;
    }
};




Time: O(log_5 n), Space: O(1). The loop runs once per power of five not exceeding n, which is logarithmic.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The whole problem hinges on one observation: zeros = count of 5s, because 2s are always in surplus. Lead with that and the code writes itself.
    • 

  • Use long long for powerOfFive so 5^k doesn't overflow int when it grows past the input — a subtle overflow trap interviewers watch for.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "How many trailing zeros in base 12?" → factor 12 = 2^2 * 3, count min over the limiting prime power; this tests whether you truly understood the factor-counting idea.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 134 of 150


134
Sqrt(x)
Easy


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleBloombergApple




Problem. Given a non-negative integer inputValue, return the integer square root — that is, floor(sqrt(inputValue)), the result truncated toward zero. You may not use any built-in sqrt function. Constraints: 0 <= inputValue <= 2^31 - 1.


Example. Input 8 → 2 (sqrt(8) ≈ 2.828, floored to 2). Input 4 → 2. Input 16 → 4.




Hint 1You need the integer square root without floats.


Hint 2Binary search the answer range.


Hint 3Binary search x in [0, n]; find the largest mid whose square is <= n (guard against overflow by comparing mid <= n/mid).






Brute Force. Count upward, squaring each candidate until the square exceeds the target; the last candidate that didn't exceed is the answer. Correct but slow for large inputs near 2^31.


class Solution {
public:
    int mySqrt(int inputValue) {
        long long candidate = 0;
        while (candidate * candidate <= inputValue)
            ++candidate;
        return candidate - 1;
    }
};




Time: O(sqrt(n)), Space: O(1). The loop runs up to the square root of the input — about 46,000 iterations for the maximum value.


Optimized. Binary search the answer over the range [0, inputValue]. The integer square root is the largest value whose square does not exceed the target, and "square <= target" is monotonic, which is exactly what binary search needs. At each step test the midpoint's square (in long long to avoid overflow); if it fits, the answer is at least the midpoint, so move the lower bound up; otherwise move the upper bound down. Return the highest value that fit.


class Solution {
public:
    int mySqrt(int inputValue) {
        long long low = 0;
        long long high = inputValue;
        long long bestRoot = 0;
        while (low <= high) {
            long long mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (mid * mid <= inputValue) {
                bestRoot = mid;
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        return bestRoot;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). The search space halves every iteration.


Interview Notes.


    

  • mid * mid overflows a 32-bit int for large inputs — compute it in long long, or equivalently compare mid <= inputValue / mid. State this overflow guard explicitly; it's the single most common mistake on this problem.
    • 

  • Use low + (high - low) / 2 rather than (low + high) / 2 to avoid summation overflow — a reflex worth showing.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "Now give me the answer to k decimal places" → switch to Newton's method (x = (x + n/x) / 2) which converges quadratically, or binary search on a real range with an epsilon. Mentioning Newton's method signals depth.
    •
