LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 135 of 150


135
Pow(x, n)
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftLinkedInBloombergApple




Problem. Implement pow(baseValue, exponent), computing baseValue raised to the integer power exponent. The exponent may be negative (return the reciprocal). Constraints: -100.0 < baseValue < 100.0, -2^31 <= exponent <= 2^31 - 1.


Example. Input baseValue = 2.0, exponent = 10 → 1024.0. Input baseValue = 2.0, exponent = -2 → 0.25 (1 / 2^2). Input baseValue = 2.1, exponent = 3 → 9.261.




Hint 1Repeated multiplication is too slow for large exponents.


Hint 2Exponentiation by squaring halves the work each step.


Hint 3Recurse/iterate squaring the base and halving the exponent, multiplying in the base on odd exponents; handle negative exponents by inverting the base.






Brute Force. Multiply baseValue by itself |exponent| times in a loop, then take the reciprocal if the exponent was negative. With an exponent up to 2^31, that's over two billion multiplications — far too slow.


class Solution {
public:
    double myPow(double baseValue, int exponent) {
        long long absoluteExponent = abs((long long)exponent);
        double result = 1.0;
        for (long long step = 0; step < absoluteExponent; ++step)
            result *= baseValue;
        return exponent < 0 ? 1.0 / result : result;
    }
};




Time: O(n) where n is the exponent magnitude, Space: O(1). Linear in the exponent — billions of operations in the worst case.


Optimized. Use exponentiation by squaring (fast power). Any power can be built from squarings: x^n = (x^2)^(n/2) when n is even, and x^n = x * (x^2)^((n-1)/2) when n is odd. So repeatedly square the base and halve the exponent, multiplying the running answer by the current base whenever the exponent's lowest bit is set. This reduces ~two billion multiplications to about 31. Handle a negative exponent by inverting the base once up front; promote the exponent to long long first because -(-2^31) overflows a 32-bit int.


class Solution {
public:
    double myPow(double baseValue, int exponent) {
        long long power = exponent;
        if (power < 0) {
            baseValue = 1.0 / baseValue;
            power = -power;
        }
        double result = 1.0;
        double currentBase = baseValue;
        while (power > 0) {
            if (power & 1)
                result *= currentBase;
            currentBase *= currentBase;
            power >>= 1;
        }
        return result;
    }
};




Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Each iteration halves the exponent, so the loop runs about log2(n) times — at most 31.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The overflow trap is exponent = INT_MIN: negating it as an int overflows. Copy it into a long long before negating — interviewers plant exactly this test case.
    • 

  • Be ready to explain both the iterative bit-walking version above and the recursive (x*x)^(n/2) formulation; they're equivalent, but recursion costs O(log n) stack space.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "Apply this to matrix exponentiation" — the same squaring trick computes Fibonacci or graph-path counts in O(log n) matrix multiplies.
    • 

  • Don't worry about floating-point precision drift unless asked; if asked, note that the order of multiplications affects rounding.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Math›Problem 136 of 150


136
Max Points on a Line
Hard


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftLinkedInBloombergApple




Problem. Given an array points where each element is [x, y], return the maximum number of points that lie on the same straight line. Points may repeat. Constraints: 1 <= points.size() <= 300.


Example. Input [[1,1],[2,2],[3,3]] → 3 (all three sit on the line y = x). Input [[1,1],[3,2],[5,3],[4,1],[2,3],[1,4]] → 4.




Hint 1Collinear points through a fixed point share the same slope.


Hint 2Group points by slope relative to each anchor.


Hint 3For each point, tally slopes to every other point in a hashmap (use a normalized fraction or gcd-reduced pair to avoid float error), tracking the largest same-slope group plus the anchor.






Brute Force. Try every pair of points to define a line, then count how many of the remaining points are collinear with that pair (using the cross-product test to avoid division). Three nested loops.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxPoints(vector<vector<int>>& points) {
        int totalPoints = points.size();
        if (totalPoints <= 2) return totalPoints;
        int best = 2;
        for (int first = 0; first < totalPoints; ++first) {
            for (int second = first + 1; second < totalPoints; ++second) {
                int onLine = 2;
                for (int third = 0; third < totalPoints; ++third) {
                    if (third == first || third == second) continue;
                    long long crossProduct =
                        (long long)(points[second][0] - points[first][0]) * (points[third][1] - points[first][1]) -
                        (long long)(points[second][1] - points[first][1]) * (points[third][0] - points[first][0]);
                    if (crossProduct == 0) ++onLine;
                }
                best = max(best, onLine);
            }
        }
        return best;
    }
};




Time: O(n^3), Space: O(1). Three nested loops over up to 300 points — about 27 million operations, acceptable here but not scalable.


Optimized. Anchor each point in turn and group every other point by the slope of the line connecting them; points sharing a slope through the same anchor are collinear. For each anchor, the largest slope group plus the anchor itself is the best line through that anchor. To compare slopes exactly without floating-point error, represent each slope as a reduced fraction (deltaY, deltaX): divide both by their gcd and normalize the sign so directions like (1,2) and (-1,-2) hash identically. Vertical lines (deltaX == 0) get a canonical key. Take the max over all anchors.


class Solution {
public:
    int maxPoints(vector<vector<int>>& points) {
        int totalPoints = points.size();
        if (totalPoints <= 2) return totalPoints;
        int best = 1;
        for (int anchor = 0; anchor < totalPoints; ++anchor) {
            unordered_map<long long, int> slopeCount;
            for (int other = 0; other < totalPoints; ++other) {
                if (other == anchor) continue;
                int deltaX = points[other][0] - points[anchor][0];
                int deltaY = points[other][1] - points[anchor][1];
                int divisor = gcd(deltaX, deltaY);
                deltaX /= divisor;
                deltaY /= divisor;
                if (deltaX < 0 || (deltaX == 0 && deltaY < 0)) {
                    deltaX = -deltaX;
                    deltaY = -deltaY;
                }
                long long slopeKey = (long long)deltaX * 100003 + deltaY;
                best = max(best, ++slopeCount[slopeKey] + 1);
            }
        }
        return best;
    }
};




Time: O(n^2) on average, Space: O(n). For each of n anchors we hash n-1 slopes; the hash map lookups are amortized constant.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Never compare slopes as double — floating-point rounding makes truly collinear points look different. Reducing to a gcd-normalized integer fraction is the standard fix; say this before the interviewer asks.
    • 

  • Sign normalization matters: without it, (1,2) and (-1,-2) are the same line but hash to different keys, undercounting. Pick a canonical sign for the pair.
    • 

  • The brute-force cross-product test ((x2-x1)*(y3-y1) == (y2-y1)*(x3-x1), computed in long long) is a clean way to demonstrate the no-division idea even if you then optimize.
    • 

  • FAANG follow-up: "What about duplicate points?" — the slope-map version naturally counts duplicates as deltaX == deltaY == 0; handle them as a separate count added to every line through the anchor.
    • 

  • gcd is in <numeric> since C++17; if unavailable, write a two-line Euclidean helper.
    •

Patterns›1D Dynamic Programming›Problem 137 of 150


137
Climbing Stairs
Easy✓ auto-graded


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftAdobeBloombergApple




Problem. You climb a staircase of n steps. Each move is either 1 step or 2 steps. Count the distinct ways to reach the top. Constraints: 1 <= n <= 45.


Example. n = 3 → 3. Trace: the orderings are 1+1+1, 1+2, 2+1 — three ways.




Hint 1The ways to reach a step depend on the previous one or two steps.


Hint 2This is Fibonacci in disguise.


Hint 3Define dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]; iterate keeping just the last two values for O(1) space.






Brute Force. Recurse on "ways to reach step n". The last move came from step n-1 (a single step) or step n-2 (a double step), so ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2), with ways(0) = ways(1) = 1. Written directly this re-explores the same steps exponentially, exactly like naive Fibonacci.


class Solution {
public:
    int climbStairs(int n) {
        if (n <= 1) return 1;
        return climbStairs(n - 1) + climbStairs(n - 2);
    }
};




Time: O(2^n) — the recursion branches twice at almost every call. Space: O(n) for the recursion stack depth.


Optimized. State: dp[i] = number of ways to reach step i. Recurrence in words: ways to reach a step equal the ways to reach the step just below it plus the ways to reach two steps below it. Base cases dp[0] = dp[1] = 1. Because dp[i] only ever looks back two slots, we keep just two rolling variables instead of a full array, giving O(1) space.


class Solution {
public:
    int climbStairs(int n) {
        if (n <= 1) return 1;
        int previous = 1, current = 1;
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
            int next = previous + current;
            previous = current;
            current = next;
        }
        return current;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one pass. Space: O(1) — two variables regardless of n.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Derive the recurrence live by asking "what was my last move?" — that single question splits the problem into n-1 and n-2 and writes the recurrence for you.
    • 

  • State out loud that this is Fibonacci in disguise; interviewers love the recognition.
    • 

  • Mention the O(1) rolling-variable optimization unprompted — it shows you see that only the last two states matter.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›1D Dynamic Programming›Problem 138 of 150


138
House Robber
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergAdobe




Problem. Houses in a row each hold some money in array nums. You cannot rob two adjacent houses (an alarm triggers). Return the maximum money you can rob. Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100, 0 <= nums[i] <= 400.


Example. nums = [2,7,9,3,1] → 12. Trace: rob houses 0, 2, 4 → 2 + 9 + 1 = 12, which beats 7 + 3 = 10.




Hint 1You can't rob two adjacent houses, so each house's best depends on earlier choices.


Hint 2Track the best total whether or not you take the current house.


Hint 3DP: best[i] = max(best[i-1], best[i-2] + nums[i]); keep two rolling variables for the previous two bests.






Brute Force. At each house decide rob or skip. If you rob house i you add nums[i] and jump to i+2; if you skip you move to i+1. Take the max of the two branches. This explores every valid subset of non-adjacent houses, which is exponential.


class Solution {
public:
    int rob(vector<int>& nums) {
        return robFrom(nums, 0);
    }
private:
    int robFrom(vector<int>& nums, int index) {
        if (index >= (int)nums.size()) return 0;
        int robThis = nums[index] + robFrom(nums, index + 2);
        int skipThis = robFrom(nums, index + 1);
        return max(robThis, skipThis);
    }
};




Time: O(2^n) — two choices per house. Space: O(n) recursion depth.


Optimized. State: dp[i] = the most money robbable considering houses 0..i. Recurrence in words: at house i you either skip it (keep dp[i-1]) or rob it (nums[i] plus the best up to i-2, since i-1 is now off-limits). So dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). Only the previous two answers matter, so collapse to two variables for O(1) space.


class Solution {
public:
    int rob(vector<int>& nums) {
        int previous = 0, current = 0;
        for (int money : nums) {
            int next = max(current, previous + money);
            previous = current;
            current = next;
        }
        return current;
    }
};




Time: O(n) — one sweep. Space: O(1) — two rolling values.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Same "rob or skip" decision structure as Climbing Stairs' "1-step or 2-step" — recognizing the shared shape lets you write the recurrence in seconds.
    • 

  • previous represents dp[i-2] and current represents dp[i-1]; narrate that mapping so the interviewer follows the space optimization.
    • 

  • Common follow-up: House Robber II (houses in a circle) — solve it by running this twice, once excluding the first house and once excluding the last, then taking the max.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›1D Dynamic Programming›Problem 139 of 150


139
Word Break
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonGoogleMetaMicrosoftBloombergUber




Problem. Given a string s and a dictionary wordDict, return whether s can be segmented into a sequence of one or more dictionary words (words may be reused). Constraints: 1 <= s.length <= 300, dictionary words up to length 20.


Example. s = "leetcode", wordDict = ["leet","code"] → true. Trace: split after index 4 — "leet" is in the dictionary and "code" is in the dictionary.




Hint 1You're asking whether prefixes can be segmented into dictionary words.


Hint 2DP over prefix lengths, checking word-ending cuts.


Hint 3Let dp[i] mean s[0:i] is segmentable; dp[i] is true if some j has dp[j] true and s[j:i] is in the dictionary.






Brute Force. Try every prefix of s; if a prefix is a dictionary word, recurse on the remaining suffix. Without caching, the same suffix gets re-checked through many different prefix paths, so the work is exponential in the string length.


class Solution {
public:
    bool wordBreak(string s, vector<string>& wordDict) {
        unordered_set<string> dictionary(wordDict.begin(), wordDict.end());
        return canBreak(s, 0, dictionary);
    }
private:
    bool canBreak(const string& s, int start, unordered_set<string>& dictionary) {
        if (start == (int)s.size()) return true;
        for (int end = start + 1; end <= (int)s.size(); ++end) {
            string prefix = s.substr(start, end - start);
            if (dictionary.count(prefix) && canBreak(s, end, dictionary)) return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
};




Time: O(2^n) in the worst case — each position can branch into many splits. Space: O(n) recursion depth.


Optimized. State: dp[i] = true if the first i characters s[0..i-1] can be fully segmented. Recurrence in words: dp[i] is true if there is some earlier cut j where the front part dp[j] is already segmentable and the chunk s[j..i-1] is itself a dictionary word. Base case dp[0] = true (the empty prefix is trivially segmentable). The answer is dp[s.size()].


class Solution {
public:
    bool wordBreak(string s, vector<string>& wordDict) {
        unordered_set<string> dictionary(wordDict.begin(), wordDict.end());
        int length = s.size();
        vector<bool> dp(length + 1, false);
        dp[0] = true;
        for (int i = 1; i <= length; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < i; ++j) {
                if (dp[j] && dictionary.count(s.substr(j, i - j))) {
                    dp[i] = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[length];
    }
};




Time: O(n^2) string positions times O(n) substring/hash work = O(n^3) worst case (often quoted as O(n^2) when word length is bounded). Space: O(n) for dp plus the dictionary set.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Define the state as "can the prefix of length i be broken" — phrasing it as a prefix is what turns the recursion into a clean 1D table.
    • 

  • The inner loop is "where was the last word's left edge?" — same last-move reasoning as the earlier problems, just over cut positions instead of steps.
    • 

  • If asked to return the actual segmentation, store the cut index that made dp[i] true and backtrack; mention bounding the inner loop by the max dictionary word length as a speedup.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›1D Dynamic Programming›Problem 140 of 150


140
Coin Change
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftMetaBloombergGoldman Sachs




Problem. Given coin denominations coins and a target amount, return the fewest coins that sum to amount, or -1 if it's impossible. Each coin may be used unlimited times. Constraints: 1 <= coins.length <= 12, 0 <= amount <= 10^4.


Example. coins = [1,2,5], amount = 11 → 3. Trace: 5 + 5 + 1 = 11 uses three coins, the minimum.




Hint 1This is an unbounded-coin minimization, screaming dynamic programming.


Hint 2Build up the fewest coins for every amount from 0 to target.


Hint 3dp[a] = 1 + min over coins c<=a of dp[a-c]; initialize dp[0]=0 and treat unreachable amounts as infinity.






Brute Force. Recurse on the remaining amount: for each coin, subtract it and solve for the smaller remainder, taking the minimum coin count over all choices. The same remaining amounts recur across different coin orderings, so the tree is exponential.


class Solution {
public:
    int coinChange(vector<int>& coins, int amount) {
        if (amount == 0) return 0;
        if (amount < 0) return -1;
        int best = INT_MAX;
        for (int coin : coins) {
            int subResult = coinChange(coins, amount - coin);
            if (subResult >= 0) best = min(best, subResult + 1);
        }
        return best == INT_MAX ? -1 : best;
    }
};




Time: O(coins^amount) — exponential branching on every remaining amount. Space: O(amount) recursion depth.


Optimized. State: dp[a] = the fewest coins needed to make amount a. Recurrence in words: to make a, the last coin used was some coin, so the cost is one plus the best way to make a - coin; take the minimum over all coins that fit. Initialize dp to a sentinel "infinity" (amount + 1 is safely larger than any real answer) and set dp[0] = 0. Build the table from 1 up to amount.


class Solution {
public:
    int coinChange(vector<int>& coins, int amount) {
        vector<int> dp(amount + 1, amount + 1);
        dp[0] = 0;
        for (int a = 1; a <= amount; ++a) {
            for (int coin : coins) {
                if (coin <= a) {
                    dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a - coin] + 1);
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount];
    }
};




Time: O(amount × coins) — each amount tries every coin. Space: O(amount) for the table.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Derive it with the last-move trick again: "the last coin I placed was coin, so I owe dp[amount - coin] + 1" — minimize over coins.
    • 

  • Warn the interviewer that greedy fails here (largest-coin-first breaks on cases like coins = [1,3,4], amount = 6), which is exactly why DP is required.
    • 

  • The sentinel amount + 1 avoids integer overflow from INT_MAX + 1; flag that as a deliberate safety choice.
    • 

  • Sibling problem Coin Change II counts the number of ways rather than minimum coins — same table shape, sum instead of min, and loop coins outermost to avoid double-counting orderings.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›1D Dynamic Programming›Problem 141 of 150


141
Longest Increasing Subsequence
Medium✓ auto-graded


AmazonMicrosoftGoogleMetaBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence keeps original order but need not be contiguous. Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 2500.


Example. nums = [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18] → 4. Trace: [2,3,7,18] (or [2,5,7,101]) has length 4.




Hint 1You want the longest chain of strictly increasing picks.


Hint 2An O(n^2) DP or an O(n log n) patience-sorting approach both work.


Hint 3DP: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over j<i with nums[j]<nums[i]; for O(n log n), keep a 'tails' array and binary-search the insertion point for each number.






Brute Force. For each element, recursively try extending every increasing subsequence: at each position, either skip the element or include it if it's larger than the previously included one. This enumerates subsequences, which is exponential.


class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLIS(vector<int>& nums) {
        return fromIndex(nums, 0, INT_MIN);
    }
private:
    int fromIndex(vector<int>& nums, int index, int previousValue) {
        if (index == (int)nums.size()) return 0;
        int skip = fromIndex(nums, index + 1, previousValue);
        int take = 0;
        if (nums[index] > previousValue) {
            take = 1 + fromIndex(nums, index + 1, nums[index]);
        }
        return max(skip, take);
    }
};




Time: O(2^n) — include/exclude branch per element. Space: O(n) recursion depth.


Optimized. State: dp[i] = the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends exactly at index i. Recurrence in words: for each earlier index j < i, if nums[j] < nums[i] then nums[i] can extend the subsequence ending at j, so dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Every element alone is a subsequence of length 1, so initialize all dp[i] = 1. The answer is the maximum entry, not dp[last], because the longest run can end anywhere.


class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLIS(vector<int>& nums) {
        int length = nums.size();
        vector<int> dp(length, 1);
        int best = 1;
        for (int i = 1; i < length; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < i; ++j) {
                if (nums[j] < nums[i]) {
                    dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1);
                }
            }
            best = max(best, dp[i]);
        }
        return best;
    }
};




Time: O(n^2) — each i scans all earlier j. Space: O(n) for the table.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Anchor the state as "LIS ending at i" — beginners often try "LIS up to i" and get a broken recurrence; ending-at makes the comparison nums[j] < nums[i] clean.
    • 

  • Remember the answer is max(dp), not the last cell — a classic off-by-one trap worth saying aloud.
    • 

  • O(n log n) follow-up: maintain a tails array where tails[k] is the smallest possible tail of an increasing subsequence of length k+1. For each number, binary-search (lower_bound) for the first tail >= num and overwrite it (or append if num is bigger than all tails); the length of tails is the answer. This replaces the inner O(n) scan with an O(log n) search. Note tails is not a real subsequence — only its length is meaningful.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Multidimensional Dynamic Programming›Problem 142 of 150


142
Triangle
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given a triangle as a vector of rows (row i has i + 1 numbers), find the minimum path sum from top to bottom, where from index j in a row you may step to index j or j + 1 in the next row. Constraints: up to 200 rows, values fit in int.


Example. triangle = [[2],[3,4],[6,5,7],[4,1,8,3]] → 11. Path 2 → 3 → 5 → 1 = 11, the cheapest descent.




Hint 1You want the minimum top-to-bottom path sum down the triangle.


Hint 2Bottom-up DP avoids edge handling.


Hint 3Process rows from the bottom: each cell becomes its value plus the smaller of the two children below it; the apex holds the answer.






Brute Force. Recurse from the apex: the best cost starting at (row, col) is the cell's value plus the cheaper of the two children below it. Without memoization this re-explores the same lower cells exponentially often.


class Solution {
public:
    int minimumTotal(vector<vector<int>>& triangle) {
        return descend(triangle, 0, 0);
    }
private:
    int descend(vector<vector<int>>& triangle, int row, int col) {
        if (row == (int)triangle.size()) return 0;
        int down = descend(triangle, row + 1, col);
        int downRight = descend(triangle, row + 1, col + 1);
        return triangle[row][col] + min(down, downRight);
    }
};




Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n). Each call branches into two and the recursion depth is the number of rows n, so the call tree is exponential while the stack holds at most one root-to-leaf path.


Optimized. Let dp[col] be the minimum cost to reach the bottom starting from the current row at index col. Work bottom-up: initialize dp with the last row, then for each row above set dp[col] = triangle[row][col] + min(dp[col], dp[col + 1]). Because we go bottom-up and only read the row directly below (already stored in dp), one 1D array suffices.


class Solution {
public:
    int minimumTotal(vector<vector<int>>& triangle) {
        int rows = triangle.size();
        vector<int> dp(triangle[rows - 1].begin(), triangle[rows - 1].end());
        for (int row = rows - 2; row >= 0; --row) {
            for (int col = 0; col <= row; ++col) {
                dp[col] = triangle[row][col] + min(dp[col], dp[col + 1]);
            }
        }
        return dp[0];
    }
};




Time: O(n^2), Space: O(n). There are about n^2 / 2 cells and each is processed once; the rolling array holds only one row of size n.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Going bottom-up avoids tricky boundary handling at the edges of each row that a top-down fill would need.
    • 

  • Mention that you can do it in-place by overwriting the input triangle, achieving O(1) extra space — interviewers like that you considered it.
    • 

  • This is the canonical "DAG with two children per node" shape; recognising it makes Minimum Path Sum and Unique Paths feel familiar.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Multidimensional Dynamic Programming›Problem 143 of 150


143
Minimum Path Sum
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. Given an m × n grid of non-negative numbers, find the minimum sum path from the top-left to the bottom-right, moving only right or down. Constraints: grid up to 200 × 200.


Example. grid = [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]] → 7. Path 1 → 3 → 1 → 1 → 1.




Hint 1You can only move right or down, accumulating cost.


Hint 2DP where each cell's best comes from above or left.


Hint 3dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]); fill the first row/column as prefix sums; can be done in place.






Brute Force. Recurse: cost to reach (row, col) from the start equals the cell value plus the cheaper of coming from above or from the left. Naively this revisits cells exponentially.


class Solution {
public:
    int minPathSum(vector<vector<int>>& grid) {
        return best(grid, grid.size() - 1, grid[0].size() - 1);
    }
private:
    int best(vector<vector<int>>& grid, int row, int col) {
        if (row == 0 && col == 0) return grid[0][0];
        if (row < 0 || col < 0) return INT_MAX;
        int fromTop = best(grid, row - 1, col);
        int fromLeft = best(grid, row, col - 1);
        return grid[row][col] + min(fromTop, fromLeft);
    }
};




Time: O(2^(m+n)), Space: O(m+n). Two branches per call and a path length of m + n give an exponential tree; the stack depth is the path length.


Optimized. Let dp[col] be the minimum cost to reach the current row at column col. Process rows top to bottom; within a row, dp[col] before update still holds the cell above (fromTop) and dp[col - 1] after its own update holds the cell to the left (fromLeft). The first cell of each row only inherits from above; the first column overall only inherits from the left.


class Solution {
public:
    int minPathSum(vector<vector<int>>& grid) {
        int cols = grid[0].size();
        vector<int> dp(cols);
        dp[0] = grid[0][0];
        for (int col = 1; col < cols; ++col) dp[col] = dp[col - 1] + grid[0][col];
        for (int row = 1; row < (int)grid.size(); ++row) {
            dp[0] += grid[row][0];
            for (int col = 1; col < cols; ++col) {
                dp[col] = grid[row][col] + min(dp[col], dp[col - 1]);
            }
        }
        return dp[cols - 1];
    }
};




Time: O(m·n), Space: O(n). Every cell is touched once; the rolling row stores one row of width n.


Interview Notes.


    

  • The key insight for the rolling array: when you read dp[col] it is "from above" (not yet overwritten) and dp[col - 1] is "from left" (already overwritten this row). State this order explicitly.
    • 

  • Follow-up: "what if you may also move diagonally / up / left?" — that breaks the clean DAG ordering and pushes you toward Dijkstra on the grid.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
LeetCode ↗


📝 Notes











How well did you know it?








Patterns›Multidimensional Dynamic Programming›Problem 144 of 150


144
Unique Paths II
Medium


AmazonGoogleMicrosoftBloombergAdobe




Problem. A robot starts top-left and wants the bottom-right of an m × n grid, moving only right or down. Some cells contain obstacles (1); they cannot be entered. Count the number of distinct obstacle-free paths. Constraints: grid up to 100 × 100, answer fits in int per the problem.


Example. obstacleGrid = [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]] → 2. The single central obstacle removes the two paths that would pass through it, leaving two.




Hint 1Obstacles block certain cells, zeroing their path counts.


Hint 2DP summing paths from top and left, with obstacles contributing zero.


Hint 3dp[i][j] = 0 if blocked, else dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]; initialize the start and propagate, using a 1D rolling row for space.






Brute Force. Recurse: paths to (row, col) = paths from above + paths from left, returning 0 at obstacles and 1 at the start. Exponential without caching.


class Solution {
public:
    int uniquePathsWithObstacles(vector<vector<int>>& obstacleGrid) {
        return count(obstacleGrid, 0, 0);
    }
private:
    int count(vector<vector<int>>& grid, int row, int col) {
        int rows = grid.size(), cols = grid[0].size();
        if (row >= rows || col >= cols || grid[row][col] == 1) return 0;
        if (row == rows - 1 && col == cols - 1) return 1;
        return count(grid, row + 1, col) + count(grid, row, col + 1);
    }
};




Time: O(2^(m+n)), Space: O(m+n). Same branching shape as Minimum Path Sum.


Optimized. Let dp[col] be the number of ways to reach the current row at column col. An obstacle forces dp[col] = 0; otherwise dp[col] += dp[col - 1] (paths from above already in dp[col], paths from left in dp[col - 1]). Seed dp[0] = 1 only if the start is clear.


class Solution {
public:
    int uniquePathsWithObstacles(vector<vector<int>>& obstacleGrid) {
        int cols = obstacleGrid[0].size();
        vector<long long> dp(cols, 0);
        dp[0] = (obstacleGrid[0][0] == 0) ? 1 : 0;
        for (int row = 0; row < (int)obstacleGrid.size(); ++row) {
            for (int col = 0; col < cols; ++col) {
                if (obstacleGrid[row][col] == 1) {
                    dp[col] = 0;
                } else if (col > 0) {
                    dp[col] += dp[col - 1];
                }
            }
        }
        return dp[cols - 1];
    }
};




Time: O(m·n), Space: O(n). One pass over all cells with a single rolling row.


Interview Notes.


    

  • Using long long for the counter guards against overflow that the obstacle-free variant is famous for on larger grids — cheap insurance worth mentioning.
    • 

  • Note the subtlety: when row == 0 and col == 0 we must not run the dp[col] += dp[col - 1] branch, which is why the seed is done once before the loop and the loop guards col > 0.
    • 

  • Without obstacles the closed form is the binomial C(m+n-2, m-1); obstacles destroy that shortcut, which is exactly why DP is required.
    • 





⌨ Code & run · C++ (g++ 13)
