    • 

  • Circuit breaker — wraps a call to a failing dependency. After a failure threshold it opens (fails fast without calling, giving the dependency room to recover), periodically goes half-open to test, and closes when healthy. Prevents a slow dependency from exhausting all your threads and cascading the failure upstream.
    • 

  • Rate limiting — caps requests per client/key to protect the system from overload and abuse. Algorithms: token bucket (allows bursts up to bucket size, refills at a steady rate — the common default), leaky bucket (smooths to a constant rate), fixed/sliding window counters. Place it at the gateway. Pair with load shedding (drop low-priority work under stress).
    • 

  • Bulkheads — isolate resource pools so one overloaded feature can't starve the rest.
    • 

  • Timeouts — every remote call needs one; an unbounded wait is how one slow dependency hangs the whole fleet.
    • 

  • Graceful degradation — serve a cached/partial response rather than an error when a non-critical dependency is down.
    • 



Observability — the three pillars


You cannot operate what you cannot see. Observability is the ability to ask why the system is misbehaving from its outputs.






Pillar
Answers
Example






Metrics
"What/how much?" — numeric, aggregatable, cheap
QPS, p99 latency, error rate, CPU (the RED: Rate/Errors/Duration, and USE: Utilization/Saturation/Errors)




Logs
"What happened in this event?" — discrete records
Structured JSON request logs, stack traces




Traces
"Where did the time go across services?"
Distributed trace following one request across microservices via a trace id






Metrics drive dashboards and alerting; logs give detail for debugging; distributed tracing localizes latency/errors in a microservice call chain. Alert on symptoms (user-facing SLO breaches) rather than every cause to avoid alert fatigue.


SLA, SLO, SLI


    

  • SLI (Indicator) — the measured number (e.g. % of requests under 200ms).
    • 

  • SLO (Objective) — your internal target (e.g. 99.9% availability).
    • 

  • SLA (Agreement) — the external contract with penalties if breached; always looser than the SLO.
    • 



The gap between perfection and the SLO is the error budget — the allowed amount of failure. It turns reliability into a quantified trade-off: if you're within budget you ship features faster; if you've burned it, you freeze and harden. Quote nines concretely: 99.9% ≈ 8.7h downtime/year, 99.99% ≈ 52min/year. In an interview, define the SLO first, then justify redundancy and patterns as the means to hit it.


← System Design · HLD · Social & Media
Twitter / X
Problem & requirements


Functional
- Post a tweet (text up to 280 chars, optional media).
- Follow / unfollow users.
- Home timeline: reverse-chronological (or ranked) merge of tweets from followees.
- User timeline: a single user's own tweets.
- Like, retweet, reply.
- Search and trending (often scoped out for HLD).


Non-functional
- Read-heavy: timeline reads dominate writes by ~100:1.
- Low latency: home timeline p99 < 200 ms.
- High availability over strong consistency (eventual consistency for timelines is fine).
- Durable tweets (never lose a posted tweet).


Scale (assumptions to state aloud)
- 500M DAU, ~200M produce tweets.
- 500M tweets/day.
- Avg fanout (followers) ~200; celebrities up to 100M.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Tweet writes
500M / 86400
~6K writes/s (peak ~15K)




Timeline reads
500M DAU × 20 refreshes / 86400
~115K reads/s (peak ~500K)




Tweet storage/day
500M × 300 B
~150 GB/day (~55 TB/yr)




Media
10% × 200 KB × 500M
~10 TB/day → object store




Fanout writes
6K × 200 avg
~1.2M timeline inserts/s






The fanout-write number is the headline: it dwarfs tweet writes and drives the architecture.


API design


    

  • POST /v1/tweets {text, media_ids[]} → {tweet_id}
    • 

  • GET /v1/timeline/home?cursor=&limit=50 → {tweets[], next_cursor}
    • 

  • GET /v1/users/{id}/tweets?cursor=
    • 

  • POST /v1/users/{id}/follow
    • 

  • POST /v1/tweets/{id}/like
    • 



Use cursor-based pagination (opaque cursor = last tweet_id/snowflake), never offset — offsets break under inserts. Auth via OAuth2 bearer token at the gateway.


Data model


    

  • Tweets: NoSQL wide-column (Cassandra) or sharded SQL keyed by tweet_id (Snowflake ID = time-sortable). tweet_id, user_id, text, media_refs, created_at. Sharded by tweet_id.
    • 

  • Social graph: follows(follower_id, followee_id). Store both directions for fast "who do I follow" and "who follows me". Graph DB or sharded SQL/Redis sets.
    • 

  • Timeline cache: Redis list per user: timeline:{user_id} → list of tweet_ids (capped at ~800).
    • 

  • Media: blobs in S3, served via CDN; DB stores only URLs.
    • 



Why mixed: tweets are append-only and huge → NoSQL for write throughput and horizontal scale. The graph needs set operations → Redis/graph store. Timelines are ephemeral derived data → in-memory cache.


High-level architecture


Request flow (write): Client → API Gateway/LB → Tweet Service → writes tweet to Tweet DB + emits event to Kafka → Fanout Service consumes event, looks up followers, and pushes tweet_id into each follower's Redis timeline list.


Request flow (read): Client → Gateway → Timeline Service → reads timeline:{user_id} from Redis (already-materialized list of ids) → hydrates ids into full tweets from Tweet cache/DB → returns. A diagram shows: clients on the left; a gateway; Tweet, Timeline, Fanout, Graph, User services in the middle; Kafka between Tweet and Fanout; Redis timeline cache and Cassandra/S3 on the right.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Fanout-on-write vs fanout-on-read.
- Fanout-on-write (push): at post time, write tweet_id into every follower's timeline. Reads are O(1) cache reads — great for the read-heavy workload. Cost: write amplification (1.2M inserts/s) and the celebrity problem.
- Fanout-on-read (pull): at read time, query each followee's recent tweets and merge. Cheap writes, expensive reads, bad p99.
- Hybrid (production answer): push for normal users; for celebrities (>~10K–100K followers) do not fan out — store their tweets separately and merge them in at read time. So a home timeline = merge(precomputed_push_list, pull(celebrities_I_follow)). This caps write amplification while keeping reads fast.


2) Hot users / celebrities. A single 100M-follower tweet would create 100M writes. Skip fanout, cache the celebrity's recent tweets aggressively in a dedicated hot cache, and inject at read. Use read-time merge only for the (small) set of celebrities each user follows.


3) Sharding & ID generation. Snowflake IDs (timestamp + machine + sequence) give globally unique, roughly time-sortable ids without a central counter — enabling cursor pagination and shard routing by id. Tweet DB sharded by tweet_id; timeline cache sharded by user_id (consistent hashing).


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Thundering herd on a viral tweet → cache the hydrated tweet object, not just the id; use request coalescing.
    • 

  • Redis memory → cap timelines (~800 ids), evict inactive users, rebuild lazily on next read.
    • 

  • Ranking → swap reverse-chron for an ML-ranked feed; add a feature/ranking service before return.
    • 

  • Consistency → a new follow won't backfill old tweets; backfill job or pull-merge handles recency. Deletes are tombstoned and filtered at read.
    • 

  • Follow-ups they'll ask: how to handle a user who follows 50K accounts; cold-start empty timeline; trending/search (separate inverted-index pipeline).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Social & Media
Chat System (WhatsApp / Messenger)
Problem & requirements


Functional
- 1:1 messaging and group messaging.
- Online/last-seen presence.
- Delivery receipts (sent → delivered → read) and typing indicators.
- Offline delivery: messages queued and delivered when the recipient reconnects.
- Message ordering per conversation; optional media attachments.


Non-functional
- Low latency: message delivery p99 < 500 ms when both online.
- Durable: a sent message is never lost.
- Highly available; horizontally scalable to billions of connections.
- Per-conversation ordering guaranteed; end-to-end encryption optional (mention Signal protocol).


Scale
- 2B users, 500M concurrent connections.
- 100B messages/day.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Message writes
100B / 86400
~1.2M msgs/s (peak ~3M)




Concurrent conns
given
500M persistent WebSockets




Conns per gateway box
~500K–1M sockets/box
~500–1000 gateway servers




Storage/day
100B × 300 B
~30 TB/day




Presence updates
500M × heartbeat/30s
~16M ops/s (keep in memory)






API design


Mostly a persistent connection protocol (WebSocket / MQTT), not REST.
- CONNECT (auth token) → establishes session, registers in session registry.
- SEND {client_msg_id, conv_id, body} → server assigns server_msg_id, persists, acks.
- ACK {server_msg_id, status: delivered|read} → receipts.
- Server→client PUSH {msg}, PRESENCE {user, status}, TYPING.
- REST side: GET /conversations, GET /conversations/{id}/messages?before=, media upload via pre-signed URL.


client_msg_id enables idempotent retries and dedup.


Data model


    

  • Messages: NoSQL wide-column (Cassandra/HBase). Partition key = conversation_id, clustering key = message_id (time-sortable). Gives ordered, append-only, range-scannable history with massive write throughput.
    • 

  • Conversations / membership: conversation_id, members[], type, last_message_id.
    • 

  • Per-user inbox / offline queue: messages pending for offline users (Cassandra or a durable queue keyed by user_id).
    • 

  • Presence: Redis (presence:{user_id} → status + last_seen, TTL on heartbeat). Volatile → memory.
    • 

  • Session registry: Redis mapping user_id → gateway_server_id so the system knows which box holds the live socket.
    • 



NoSQL chosen for write volume (1.2M/s) and natural conversation-partitioning; SQL would not scale writes here.


High-level architecture


Flow (1:1, both online): Sender's WebSocket → its Gateway server → Message Service persists to Cassandra and assigns server_msg_id → looks up recipient in Session Registry (Redis) → routes via internal pub/sub (Kafka/RPC) to the Gateway holding the recipient's socket → pushes to recipient → recipient sends delivered ack back along the reverse path → sender shows double-tick.


Flow (recipient offline): after persist, message goes to recipient's offline queue; on reconnect, gateway drains the queue and pushes, then marks delivered.


Diagram in words: clients hold WebSockets to a fleet of stateless-ish gateway servers behind an L4 LB; gateways talk to Message Service, Presence Service, and a Session Registry; a message bus (Kafka) connects gateways for cross-server routing; Cassandra + offline-queue + S3 (media) on the storage tier.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Routing across millions of connections. A socket lives on exactly one gateway box. The Session Registry (Redis) maps user→box. To deliver, the sender's box looks up the recipient's box and forwards over an internal message bus. On disconnect, registry entry is removed (TTL'd) and traffic falls back to the offline queue. Consistent hashing balances sockets across boxes; on box failure clients reconnect and re-register.


2) Delivery & read receipts. Three states tracked per message: sent (persisted server-side), delivered (recipient device acked), read (recipient opened thread). Receipts are themselves small messages flowing back. For groups, delivered/read is per-member — store a fanout-state map; "read by N" aggregates it. Avoid O(N²) receipt storms in large groups by batching/aggregating receipts.


3) Group messaging fanout. Small/medium groups: server fans out the message to each member (push if online, queue if offline). Large groups (10K+): treat like a feed — write once, members pull, or fan out asynchronously via Kafka. Per-conversation ordering preserved by assigning monotonic message_ids within a partition.


4) Presence at scale. Heartbeats every ~30s update Redis TTL; "online" = key present. Don't broadcast every change to everyone — only push presence to users currently viewing that contact/conversation (subscribe-on-open). Last-seen is just the last heartbeat timestamp.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Connection storms after a region outage → reconnect backoff + jitter, capacity headroom.
    • 

  • Ordering with multi-device → server-assigned ids + per-device cursors; sync read state across devices.
    • 

  • E2E encryption → server stores ciphertext; key exchange via Signal/double-ratchet; server can't read content but still routes.
    • 

  • Exactly-once is impractical; use at-least-once + client dedup on client_msg_id.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: media sharing pipeline, message history sync on new device, push notifications when app is killed (hand off to APNs/FCM).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Social & Media
Instagram
Problem & requirements


Functional
- Upload a photo/video with caption.
- Follow users; view a home feed of followees' posts.
- View a user's profile grid.
- Like and comment.
- (Stories / explore often scoped out, mention as extensions.)


Non-functional
- Read-heavy (feed reads ≫ uploads).
- Low feed latency (p99 < 200 ms).
- High availability, eventual consistency acceptable.
- Durable media; efficient image delivery worldwide.


Scale
- 500M DAU.
- 100M photos uploaded/day.
- Avg followers ~300; celebrities into the 100Ms.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Upload writes
100M / 86400
~1.2K uploads/s (peak ~5K)




Feed reads
500M × 20/day / 86400
~115K reads/s (peak ~500K)




Media storage/day
100M × 2 MB (multi-res)
~200 TB/day




Media storage/yr

~70 PB/yr → object store + lifecycle




Metadata/day
100M × 1 KB
~100 GB/day




Egress (CDN)
500M × 50 images × 200 KB
huge → CDN offload, ~95% hit






The dominant cost is media storage + bandwidth; the dominant request path is feed reads.


API design


    

  • POST /v1/media/upload-url → pre-signed S3 URL (client uploads directly).
    • 

  • POST /v1/posts {media_id, caption, location} → {post_id}
    • 

  • GET /v1/feed?cursor=&limit=30 → {posts[], next_cursor}
    • 

  • GET /v1/users/{id}/posts?cursor=
    • 

  • POST /v1/posts/{id}/like, POST /v1/posts/{id}/comments
    • 



Clients upload bytes straight to object storage via pre-signed URL — app servers never proxy large media.


Data model


    

  • Posts (metadata): sharded SQL or NoSQL. post_id (snowflake), user_id, media_refs[], caption, created_at, like_count. Sharded by post_id; secondary index user_id→posts for profile grid.
    • 

  • Media: S3/blob store, multiple resolutions (thumb/medium/full), keyed by media_id. Metadata DB holds only URLs.
    • 

  • Social graph: follows in sharded SQL / Redis sets.
    • 

  • Feed cache: Redis list feed:{user_id} → post_ids (capped).
    • 

  • Counters: like/comment counts in Redis (with periodic DB flush) to avoid hot-row contention.
    • 



SQL works for metadata (modest write rate ~5K/s, relational-ish), but the media tier must be object storage — never blobs in the DB.


High-level architecture


Upload flow: Client requests pre-signed URL → uploads bytes directly to S3 → calls POST /posts with media_id → Post Service writes metadata → emits event to Kafka → Fanout Service pushes post_id into followers' Redis feeds → an async Image Processing worker generates thumbnails/resolutions and stores them, optionally runs moderation/ML tagging.


Feed read flow: Client → Gateway → Feed Service reads feed:{user_id} from Redis → hydrates post_ids → returns metadata + media URLs (pointing at CDN). Client fetches images from CDN edge.


Diagram in words: clients → CDN (for media) and API gateway (for metadata); gateway → Post/Feed/Graph services; Kafka feeding Fanout and Image-processing workers; Redis feed cache + sharded metadata DB + S3 behind it all.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Feed generation: fanout-on-write vs read (same trade-off as Twitter). Push post_ids into followers' Redis lists at upload time → O(1) reads, perfect for read-heavy. Celebrity problem: a 100M-follower post would cause 100M writes → use hybrid: push for normal accounts, pull-and-merge for celebrities at read time. Feed = precomputed list merged with recent celebrity posts.


2) Media pipeline & delivery. Direct-to-S3 upload avoids app-server bottlenecks. An async worker transcodes into several resolutions (mobile/tablet/web, WebP/AVIF). Everything served via CDN with long TTLs; ~95% edge hit rate slashes origin egress. Use a hash/versioned URL so re-uploads bust cache. This decouples the slow image work from the fast post-create ack.


3) Hot counters & hot posts. Like counts on a viral post would hammer a single row. Keep counts in Redis (atomic INCR), shard the counter across keys for extreme hotness, flush to DB periodically. Cache the hydrated hot post object to absorb the thundering herd.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Storage growth (70 PB/yr) → tiered storage / lifecycle to cold storage for old media; dedup identical uploads by content hash.
    • 

  • Feed ranking → replace chronological with an ML ranker (engagement features) before return.
    • 

  • Cold start / new follow → backfill or pull-merge to surface recent posts.
    • 

  • Moderation → async ML scan in the processing pipeline; quarantine before publish if needed.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: Stories (ephemeral, 24h TTL — TTL on cache/objects), Explore (separate recommendation pipeline), consistency of like counts (eventually consistent, acceptable).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Social & Media
News Feed System
Problem & requirements


This is the generalized feed problem underlying Facebook/Twitter/Instagram/LinkedIn — worth treating abstractly because interviewers ask it on its own.


Functional
- Publish a post (text/media/link) from a user or page.
- Build each user's feed from sources they follow/friend.
- Rank feed (chronological or ML-ranked by relevance/engagement).
- Support likes/comments/shares as feed items / signals.


Non-functional
- Read-heavy; feed reads p99 < 200 ms.
- Eventually consistent (a post appearing seconds late is fine).
- Highly available, horizontally scalable.
- Personalized and ranked, not just time-ordered.


Scale
- 1B DAU, ~500M producers.
- Avg ~300 connections; power users / pages have millions of followers.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Post writes
~200M posts/day / 86400
~2.3K writes/s (peak ~10K)




Feed reads
1B × 15 refreshes/day / 86400
~175K reads/s (peak ~700K)




Fanout inserts
2.3K × 300 avg
~700K inserts/s




Feed cache size
1B users × 500 ids × 8 B
~4 TB Redis (sharded)




Post storage/day
200M × 1 KB
~200 GB/day






API design


    

  • POST /v1/posts {content, media_ids[], audience} → {post_id}
    • 

  • GET /v1/feed?cursor=&limit=30 → {ranked_items[], next_cursor}
    • 

  • POST /v1/posts/{id}/react, /comments
    • 

  • GET /v1/posts/{id} (hydration / deep link)
    • 



Cursor pagination on a stable sort key; the cursor encodes ranking position or (score, id).


Data model


    

  • Posts: NoSQL/sharded SQL, key post_id (snowflake). author_id, content, media_refs, created_at, audience, engagement_stats.
    • 

  • Graph (edges): follows/friends — adjacency in sharded SQL or a graph store; cached as Redis sets.
    • 

  • Feed store: Redis per-user list of post_ids (or scored sorted-set for ranked feeds). Derived/ephemeral.
    • 

  • Feature store: precomputed user & post features for the ranking model (KV store).
    • 



Feed data is derived and disposable → cache/NoSQL. Posts are durable, append-heavy → NoSQL. Relational integrity isn't the bottleneck; throughput is.


High-level architecture


Write path: Client → Gateway → Post Service persists post + emits to Kafka → Fanout Service resolves followers and inserts post_id into each follower's feed store (push), skipping hot producers.


Read path: Client → Gateway → Feed Service → fetch candidate post_ids from feed store + pull recent posts from followed hot producers → Ranking Service scores candidates using the feature store + ML model → hydrate top-N from Post store/cache → return. 


Diagram in words: a write side (Post Service → Kafka → Fanout → feed store) and a read side (Feed Service → candidate generation → Ranking → hydration). The feature/ranking pipeline sits on the read side; storage (posts NoSQL, feed Redis, feature KV, media S3/CDN) underneath.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Push vs pull vs hybrid (the core decision).






Model
Write cost
Read cost
Best for






Push (fanout-on-write)
High (N inserts/post)
O(1)
Most users, read-heavy




Pull (fanout-on-read)
O(1)
High (merge K sources)
Inactive users, celebrities




Hybrid
Bounded
Low
Production default






Hybrid: push for normal authors; for authors with >~10K followers, don't fan out — readers pull their recent posts and merge at read time. Also pull-only for long-inactive users to avoid wasting writes on feeds nobody reads.


2) Ranking. Chronological is the baseline; real feeds rank. Pipeline = candidate generation (push list + pulls + recommended content) → feature fetch → lightweight model scoring → diversity/dedup rules → top-N. Trade latency for relevance; precompute features offline, keep online scoring cheap.


3) Fanout worker scaling & consistency. Fanout is async via Kafka so post-create returns fast. Workers scale horizontally and are idempotent (dedupe by post_id). New edges (a fresh follow) don't backfill history — handled by pull-merge or a lightweight backfill job. Deletes/audience changes are filtered at read via tombstones rather than rewriting every feed.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Hot producers / viral posts → pull-merge + aggressive object caching + request coalescing.
    • 

  • Redis cost (4 TB) → cap feed length, evict inactive users, lazy rebuild on next visit.
    • 

  • Ranking latency → cap candidate set, async feature fetch, cache scores.
    • 

  • Thundering herd on a celebrity post → cache hydrated post.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: how to A/B test ranking, how to inject ads/recommendations, dedup/seen-state so users don't re-see items, audience/privacy filtering at read.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Video & Streaming
YouTube / Netflix
Problem & requirements


Functional
- Upload a video (YouTube) or ingest a catalog title (Netflix).
- Transcode into multiple resolutions/bitrates and formats.
- Stream adaptively (ABR) to any device/network.
- Browse/search; view counts; (comments/recommendations as extensions).


Non-functional
- Massive read/streaming bandwidth, low startup latency (<2 s to first frame), minimal rebuffering.
- Durable originals; high availability.
- Global low-latency delivery (CDN-first).
- Upload is write-light; playback is the heavy path.


Scale
- 2B users; 1B hours watched/day.
- 500 hours of video uploaded/min (YouTube-scale).


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Upload ingest
500 hr/min × ~1 GB/hr raw
~8 GB/s ingest, huge




Transcode fanout
1 source → ~6 renditions × formats
~6–10× storage of source




Streaming egress
1B hr/day × ~3 Mbps
~petabits/day → CDN




Metadata QPS
catalog/browse reads
~100K+ QPS




Storage
originals + renditions, PB–EB
object store + tiering






The headline: streaming bandwidth is the system. CDN offload (>95% from edge) is mandatory; origin only fills caches.


API design


    

  • POST /v1/videos → create record + {upload_url} (resumable/chunked upload to object store).
    • 

  • POST /v1/videos/{id}/complete → triggers transcoding pipeline.
    • 

  • GET /v1/videos/{id} → metadata + manifest URL.
    • 

  • GET /v1/videos/{id}/manifest.m3u8 (HLS) or .mpd (DASH) → adaptive manifest pointing at CDN segment URLs.
    • 

  • Player then GETs …/segment_{res}_{n}.ts from the CDN.
    • 



Resumable chunked upload (e.g. tus / multipart) so a dropped connection doesn't restart a 4 GB upload.


Data model


    

  • Video metadata: SQL or NoSQL. video_id, uploader_id, title, desc, duration, status(processing/ready), rendition_manifest, created_at. Modest write rate → either works; relational for catalog relationships (Netflix), NoSQL for scale (YouTube).
    • 

  • Originals + renditions: object storage (S3/GCS), keyed by video_id + rendition. The bulk of bytes. Lifecycle-tier cold titles.
    • 

  • View counts / analytics: stream events to Kafka → batch/stream aggregation (not synchronous DB writes).
    • 

  • Search index: inverted index (Elasticsearch) over titles/metadata.
    • 



DB never stores video bytes — only pointers/manifests. Object storage + CDN hold the media.


High-level architecture


Upload→publish flow: Client chunk-uploads original to object store → complete enqueues a job → Transcoding Pipeline (a DAG of workers) splits the video into chunks, transcodes each chunk in parallel into multiple bitrates/resolutions and codecs (H.264/VP9/AV1), packages into HLS/DASH segments + manifests, writes outputs to object store, updates metadata status=ready. CDN pulls segments from origin on first request.


Playback flow: Player → metadata service for the manifest URL → fetches manifest → for each segment, requests the appropriate bitrate from the nearest CDN edge → ABR logic on the client raises/lowers bitrate based on measured bandwidth/buffer. Origin object store only serves CDN misses.


Diagram in words: left = uploaders and viewers; an API/metadata tier; a Kafka-fed transcoding worker farm writing to object storage; a global CDN in front of object storage serving 95%+ of bytes; analytics pipeline siphoning view events.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Transcoding pipeline (the hard part). Treat one video as many independent chunks → transcode chunks in parallel across a worker fleet → massively reduces wall-clock time and lets a job survive worker failure (retry just the failed chunk). Each chunk produces a ladder of renditions (e.g. 144p→4K) and is packaged into ~2–10 s segments. Trade-offs: more renditions/codecs = better device coverage and bandwidth efficiency (AV1 saves bytes) but more compute + storage. Pipeline is a DAG: split → transcode → package → QC → publish.


2) Adaptive bitrate streaming (ABR) + CDN. Video is chunked into short segments at multiple bitrates; the manifest lists them. The player picks per-segment bitrate from current throughput/buffer, so it degrades gracefully instead of stalling. CDN caches popular segments at the edge; cache key = segment URL. Pre-warm/pre-position popular titles (Netflix pushes content to ISP-embedded edge caches — Open Connect — ahead of demand).


3) Storage & cost tiering. Originals + N renditions = 6–10× source size and grows forever. Tier cold/unpopular content to cheaper storage; possibly drop rarely-used high renditions and re-generate on demand. Dedup identical uploads by content hash.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Startup latency / rebuffering → short initial segments, prefetch first segments, edge proximity.
    • 

  • Live streaming (vs VOD) → low-latency HLS/DASH, tighter segments, real-time transcode — call out the difference.
    • 

  • Thundering herd on a viral new video → CDN cache + origin shielding (mid-tier cache) so origin isn't hammered on cold cache.
    • 

  • DRM (Netflix) → Widevine/FairPlay, encrypted segments + license server.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: view-count accuracy (dedup, bot filtering, eventually-consistent counters), recommendations pipeline, resumable upload internals, per-region compliance/geoblocking.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Location & Realtime
Uber / Lyft
Problem & requirements


Functional
- Riders request a ride from A→B; drivers receive and accept/reject offers.
- Continuous driver location updates; rider sees driver position live.
- Match a request to the best nearby available driver.
- Trip lifecycle: requested → matched → en route → in progress → completed → paid.
- ETA and fare estimation.


Non-functional
- Low latency matching (seconds to find a driver).
- High write throughput from constant location pings.
- High availability; geographically partitioned.
- Consistency where it matters: one driver matched to one rider (no double-booking).


Scale
- 100M users, ~5M active drivers.
- Drivers ping location every ~4 s.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Location writes
5M drivers / 4 s
~1.25M location updates/s




Ride requests
~peak
~10K–50K requests/s




Geo-query QPS
each request → nearby search
tied to request rate




Location store
5M × small record, in-memory
a few GB (Redis/geo index)




Trip storage
durable, SQL
modest write rate






The headline write load is 1.25M location updates/s — they must be cheap, mostly in-memory, not durably written to a SQL DB on every ping.


API design


    

  • POST /v1/location {driver_id, lat, lng, ts} (high-frequency, fire-and-forget, often over a persistent connection).
    • 

  • POST /v1/rides {rider_id, pickup, dropoff} → {ride_id, status}
    • 

  • GET /v1/rides/{id} → status + matched driver + live driver location.
    • 

  • POST /v1/rides/{id}/accept (driver), /cancel, /complete.
    • 

  • Push channel (WebSocket) for real-time driver-offer and rider-tracking updates.
    • 



Data model


    

  • Driver location (live): in-memory geo index — Redis GEO or a quadtree/geohash service. Volatile, overwritten every ping. Key by driver_id; spatially indexed by cell.
    • 

  • Trips: SQL (Postgres/MySQL), strongly consistent. ride_id, rider_id, driver_id, status, pickup, dropoff, fare, timestamps. State transitions need ACID + transactions (don't double-assign a driver).
    • 

  • Users/drivers: SQL.
    • 

  • Geo partitioning: the world is divided into cells (geohash/S2/H3); each region's data and matching handled by region-local services.
    • 



Why split: locations are high-churn ephemeral → in-memory; trips are money + state-machine → SQL with transactions.


High-level architecture


Location flow: Driver app pings → Location Service (over WebSocket) → updates the in-memory geo index (Redis GEO / geohash buckets) and a session registry. No durable write per ping.


Match flow: Rider requests → Ride Service creates a trip (status=requested) → calls Matching Service → Matching queries the geo index for available drivers within radius of pickup (geospatial search), ranks by ETA/distance/rating → sends an offer to the top driver via push → on accept, Ride Service transactionally assigns driver↔trip (atomic check-and-set so two riders can't grab the same driver). Rider then receives live driver location streamed from the Location Service.


Diagram in words: rider & driver apps hold WebSockets to gateway; Location Service + geo index on the hot path of pings; Ride Service + Matching Service + SQL trip store on the booking path; everything partitioned by geographic region; Kafka for trip events → pricing, ETA, analytics.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Geo-indexing & nearby search (the core). Naive "compute distance to all 5M drivers" is impossible per request. Partition space with geohash / quadtree / S2 / H3 cells: index drivers by the cell they're in; a nearby search reads the rider's cell + neighboring cells and filters by exact distance. Cell granularity trades precision vs fan-out (too coarse → too many candidates; too fine → must scan many cells). Redis GEO (sorted-set of geohash scores) implements this directly with GEOSEARCH.


2) Handling 1.25M location writes/s. Keep them in memory only — last-known-position overwrite, no per-ping durable write. Shard the geo index by region so no single node holds the world. Drivers connect to a region-local Location Service via consistent hashing. Periodically snapshot or stream to analytics, but the hot path is RAM.


3) Matching correctness & race conditions. Two riders must not be matched to one driver. Make assignment a transactional compare-and-set on the trip/driver record (SQL row lock or a distributed lock keyed by driver_id). Offer with a short timeout; if the driver doesn't accept, release and offer the next candidate. Idempotent accept handling prevents double-assign on retries.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Hot regions (airport, concert) → finer cells + more matching capacity in that region; surge pricing as a load valve.
    • 

  • Driver supply/demand imbalance → surge pricing service consuming demand signals.
    • 

  • ETA accuracy → routing/traffic service (separate), not straight-line distance.
    • 

  • Region boundaries → search must span adjacent cells across shard borders; replicate edge data.
    • 

  • Failure → if a driver's pings stop (TTL expiry), mark unavailable; reassign in-progress trips on driver disconnect.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: payments/idempotency, trip replay/audit, pool/shared rides matching (much harder), exactly-once trip-state transitions.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
Notification System
Problem & requirements


Functional
- Send notifications across channels: mobile push (APNs/FCM), SMS, email, in-app.
- Support triggered (event-driven) and scheduled/batch (campaign) sends.
- Fanout: one event → many recipients (e.g. "creator posted" → 1M followers).
- Templating, per-user preferences/opt-out, rate limiting, deduplication.
- Track delivery status (sent/delivered/failed/opened).


Non-functional
- High throughput, async, decoupled from callers.
- At-least-once delivery with dedup (avoid double-notifying).
- Resilient to third-party provider failures (APNs/Twilio/SES outages).
- Respect user preferences and rate limits (no spam).


Scale
- 1B users; 10B notifications/day across channels.
- Campaign bursts: a single push to 100M users.


Capacity estimation






Metric
Calculation
Result






Steady throughput
10B / 86400
~115K notifications/s




Peak (campaign burst)
100M over a few min
~500K–1M/s spikes




Provider calls
same, but batched
batch to APNs/FCM/SES




Status/event storage
10B × ~200 B
~2 TB/day (TTL/aggregate)




Queue depth
bursts buffered
size queues for backlog






The system is fundamentally a queue-and-fanout problem; the headline is absorbing bursty fanout without hammering downstream providers.


API design


    

  • POST /v1/notify {user_id|segment, template_id, channel_prefs, data, dedup_key, priority} → {notification_id} (returns fast; work is async).
    • 

  • POST /v1/campaigns {segment_id, template_id, schedule} → batch job.
    • 

  • GET /v1/notifications/{id} → status.
    • 

  • Webhooks/callbacks from providers update delivery status.
    • 



dedup_key ensures the same event doesn't double-send on retries.


Data model


    

  • Notification log: NoSQL/wide-column. notification_id, user_id, channel, status, template_id, created_at, dedup_key. High write volume, append-heavy, TTL'd → NoSQL.
    • 

  • User preferences: SQL/KV. user_id, channels_enabled, quiet_hours, frequency_caps, device_tokens[]. Relational-ish, read on every send → cache heavily.
    • 

  • Templates: SQL/KV, versioned.
    • 

  • Device-token registry: KV (user_id → [APNs/FCM tokens]); prune invalid tokens on provider feedback.
    • 

  • Dedup store: Redis set of dedup_keys with TTL.
    • 



High-level architecture


Flow: Caller → POST /notify → Notification Service validates, enqueues onto Kafka (returns immediately). For a segment/campaign, a Fanout Service expands the segment into per-user messages and enqueues them. Channel Workers (separate pools per channel) consume the queue: each worker (a) checks user preferences + frequency caps + quiet hours, (b) checks the dedup store, (c) renders the template, (d) calls the channel provider adapter (APNs/FCM/Twilio/SES), (e) writes status to the notification log. Provider callbacks update delivered/opened status asynchronously.


Diagram in words: producers (any service) → Notification API → Kafka → Fanout (segment expansion) → per-channel Kafka topics → per-channel worker pools → provider adapters → external providers (APNs/FCM/SES/Twilio). Preference service, template service, dedup store, and notification log hang off the workers. A retry/DLQ path loops failed messages back.


Deep dives & scaling


1) Fanout for huge segments. A campaign to 100M users can't be a single synchronous loop. The Fanout Service reads the segment from a precomputed segment store and produces messages in batches onto Kafka; partitioning by user_id spreads load across many partitions/workers. This smooths a 100M burst into a high-but-bounded stream and isolates campaign traffic from latency-sensitive transactional notifications (separate priority queues/topics).


2) Reliability: at-least-once + dedup + retries. Queues give durability and backpressure. Workers ack only after a successful provider call; failures go to a retry topic with exponential backoff, then a dead-letter queue after N attempts. Because retries cause duplicates, every send checks a Redis dedup_key (TTL'd) so the user isn't notified twice for the same logical event. Exactly-once is impractical end-to-end (providers can double-deliver), so the contract is at-least-once + idempotent dedup.


3) Provider abstraction & failure isolation. A per-channel adapter layer normalizes APNs/FCM/Twilio/SES quirks (batching limits, token formats, rate limits). Use circuit breakers and per-provider rate limiters so one provider's outage or throttling doesn't back up the whole system; failover to a secondary provider where possible. Prune dead device tokens on provider feedback to avoid wasted sends.


4) Preferences, quiet hours, rate limiting. Every send is gated by the preference service: channel opt-outs, frequency caps (e.g. max N/day), and quiet-hours windows (defer to later). This is the anti-spam layer and a common interviewer probe — it must be checked at send time, with preferences cached for speed.


Bottlenecks & interviewer follow-ups


    

  • Provider rate limits → batch sends (APNs/FCM accept multicast), token bucket per provider.
    • 

  • Hot queue partitions during a campaign → partition by user_id, isolate campaign topic from transactional topic.
    • 

  • Status tracking at 10B/day → aggregate/TTL the log; don't keep per-message rows forever.
    • 

  • Priority inversion → separate high-priority (OTP/2FA) and bulk (marketing) lanes so an OTP never queues behind a campaign.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: scheduling/delayed delivery (delay queue), localization/templating, idempotency guarantees, analytics on open/click rates, GDPR opt-out enforcement.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
URL Shortener (TinyURL)
Problem & requirements


A URL shortener takes a long URL and returns a short alias; visiting the alias 301/302-redirects to the original. This is the canonical "read-heavy storage + ID generation" warm-up question.


Functional
- shorten(longUrl) → short code (e.g. bit.ly/aZ4x9Q).
- resolve(shortCode) → redirect to long URL.
- Optional: custom aliases, expiry/TTL, analytics (click counts), user accounts.


Non-functional
- Read-heavy: ~100:1 read:write ratio (redirects vastly outnumber creates).
- Low latency: redirect must feel instant — p99 < ~50ms.
- High availability: a dead shortener breaks every embedded link; favor AP. A redirect that occasionally fails is worse than a stale one.
- Short, non-guessable codes; no collisions.
- Durability: links must survive for years.


Capacity estimation


Assume 100M new URLs/month.






Metric
Calculation
Result






Write QPS
100M / (30·86400)
~40 writes/s




Read QPS
100:1
~4,000 reads/s




Peak read QPS
2–3×
~10,000/s




Storage/row
~500 B (URLs + metadata)
—




5-yr rows
100M·12·5
6B rows




Total storage
6B · 500 B
~3 TB






Reads dominate, so an in-memory cache is the workhorse. 3 TB fits comfortably on a sharded DB.


Keyspace: base62 ([a-zA-Z0-9]). 62^7 ≈ 3.5 trillion codes → 7 chars is plenty; many products use 6–7.


API design


POST /api/v1/shorten
  body: { longUrl, customAlias?, expiryDate? }
  → 200 { shortUrl }

GET /{shortCode}
  → 301/302 Location: <longUrl>




    

  • 301 (permanent) caches in browser → fewer hits but no analytics. 302 (temporary) routes every click through you → enables click tracking. Pick 302 if analytics matter.
    • 



Data model / schema


Single wide table, keyed by short code:






Column
Type






short_code (PK)
varchar(7)




long_url
text




created_at
timestamp




user_id
bigint




expiry_at
timestamp null






SQL vs NoSQL: This is a key→value lookup with no joins, so a NoSQL KV store (DynamoDB/Cassandra) scales horizontally and gives O(1) lookup by partition key (short_code). SQL (Postgres + sharding) is also fine at this scale and simpler if you want secondary indexes / analytics joins. Either is defensible — justify by access pattern (point lookups → KV).


High-level architecture


Request flow, write path: client → DNS → Load Balancer → stateless app servers → ID/code generator → DB (+ cache write-through).


Read path: client → LB → app server → cache (Redis) → on miss, DB → populate cache → 302 redirect.


Components:
- LB spreads traffic across stateless app tiers (easy to autoscale).
- Redis cache holds hot codes; redirect latency is dominated by cache hits. With 80/20, caching ~20% of links serves most reads.
- DB sharded by short_code hash.
- CDN/edge: for very hot links, cache the redirect at the edge.
- Analytics pipeline: redirects emit an event to Kafka → stream processor → OLAP store, decoupled so click logging never slows the redirect.


Deep dives & scaling


1. Generating the short code — the core decision.
- Hash-based (MD5/base62 of longUrl, take 7 chars): collisions need a check-and-retry loop; same URL → same code (dedup) but messy on collisions.
- Counter + base62 encode: a global monotonic counter, base62-encoded, is collision-free and short. The counter is the bottleneck — solve with a distributed ID generator (Snowflake) or range allocation: each app server grabs a block (e.g. 1,000 IDs) from a central service (ZooKeeper/DB), hands them out locally, refills when low. No per-write coordination → huge throughput. Codes look sequential, so optionally bijective-scramble them.
- KGS (Key Generation Service): pre-generate random unique keys offline into a DB; app servers check out keys in batches. Decouples uniqueness from the write path entirely.


2. Cache strategy & hot keys. A single viral link can hammer one cache node. Mitigate with local in-process caching on app servers (LRU) in front of Redis, and replicate hot keys across nodes. Use TTL + write-through so cache and DB stay consistent.


3. Read-heavy scaling. Add read replicas; serve redirects from replicas, writes to primary. Eventual consistency is acceptable — a newly created link being unreadable for milliseconds is fine.


Bottlenecks & follow-ups


    

  • Custom aliases: must check uniqueness against the same keyspace → conditional write (INSERT IF NOT EXISTS).
    • 

  • Link expiry / cleanup: lazy deletion on read + periodic batch sweeper.
    • 

  • Abuse: rate-limit creation; scan long URLs against malware/phishing blocklists.
    • 

  • Analytics at scale: don't UPDATE count per click (write hot row) — aggregate in the stream layer.
    • 

  • Interviewer probes: "What if the counter service dies?" (range allocation survives — servers still have unspent blocks), "301 vs 302 trade-off?", "How do you prevent enumeration?" (random/scrambled codes, not raw sequential).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
Rate Limiter
Problem & requirements


A rate limiter caps how many requests a client (user/IP/API key) may make in a time window, protecting services from abuse, accidental floods, and cascading overload. It's both a design question and an algorithms question.


Functional
- Allow/deny a request given a key and a configured limit (e.g. 100 req/min per API key).
- Multiple rules: per-user, per-IP, per-endpoint, tiered by plan.
- Return clear signaling: HTTP 429 Too Many Requests + Retry-After / X-RateLimit-Remaining headers.


Non-functional
- Very low latency: it sits on the hot path of every request; must add < ~1–2ms.
- High availability: limiter outage shouldn't take down the API — decide fail-open vs fail-closed.
- Accurate enough under distributed traffic; memory-efficient.
- Distributed: limits must hold across many app servers, not per-box.


Capacity estimation


For a service at 1M req/s, the limiter is consulted 1M times/s. Each check is a read-modify-write on a counter. Counters are tiny (key → int + timestamp ≈ 100 B). For 100M distinct keys → ~10 GB of counter state — fits in a Redis cluster in memory. The dominant cost is QPS against the counter store, not storage, so the design centers on cheap atomic ops and minimizing round-trips.


API design


Usually a middleware/library + a shared counter store, not a public API. Internally:


allow(key, rule) → { allowed: bool, remaining: int, resetAt: ts }




Response headers on the protected service:


X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, Retry-After




Data model


Key-value, keyed by {client_id}:{rule}:{window}:
- Token-bucket: { tokens, last_refill_ts }.
- Fixed-window: { count, window_start }.
- Sliding-log: a sorted set of request timestamps.


Store choice: Redis — in-memory, atomic ops (INCR, EXPIRE), and Lua scripts for atomic read-modify-write. NoSQL/in-memory beats SQL here because state is ephemeral, high-churn, and needs sub-ms atomic increments.


Algorithms (the heart of the answer)






Algorithm
Idea
Pros
Cons






Fixed window
count per calendar window
trivial, low memory
boundary burst: 2× limit across the window edge




Sliding window log
store every request timestamp
exact
memory-heavy, costly to trim




Sliding window counter
weighted blend of current + previous window
smooth, cheap
slight approximation




Token bucket
tokens refill at rate R, each req spends 1
allows bursts up to bucket size; intuitive
needs refill math




Leaky bucket
requests queue, drain at constant rate
smooths output
adds latency, queue mgmt






Token bucket is the most common interview answer: capacity = burst allowance, refill rate = sustained limit. Sliding window counter is the best accuracy/memory trade-off for fixed quotas.


High-level architecture


Flow: client → LB → rate-limiter middleware (in API gateway / sidecar) → counter store (Redis) → if allowed, forward to service; else return 429.


    

  • Where it lives: at the API gateway / Envoy sidecar so it's enforced before business logic. Rules loaded from a config service.
    • 

  • Shared state: all gateway instances read/write the same Redis cluster, so the limit is global, not per-instance.
    • 

  • Atomicity: do the check-and-decrement in a single Redis Lua script so concurrent requests on the same key can't both pass (avoids read-then-write race).
    • 



Deep dives & scaling


1. Distributed accuracy vs latency. Centralized Redis is accurate but every request pays a network hop. Optimization: per-node local counters that sync periodically — fast but can overshoot the global limit. Trade-off: strict global accuracy (central, slower) vs approximate-but-fast (local + eventual sync). State the choice explicitly.


2. Race conditions. Naive GET→check→SET lets two requests both see remaining=1. Fix with atomic INCR+EXPIRE (fixed window) or a Lua script (token bucket) that reads, refills, and decrements in one round-trip.


3. Hot keys. A single abusive key concentrates load on one Redis shard. Mitigate by sharding on the key, local caching of "already blocked" decisions, and short-circuiting known offenders at the edge.


4. Failure mode. If Redis is unreachable: fail-open (allow traffic — availability over protection, typical for user-facing APIs) or fail-closed (deny — for security-critical endpoints). Pick per use case.


Bottlenecks & follow-ups


    

  • Clock skew across servers affects window math → use a single time source or store server-relative timestamps.
    • 

  • Rule explosion: many tiers/endpoints → config service + caching of rules.
    • 

  • Multi-region: do you want a global limit or per-region? Global needs cross-region replication (latency); per-region is simpler and usually acceptable.
    • 

  • Interviewer probes: "Why does fixed window allow 2× at the boundary?", "Make the check atomic", "Fail-open or closed and why?", "How would you support burst traffic?" (token bucket capacity).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
Distributed Key-Value Store / Cache
Problem & requirements


Design a distributed key-value store (à la DynamoDB / Cassandra) or distributed cache (à la Redis Cluster / Memcached): get(key) / put(key, value) across many nodes, scaling beyond one machine's memory/disk and surviving node failures.


Functional
- get(key) → value, put(key, value), optional delete(key), TTL.
- Data spread across nodes; transparent to clients.


Non-functional
- Scalability: add nodes to grow capacity/throughput linearly.
- High availability: survive node + datacenter failure (AP system in CAP terms for many designs).
- Low latency: single-digit ms for cache; configurable for stores.
- Tunable consistency: from strong to eventual.
- Durability (for stores; caches may be volatile).


This is the classic CAP question — you cannot have consistency + availability + partition tolerance simultaneously. Partitions happen, so you choose CP (reject writes to stay consistent) or AP (stay available, reconcile later). Dynamo-style picks AP.


Capacity estimation


Say 1 PB total, 10 KB avg value → 100B keys. Replication factor 3 → 3 PB raw. At 10 TB/node → ~300 nodes. Throughput: 1M ops/s spread across nodes → ~3–4K ops/s/node, trivially handled in memory. Bandwidth for replication: each write fans out to 3 nodes, so internal traffic ≈ 3× client write traffic — size the cross-node network accordingly.


API design


put(key, value, {consistency, ttl}) → ack
get(key, {consistency}) → value, version
delete(key) → ack




Consistency level per call (ONE / QUORUM / ALL) is the Dynamo/Cassandra signature.


Data model


Opaque bytes keyed by string key + a version (vector clock or timestamp) for conflict resolution. No schema, no joins — that's the point. NoSQL by definition; the question is the topology, not SQL-vs-NoSQL.


High-level architecture


Flow: client → coordinator node (any node can coordinate) → consistent-hash ring locates the N replica nodes → write/read to replicas with quorum → respond.


Key components:
- Consistent hashing ring: keys and nodes hashed onto a ring; a key is owned by the next N nodes clockwise. Adding/removing a node only remaps keys on adjacent ring segments — O(K/N) keys move, not the whole keyspace.
- Virtual nodes (vnodes): each physical node maps to many ring points → even load distribution and smooth rebalancing when nodes of unequal size join.
- Replication: each key stored on N successor nodes (preference list), spanning racks/DCs for fault tolerance.
- Gossip protocol: nodes exchange membership/health state peer-to-peer (no central master), enabling decentralized failure detection.


Deep dives & scaling


1. Quorum consistency (W + R > N). With N replicas, require W acks on write and R reads. If W + R > N, read and write sets overlap → you always read the latest write (strong-ish). Examples: N=3, W=3/R=1 (fast reads, slow writes); W=1/R=3 (fast writes); W=2/R=2 (balanced). Lower W/R → higher availability, weaker consistency. This is the central tunable knob — explain it.


2. Conflict resolution. Concurrent writes to the same key on different replicas create divergent versions. Options:
- Last-write-wins (LWW) by timestamp: simple, can lose data under clock skew.
- Vector clocks: detect concurrent vs causal updates; if truly concurrent, return both siblings to the client to merge (Dynamo's shopping-cart approach).


3. Anti-entropy & failure recovery.
- Hinted handoff: if a replica is down, a healthy node temporarily stores the write + a hint, replays it when the node returns → keeps writes available during transient failures.
- Merkle trees: replicas compare hash trees to find divergent key ranges cheaply and repair only the differences (read-repair / background sync).


4. Cache-specific concerns (if framed as Redis/Memcached):
- Eviction: LRU/LFU/TTL when memory full.
- Cache stampede / thundering herd: many misses on a hot expired key hit the DB simultaneously → fix with request coalescing (single-flight), staggered TTLs, or pre-warming.
- Write policies: write-through (consistent, slower), write-back (fast, risk of loss), cache-aside (app manages).


Bottlenecks & follow-ups


    

  • Hot keys / hot partitions: one key gets disproportionate traffic → replicate it more widely, add a client-side local cache, or split the key.
    • 

  • Rebalancing storms: adding nodes triggers data movement → throttle, use vnodes to keep moves small.
    • 

  • Cross-DC replication latency: async replication for availability; accept eventual consistency across regions.
    • 

  • Interviewer probes: "Explain consistent hashing and why mod-N hashing is bad" (mod-N remaps everything when N changes), "What is W+R>N?", "How do you handle a permanent node loss?", "Vector clock vs LWW trade-off?"
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
Pastebin
Problem & requirements


Pastebin stores arbitrary text/code blobs and returns a short URL to retrieve them. It's a close cousin of the URL shortener but with large payloads, making it the canonical "metadata DB + object store" question.


Functional
- createPaste(content, expiry?, visibility?) → URL.
- getPaste(pasteKey) → content.
- Optional: expiration (TTL), public vs unlisted, syntax highlighting, custom keys.


Non-functional
- Read-heavy (a paste is written once, read many times), but less skewed than a URL shortener.
- Large objects: pastes range from bytes to several MB → don't store blobs in the metadata DB.
- Low read latency; high availability; durable storage.
- Size limits (e.g. 1–10 MB per paste) to bound abuse.


Capacity estimation


Assume 1M new pastes/day.






Metric
Calculation
Result






Write QPS
1M / 86400
~12 writes/s




Read QPS
~10:1
~120 reads/s (peak ~500/s)




Avg paste size
~10 KB
—




Daily storage
1M · 10 KB
~10 GB/day




5-yr storage
10 GB · 365 · 5
~18 TB






QPS is modest; storage volume and large-blob handling dominate the design, hence object storage + metadata split.


API design


POST /api/v1/paste
  body: { content, expiry?, visibility, language? }
  → 201 { pasteUrl, pasteKey }

GET /{pasteKey}
  → 200 { content, metadata }

DELETE /api/v1/paste/{pasteKey}




Data model / schema


Split storage — the key design idea:
- Metadata DB (small, indexed): paste_key (PK), object_url, created_at, expiry_at, user_id, size, visibility.
- Object store (S3 / blob store): the actual paste content at object_url.






Store
Holds
Why






Metadata DB (NoSQL KV, e.g. DynamoDB)
key → pointer + attrs
point lookups, no joins




Object store (S3)
paste bodies
cheap, durable, infinite scale, offloads blob I/O from DB






Keeping multi-MB blobs out of the DB keeps it small, fast, and cheap to index. The DB row is ~200 B regardless of paste size.


High-level architecture


Write flow: client → LB → app server → (1) generate unique key → (2) write content to object store → (3) write metadata row pointing at the object → return URL.


Read flow: client → LB → app server → metadata DB (get object_url) → cache check → object store fetch → return. For public/popular pastes, front the object store with a CDN so repeated reads never touch your servers.


Components:
- LB + stateless app tier (autoscale).
- Key Generation Service / Snowflake for unique paste keys (same options as URL shortener: base62 counter, KGS, or hash).
- Object store (S3) for bodies; metadata DB for pointers.
- Cache (Redis) for hot paste content + metadata.
- CDN for public pastes.
- Cleanup worker for expirations.


Deep dives & scaling


1. Why split metadata and content? A single DB holding 18 TB of variable-size blobs would have huge rows, expensive scans, and poor cache locality. Splitting lets the metadata DB stay tiny (fast point lookups, cheap to shard/replicate) while S3 handles durability/scale of bytes at low cost. The metadata row stores only a pointer + attributes.


2. Key generation & collisions. Same trade-offs as URL shortener: a pre-generated key pool (KGS) hands out guaranteed-unique base62 keys, removing collision checks from the write path. Random keys also make pastes unguessable (privacy for unlisted pastes).


3. Expiration & cleanup. Pastes have TTLs. Use S3 lifecycle policies to auto-delete expired objects + a periodic sweeper to purge expired metadata rows, plus lazy deletion (treat expired pastes as 404 on read even before the sweeper runs). Avoids a giant nightly delete job.


4. Read scaling. Cache hot pastes in Redis; push public ones to the CDN. Most reads then bypass both the DB and your app servers entirely.


Bottlenecks & follow-ups


    

  • Large uploads: support presigned S3 URLs so clients upload content directly to the object store, bypassing your app servers (saves bandwidth and avoids large-request handling).
    • 

  • Hot paste: a viral paste → CDN + cache absorb it; one row in the DB.
    • 

  • Abuse/malware: size caps, rate-limit creation, scan content.
    • 

  • Analytics (view counts): aggregate asynchronously via a queue, don't UPDATE a hot row per view.
    • 

  • Interviewer probes: "Where does the actual text live and why not the DB?", "How do presigned URLs help?", "How do you expire millions of pastes efficiently?", "How is this different from a URL shortener?" (large payload + object-store split).
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Infrastructure
Unique ID Generator (Snowflake) + Consistent Hashing
Problem & requirements


Generate globally unique IDs across a distributed system — for primary keys, message ordering, short codes — without a single bottleneck. Bundled here with consistent hashing, the partitioning technique that underpins every distributed store.


Functional
- Produce unique 64-bit IDs at high throughput from many machines.
- IDs should ideally be roughly time-sortable (k-sorted) so they cluster by creation time (good for DB indexes, pagination).


Non-functional
- Uniqueness guaranteed across all generators, forever.
- High throughput: tens of thousands of IDs/s/node, no coordination per ID.
- Low latency: local generation, no network call.
- Highly available: no central single point of failure.
- Compact: fits in a 64-bit integer (cheaper than UUID's 128 bits, and sortable).


Why not the obvious approaches?






Approach
Problem






DB auto-increment
single-DB bottleneck, SPOF, doesn't scale horizontally




UUID v4
128-bit, not sortable, random → bad as a clustered DB index (page splits)




Central ticket server
network hop per ID, SPOF, throughput ceiling






Snowflake: the 64-bit layout


Twitter Snowflake packs a 64-bit ID:






Bits
Field
Purpose






1
sign
always 0 (positive)




41
timestamp (ms since custom epoch)
~69 years of range; makes IDs time-sortable




10
machine/worker ID
1024 distinct nodes




12
sequence number
4096 IDs per ms per node






    

  • Throughput: 4096 IDs/ms/node = ~4M IDs/s/node; ×1024 nodes = billions/s.
    • 

  • Sortable: high bits are time → IDs generated later are numerically larger (k-sorted, modulo clock skew across nodes).
    • 

  • No coordination: each node generates locally using its own clock + worker ID + a per-ms counter. Worker IDs are assigned at startup (via ZooKeeper/etcd or config).
    • 



Generation logic (per node): take current ms; if same ms as last, increment sequence; if sequence overflows 4096, busy-wait to next ms; if a new ms, reset sequence to 0. Compose the bits.


Deep dives — Snowflake


1. Clock skew / clock going backwards. Snowflake's correctness depends on a monotonic clock. If NTP rewinds the clock, you could reissue a timestamp → potential duplicates. Mitigations: refuse to generate (error/wait) while now < lastTimestamp; use a monotonic clock source; alert on large skew.


2. Worker ID assignment. 10 bits = 1024 workers. Assign via a coordination service (ZooKeeper sequential nodes) so no two live nodes share an ID. On restart, reclaim/reassign.


3. Epoch & lifetime. A custom epoch (not 1970) maximizes the usable 41-bit timestamp range → ~69 years from your epoch. Bit allocation is tunable: trade timestamp range for more nodes or more sequence headroom per your scale.


Consistent hashing — the companion concept


The problem it solves. To distribute K keys over N servers, naive hash(key) % N works until N changes — adding/removing one server changes the modulus and remaps almost every key, causing a massive cache-miss storm / data reshuffle.


The mechanism.
- Hash both keys and servers onto the same circular space (the ring, e.g. 0…2^32−1).
- A key is owned by the first server clockwise from the key's position.
- Add a server: only keys between it and its predecessor move — ~K/N keys, not all K.
- Remove a server: its keys move only to the next server clockwise.


So membership changes touch O(K/N) keys instead of O(K). This is why every Dynamo/Cassandra/memcached-cluster uses it.


Virtual nodes (the critical refinement). With few servers, random placement creates uneven load (one server may own a huge arc). Fix: map each physical server to many points on the ring (e.g. 100–200 vnodes each). This:
- evens out load (law of large numbers),
- smooths rebalancing (when a node leaves, its load spreads across many others, not one),
- supports heterogeneous capacity (give bigger machines more vnodes).


Bottlenecks & follow-ups


    

  • Snowflake duplicates only arise from clock issues or duplicate worker IDs — interviewers love probing this. Always mention monotonic-clock guard + coordinated worker IDs.
    • 

  • Sortability caveat: cross-node ordering is only approximate (clocks differ by ms) — IDs are k-sorted, not globally totally ordered.
    • 

  • Consistent hashing + replication: a key's N replicas are the next N distinct physical nodes clockwise (skip vnodes of the same physical node).
    • 

  • Interviewer probes: "Why is hash % N bad?" (full remap on resize), "Why vnodes?" (load balance + smooth rebalance + heterogeneity), "What breaks Snowflake?" (clock rollback), "UUID vs Snowflake?" (size + sortability), "How do you assign worker IDs safely?"
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Google Drive / Dropbox
1) Problem & Requirements


Build a file hosting + sync service: users upload/download files, files sync across all their devices, and folders can be shared.


Functional
- Upload/download files of arbitrary size (up to ~50 GB).
- Sync changes across all logged-in devices automatically.
- File versioning + restore.
- Share files/folders with read or write permissions.
- Offline edits that reconcile on reconnect.


Non-functional
- High durability (99.999999999% — eleven nines).
- High availability for reads; sync latency low (seconds).
- Bandwidth efficiency (only sync deltas, not whole files).
- Storage efficiency (dedup).
- Consistency: eventual is fine for sync, but no lost updates.


Scale (assumed)
- 500M users, 100M DAU.
- Avg 200 files/user, 100 KB–10 MB typical.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Total files
500M × 200 = 100B files




Avg file size
~500 KB → ~50 PB raw




After dedup (~30% savings)
~35 PB




With 3× replication
~105 PB




Daily uploads (100M DAU × 2 files × 500KB)
~100 TB/day




Write QPS (peak)
~10K–50K uploads/s




Metadata QPS
10× file QPS → ~500K/s






Metadata is small but hot; file bytes are huge but cold.


3) API Design


    

  • POST /files/upload-init → returns upload session + chunk list to upload
    • 

  • PUT /chunks/{hash} → upload one chunk (idempotent by content hash)
    • 

  • POST /files/commit {fileId, chunkHashes[], metadata} → assemble
    • 

  • GET /files/{id} → presigned download URL
    • 

  • GET /delta?cursor=X → list of changes since cursor (long-poll/websocket)
    • 

  • POST /share {fileId, userId, perm}
    • 



4) Data Model


Split metadata (SQL/strongly consistent) from blobs (object store).


    

  • Metadata DB (relational, e.g. MySQL/Spanner): users, files(id, owner, name, parent_dir, version, size, latest_revision), file_chunks(file_id, idx, chunk_hash), chunks(hash, ref_count, storage_url), shares. Relational because of strong consistency on namespace + permissions + transactional commits.
    • 

  • Block/Object store (S3-like): chunks keyed by content hash. NoSQL/object semantics — immutable, content-addressed.
    • 



Why this split: namespace operations (rename, move, permission checks) need transactions and joins; bytes need cheap, durable, infinitely scalable storage. Object stores already give 11-nines durability.


5) High-Level Architecture


Describe the diagram left-to-right: Client (with a watcher + chunker + local DB) → API/Block servers → Metadata service (SQL) + Chunk store (S3) with a Notification service pushing deltas back to clients.


Flow:
1. Client watcher detects a changed file, splits it into ~4 MB chunks, hashes each (e.g. SHA-256).
2. Client asks server which chunk hashes are missing (dedup check). Only missing chunks are PUT to the block store.
3. Client commits metadata: file → ordered list of chunk hashes + new version.
4. Metadata service writes the new revision transactionally and pushes a delta event to the Notification service.
5. Notification service (long-poll/WebSocket) tells the user's other devices: "file X changed." They pull the new chunk list and download only missing chunks.


Key components: Chunker (client), Dedup index (chunks table), Metadata DB, Block store, Notification/Sync service, CDN for popular downloads.


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) Chunking + dedup + delta sync. Fixed-size 4 MB chunks are simplest, but inserting a byte at the start shifts every chunk → poor dedup. Content-defined chunking (Rabin fingerprint) sets boundaries based on content, so an insertion only changes the local chunk → far better delta sync. Trade-off: more CPU on the client. Dedup is global (across users) via the content-addressed chunks table with ref_count; deletion decrements ref_count and GCs at zero. Security caveat: cross-user dedup can leak existence-of-file info, so many designs scope dedup per-user or use convergent encryption.


(b) Metadata scaling. 100B files → shard the metadata DB by user_id (keeps a user's whole namespace co-located, so listing a folder is a single-shard query). Hot-share folders (one folder shared with 1000s) become cross-shard — handle via a separate share-index. Use a cursor/version vector per user for delta sync so a reconnecting client fetches exactly what it missed.


(c) Sync conflicts. Two devices edit offline. On reconnect, last-writer-wins loses data; instead keep both versions (Dropbox's conflicted copy) and surface to user, since automatic 3-way merge of binary files is unsafe. Versioning makes this cheap (revisions are just different chunk lists).


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • Notification fan-out to millions of idle devices: use long-poll + a lightweight presence service; only wake devices with pending deltas.
    • 

  • Hot chunks / popular shared files: front with CDN and cache presigned URLs.
    • 

  • Metadata DB write hotspots: batch commits; consider Spanner/CockroachDB for global transactions.
    • 

  • GC of unreferenced chunks: background ref-count sweep; tombstone + delayed delete to avoid races with in-flight commits.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: encryption at rest + convergent encryption, large-file resumable uploads, mobile bandwidth caps, trash/retention policy.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Web Crawler
1) Problem & Requirements


Build a scalable web crawler that fetches web pages starting from seed URLs, extracts links, and stores content for downstream indexing (search engine).


Functional
- Crawl billions of pages from seed URLs.
- Extract and enqueue new links (graph traversal).
- Store page content for indexing.
- Respect robots.txt and politeness (rate-limit per host).
- Recrawl pages periodically (freshness).
- Avoid duplicate content and crawler traps.


Non-functional
- Scalable to billions of pages.
- Polite: don't hammer a single domain.
- Robust: handle malformed HTML, timeouts, redirects, infinite spaces.
- Extensible: pluggable parsers (HTML, PDF, images).


Scale (assumed)
- Crawl 1B pages/month.
- Avg page 100 KB compressed → ~50 KB.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Pages/month
1B




Pages/sec (avg)
1B / 2.6M s ≈ ~400 pages/s




Peak QPS
~4000 fetches/s




Storage/page (compressed)
~50 KB




Monthly storage
1B × 50 KB = 50 TB/month




URL frontier size
tens of billions of URLs (URL ~100 bytes → TBs)






Bandwidth: 400 × 100 KB ≈ 40 MB/s sustained download.


3) API / Interfaces


Mostly internal:
- frontier.add(url, priority) / frontier.next() → url
- fetcher.fetch(url) → {status, html, headers}
- parser.extract(html) → {links[], text, metadata}
- seen.contains(urlHash) / seen.add(urlHash)
- content.store(url, html)


4) Data Model


    

  • URL Frontier: a queue system (NoSQL / message-queue backed). Needs prioritization + per-host politeness, so it's a set of queues, not a single table.
    • 

  • Seen-URLs set: a huge set of URL hashes — use a bloom filter (memory) + backing key-value store (NoSQL like RocksDB/Cassandra) for membership.
    • 

  • Content store: object store (S3) or distributed FS for raw HTML, keyed by URL hash; metadata (last-crawled, etag, content-hash) in a wide-column NoSQL store.
    • 

  • Document dedup index: store content fingerprint (SimHash/MinHash) to detect near-duplicate pages.
    • 



NoSQL chosen because workload is huge-volume, key-based, append-heavy, with no need for joins or transactions.


5) High-Level Architecture


Describe the diagram as a loop: URL Frontier → Fetchers → Parser → Link Extractor → (back into Frontier), with Seen-set and Content store as side stores.


Flow:
1. Frontier hands out URLs respecting politeness (per-host queues + delay).
2. DNS resolver (cached — DNS is a real bottleneck) resolves host.
3. Fetcher workers download the page (honoring robots.txt, cached per host).
4. Content-seen check: hash the body; if duplicate content, skip storage.
5. Parser extracts links + text; raw HTML → content store.
6. URL-seen filter (bloom filter) drops already-known URLs; new ones are normalized and pushed back to the frontier with a recrawl priority.


Components: DNS cache, robots.txt cache, Frontier (priority + politeness queues), distributed fetcher fleet, parser/extractor, dedup (URL + content), storage.


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) URL Frontier design (politeness + priority). The classic Mercator design: a front set of queues by priority (important/fresh pages first) feeds a back set of queues, one per host. A per-host queue guarantees we never have two simultaneous requests to the same domain. A heap of (next-fetch-time, host) schedules which back-queue is ready. Trade-off: more queues = more memory, but prevents getting IP-banned. The frontier is distributed by hashing the hostname (not the full URL) to a shard — this keeps all of one domain's URLs on one node so politeness is enforceable locally.


(b) Duplicate detection at two levels. URL-level: normalize (lowercase host, strip fragments, sort query params) then bloom-filter membership — bloom gives O(1) memory-efficient probabilistic dedup; false positives just mean we skip a few real pages (acceptable). Content-level: many URLs serve identical/near-identical content. Use SimHash to fingerprint and detect near-duplicates within a Hamming distance, avoiding re-indexing the same article on 50 mirror sites.


(c) Crawler traps & freshness. Infinite calendars / session-id URLs create unbounded spaces — defend with max-depth, max-URLs-per-domain, and pattern detection. Freshness: recrawl frequency adaptive to observed change rate (news daily, static pages monthly); store etag/last-modified and send conditional GETs to save bandwidth.


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • DNS is a top bottleneck — run a dedicated caching DNS layer.
    • 

  • Politeness vs throughput tension — sharding by host balances it.
    • 

  • Frontier size grows unbounded — prioritize and cap; drop low-value URLs.
    • 

  • Distributed coordination — multiple crawler nodes must not double-crawl; partition by host hash so each URL has one owner.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: JavaScript-rendered pages (headless browser fleet), sitemap.xml ingestion, focused/topical crawling, spam/SEO-trap filtering.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Search Autocomplete / Typeahead
1) Problem & Requirements


As a user types a prefix, return the top-k most-relevant query completions (like Google search box).


Functional
- Given a prefix, return top 5–10 suggestions ranked by popularity (and possibly personalization).
- Update suggestions as new queries trend.
- Sub-100ms response (it fires on every keystroke).


Non-functional
- Very low latency (the hard constraint) — must feel instant.
- High read QPS (every keystroke is a request).
- Eventually-consistent freshness is fine (a trending term can appear minutes later).
- High availability; spell-tolerance is a bonus.


Scale (assumed)
- 5B searches/day; ~10% unique.
- Each search = ~4–6 keystrokes → autocomplete QPS ≈ 4–6× search QPS.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Searches/day
5B → ~60K searches/s avg




Keystrokes (autocomplete QPS)
~300K/s avg, ~1M/s peak




Unique top queries to index
~100M phrases




Trie storage
100M phrases × ~30 bytes ≈ few GB (fits in memory, sharded)






The key insight: read-heavy, latency-critical → precompute + serve from memory.


3) API Design


    

  • GET /autocomplete?q={prefix}&limit=10 → [{suggestion, score}, …]
    • 

  • Backend ingestion: POST /log-query {query, ts, userId} (async, sampled).
    • 



Client optimizations: debounce (~50–100ms), cancel in-flight requests, client-side cache of recent prefixes, request only after 2+ chars.


4) Data Model


The core structure is a Trie (prefix tree), where each node stores the top-k completions for the prefix ending at that node (precomputed and cached on the node). This avoids walking the whole subtree at query time.


    

  • Serving store: in-memory sharded tries (NoSQL-ish, custom). Persisted snapshots to disk/object store for recovery.
    • 

  • Aggregation store: raw query logs in a data warehouse / wide-column store, aggregated into (query, count, last_window) by an offline pipeline.
    • 



Why not SQL for serving: a per-keystroke prefix scan + ranking in SQL can't hit sub-ms; an in-memory trie with cached top-k per node can. SQL/warehouse is fine for the offline aggregation half.


5) High-Level Architecture


Two planes — describe the diagram as a top serving path and a bottom data-collection path.


Serving path: Client → Load balancer → Autocomplete servers (hold trie shards in memory) → return top-k. A CDN/edge cache can serve very common prefixes.


Data-collection path: Query logs → message queue (Kafka) → stream/batch aggregator (counts queries over a sliding time window) → ranker → Trie builder → publishes new trie snapshot → serving servers hot-swap the trie.


Components: Aggregator (count-min sketch for hot terms), Trie builder (offline job), Trie serving fleet (memory), snapshot store, edge cache.


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) Precompute top-k per node. Naively, for prefix "ca", you'd DFS the whole subtree and rank — too slow. Instead, each trie node caches its top-k completions, computed offline. Query = walk down the prefix (O(prefix length)) then return the cached list at the terminal node — O(1) after the walk. Trade-off: storage blows up (each node stores k phrases) and updates are expensive (a new popular query may need top-k recompute up its whole ancestor path). Accept staleness: rebuild snapshots periodically rather than live.


(b) Sharding the trie. 100M phrases > one box. Shard by first letter / first 1–2 chars — but "s" is far bigger than "z", causing hotspots. Better: shard by a hash of the prefix or use a mapping service that assigns prefix ranges to shards by load. A lightweight router resolves prefix → shard. Replicate hot shards for read scaling.


(c) Freshness via streaming aggregation. Counting every query exactly is expensive; use a count-min sketch to approximate query frequencies in bounded memory, plus a sliding window so trending terms surface and stale ones decay. The offline trie rebuild runs every N minutes and is atomically swapped in. Trade-off: approximate counts vs memory; acceptable since ranking is heuristic anyway.


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • Tail latency on every keystroke — solve with in-memory tries, edge caching, client debounce.
    • 

  • Hot shards (popular first letters) — hash-shard + replicate.
    • 

  • Trie rebuild cost — incremental updates or periodic batch swaps.
    • 

  • Spell tolerance — add fuzzy matching (edit-distance / phonetic), heavier compute.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: personalization (blend global trie with per-user history), multi-language, demoting offensive/illegal suggestions (filter list), A/B ranking models.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Distributed Message Queue (Kafka)
1) Problem & Requirements


Build a durable, high-throughput distributed pub/sub log (Kafka-like): producers append messages to topics, consumers read them, with durability and ordering guarantees.


Functional
- Producers publish to topics; topics split into partitions.
- Consumers subscribe via consumer groups (each partition read by one consumer in a group → parallelism).
- Durable, replayable message log (retain N days).
- At-least-once delivery (with exactly-once option).
- Ordering within a partition.


Non-functional
- Very high throughput (millions msgs/s).
- Horizontally scalable.
- Durable (no message loss on broker crash).
- Low end-to-end latency.
- Fault-tolerant (broker failure tolerated).


Scale (assumed)
- 1M messages/s, avg 1 KB → 1 GB/s ingest.
- 7-day retention.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Throughput
1M msg/s × 1 KB = 1 GB/s




Daily volume
~86 TB/day




7-day retention
~600 TB




With 3× replication
~1.8 PB




Partitions (target ~10 MB/s each)
~100+ partitions




Brokers (each ~10 partitions, NIC-bound)
tens of brokers






3) API Design


    

  • produce(topic, key, value) → broker appends to partition hash(key) % numPartitions, returns (partition, offset).
    • 

  • consume(topic, group, partition) → batch of (offset, value); consumer commits offset.
    • 

  • commitOffset(group, partition, offset).
    • 

  • Admin: createTopic(name, partitions, replicationFactor).
    • 



4) Data Model


The core data structure is an append-only commit log per partition — an ordered, immutable sequence of messages each with a monotonic offset. This is neither classic SQL nor a document store; it's a segmented log on disk.


    

  • Partition log: stored as segment files; sequential writes = cheap and fast (exploits OS page cache + sequential disk I/O).
    • 

  • Offsets: consumers track their own read position; the broker is dumb (pull model) — no per-message ack state to maintain.
    •
