  • Metadata (topics, partitions, leader assignments, ISR): in a coordination service (ZooKeeper / KRaft / etcd) — strongly consistent.
    • 



Why a log, not a DB: appends are O(1) sequential I/O, reads are sequential scans, and consumers replay by seeking an offset. No random reads/writes → enormous throughput.


5) High-Level Architecture


Describe the diagram: Producers → Brokers (each holding partition leaders + follower replicas) → Consumers (grouped), with a Coordination service off to the side managing metadata and a Controller broker handling leader election.


Flow:
1. Producer hashes the message key → partition; looks up that partition's leader broker (from metadata) and sends the batch.
2. Leader appends to its log, replicates to follower replicas (ISR — in-sync replicas). Producer ack policy: acks=all waits for ISR replication (durable) vs acks=1 (fast, riskier).
3. Consumers in a group are each assigned a subset of partitions (one partition → one consumer per group) and pull batches from the leader, tracking offsets.
4. On broker failure, the controller elects a new leader from the ISR for affected partitions.


Components: Brokers, Partition replicas (leader/follower), Controller, Coordination/metadata store, Consumer-group coordinator (rebalancing).


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) Partitioning = the scaling unit. A partition is the unit of parallelism and ordering. More partitions = more throughput + more consumers, but ordering is only guaranteed within a partition, and too many partitions raise leader-election + open-file-handle costs. Choosing the partition key matters: hash by userId keeps a user's events ordered while spreading load. Trade-off: ordering scope vs parallelism — a fundamental tension.


(b) Replication & durability (ISR). Each partition has 1 leader + N followers. Writes are acked once all in-sync replicas persist. If a follower lags, it's removed from ISR so it can't be elected (which would lose data). On leader crash, a new leader comes only from ISR → no committed-message loss. Trade-off: min.insync.replicas high = safer but a single replica outage blocks writes; tune per topic.


(c) Delivery semantics. At-least-once: consumer processes then commits offset — a crash before commit causes reprocessing (duplicates). At-most-once: commit before processing — risks loss. Exactly-once: needs idempotent producers (dedup by producer-id + sequence number) + transactional writes (atomic produce+offset-commit). Trade-off: exactly-once adds latency and complexity; many systems prefer at-least-once + idempotent consumers (dedup downstream).


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • Consumer-group rebalancing storms when consumers join/leave — use cooperative/incremental rebalancing.
    • 

  • Hot partition if a key is skewed — repartition or add a salt to the key.
    • 

  • Disk retention — segment-based deletion + optional log compaction (keep latest value per key).
    • 

  • Zero-copy (sendfile) from page cache to socket avoids userspace copies — key to throughput.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: tiered storage (offload cold segments to S3), geo-replication, schema registry, backpressure handling.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Ticketmaster (Reservation / Limited Inventory)
1) Problem & Requirements


Build a ticketing/reservation system for events with limited, fixed inventory (seats), where huge crowds compete for the same seats at sale-open time. The crux is concurrency on scarce inventory without overselling.


Functional
- Browse events and available seats.
- Reserve a specific seat (hold it temporarily while paying).
- Purchase (confirm) within a hold window; release if it expires.
- No double-booking / no overselling — ever.


Non-functional
- Strong consistency on inventory (correctness > availability here).
- Survive massive traffic spikes (a hot concert sells out in minutes).
- Fair-ish access; low latency for browse.
- Reads (browse) far outnumber writes (reserve/buy).


Scale (assumed)
- A hot event: 50K seats, 5M people trying to buy at t=0.
- Browse QPS in the hundreds of thousands; reservation writes in the thousands/s.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Concurrent users at drop
~5M




Seats
50K (the scarce resource)




Browse/availability QPS (peak)
~500K/s (read-heavy)




Reservation write QPS
~5–10K/s




Contention ratio
100:1 buyers per seat






The asymmetry (millions reading, thousands writing, tiny inventory) drives the design: scale reads with caches/CDN, serialize writes carefully.


3) API Design


    

  • GET /events/{id}/seats → seat map + availability (cached).
    • 

  • POST /reserve {eventId, seatIds[], userId} → creates a temporary hold with TTL (e.g. 10 min), returns holdId. Atomic.
    • 

  • POST /purchase {holdId, paymentToken} → confirms, triggers payment, marks BOOKED.
    • 

  • DELETE /reserve/{holdId} → release early.
    • 



4) Data Model


Inventory correctness demands ACID transactions → SQL (relational).


    

  • events(id, venue, start, status)
    • 

  • seats(id, event_id, section, row, num, status[AVAILABLE|HELD|BOOKED], hold_id, hold_expires_at, version)
    • 

  • bookings(id, user_id, event_id, seat_ids, payment_status)
    • 



Why SQL/NoSQL split: the seats table needs row-level locking + transactions to prevent overselling — relational engines do this natively. Browse/seat-map data can be denormalized into a cache (Redis) for read scaling. NoSQL alone struggles with the strict no-oversell invariant under contention.


5) High-Level Architecture


Describe the diagram: Clients → CDN/edge (static seat maps) → API gateway → Booking service → Inventory DB (SQL) + Redis (holds/locks), with a virtual waiting room in front and a payment service + expiry worker behind.


Flow:
1. At drop, a virtual waiting room / queue admits users in controlled batches (token-bucket / queue position), shielding the DB from the full 5M stampede.
2. Admitted user views seat map (served from cache).
3. Reserve: booking service transactionally flips chosen seats AVAILABLE → HELD with a TTL, conditioned on current status (optimistic version check). Conflicting requests fail and retry/pick other seats.
4. Purchase: payment processed; on success HELD → BOOKED. On failure or TTL expiry, an expiry worker flips HELD → AVAILABLE.


Components: Waiting room, Booking/Inventory service, SQL inventory DB, Redis (distributed locks + hold TTLs), Payment service, Hold-expiry sweeper, Read cache/CDN.


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) Preventing oversell — the core problem. Two safe approaches: (i) Pessimistic DB locking — SELECT … FOR UPDATE the seat rows inside a transaction, flip status, commit. Simple and correct but locks contend under heavy load. (ii) Optimistic concurrency — read seat version, update WHERE status='AVAILABLE' AND version=?; if 0 rows affected, someone else won → retry. Optimistic scales better when most attempts target different seats; pessimistic is safer when everyone wants the same seat. Either way the DB is the single source of truth — never trust a cache for the final commit.


(b) Temporary holds + expiry. A reservation must hold the seat while the user pays, but a crashed/abandoned checkout can't lock a seat forever. Use a TTL on the hold (stored as hold_expires_at + a Redis key with expiry). A background sweeper (or lazy check on read) releases expired holds. Trade-off: short TTL frees inventory fast but frustrates slow payers; ~5–10 min is typical. Redis TTL gives fast expiry signaling without polling the SQL DB.


(c) Surviving the stampede (waiting room). 5M simultaneous reserve attempts would melt the DB. A virtual waiting room issues queue tokens and admits a steady, DB-sized rate (e.g. only as many as inventory + churn warrants). This converts a spike into a manageable stream and also provides fairness/transparency ("you are #12,043 in line"). Trade-off: users wait, but the system stays correct and up.


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • DB write hotspot on one hot event — partition by event; the popular event may need its own dedicated shard/replica set.
    • 

  • Cache staleness on seat map — show "availability approximate"; confirm authoritatively at reserve time.
    • 

  • Payment failures / partial state — saga pattern: hold → charge → confirm, with compensating release on failure.
    • 

  • Bots / scalpers — waiting room + rate limits + CAPTCHA + per-user caps.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: idempotency keys on purchase (avoid double-charge on retry), best-available auto-assign vs pick-your-seat, surge read replicas.
    • 



← System Design · HLD · Storage & Infra
Google Maps / Proximity Service
1) Problem & Requirements


Build a proximity service: given a user's location, return nearby places (restaurants, gas stations) within a radius, ranked. (Focus on geo-search; routing/ETA is a related extension.)


Functional
- searchNearby(lat, lng, radius) → list of places sorted by distance/rating.
- Add/update/remove a place.
- Optionally filter by category.


Non-functional
- Low latency (< 200 ms) for nearby queries.
- Read-heavy (searches ≫ place updates).
- Scalable to hundreds of millions of places.
- Eventually consistent on place data is fine.


Scale (assumed)
- 200M places worldwide.
- 5B searches/day; ~100M DAU.


2) Capacity Estimation






Metric
Estimate






Places
200M




Search QPS
5B/day ≈ ~60K/s avg, ~200K/s peak




Place writes
low, thousands/s




Storage/place (~1 KB)
~200 GB (fits sharded)




Per-query candidate set
nearby grid cells → tens–hundreds of places






Read-dominated → heavy caching + a spatial index that prunes candidates fast.


3) API Design


    

  • GET /search?lat=..&lng=..&radius=..&category=..&limit=20 → ranked places
    • 

  • POST /places {name, lat, lng, category, …}
    • 

  • PUT /places/{id} / DELETE /places/{id}
    • 



4) Data Model


Two stores:
- Place details (name, address, rating, hours): SQL or document store keyed by place_id. Relational is fine; details are read by id after the spatial filter.
- Spatial index: maps a geo-cell → list of place_ids. Stored in a table/KV: (geohash_prefix, place_id). This is the workhorse for "who's near me."


The key design choice is the geo-index encoding. Two common ones: Geohash (interleave lat/lng bits into a base-32 string; shared prefix ⇒ spatial proximity) or Quadtree (recursively subdivide space into 4 quadrants until a node has < N places). Both turn a 2-D range query into a 1-D prefix/tree lookup that a normal index can serve.


5) High-Level Architecture


Describe the diagram: Client → LB → Geo-search service → (Geo-index store + Place-details store) with a Redis cache in front of hot cells and an ingestion path that geo-encodes new places.


Flow:
1. A new place is geo-encoded (compute its geohash / locate its quadtree leaf) and inserted into the spatial index alongside its detail row.
2. A searchNearby request computes the query point's geohash and reads that cell plus its 8 neighbor cells (because the point may sit near a cell boundary — neighbors avoid missing close places across the edge).
3. Candidate place_ids are fetched, their details looked up (batched), exact distance computed (Haversine), filtered to the radius, ranked (distance + rating), and returned.
4. Hot cells (dense city centers) are cached in Redis.


Components: Geo-encoder, Geo-index store (sharded by cell), Place-details store, search/ranking service, Redis cache.


6) Deep Dives & Scaling


(a) Geohash vs Quadtree (the central trade-off). Geohash is dead simple — a string prefix; you can store it in any DB and do prefix range scans, and adjusting precision (string length) changes cell size. Downside: fixed grid → dense areas (downtown) overload one cell while rural cells are nearly empty, and the boundary problem requires querying neighbor cells. Quadtree adapts to density — it subdivides only where places are dense, so each leaf holds a bounded count; great for skew. Downside: it's an in-memory tree that's harder to distribute/persist and rebuild. Many production systems use geohash (or Google's S2 cells, which map the sphere to a space-filling curve and handle polar/wraparound distortion better than a flat lat/lng grid).


(b) Variable radius / boundary correctness. A fixed-precision geohash gives one cell size, but queries have different radii. Approach: pick a geohash precision whose cell is ≥ the query radius, then always query the center cell + 8 neighbors and post-filter by exact Haversine distance. For very large radii, drop to a coarser precision. This guarantees we don't miss a place that's geographically close but lands in an adjacent cell.


(c) Hotspots & sharding. Place density is wildly uneven (NYC vs desert). If you shard the geo-index by geohash prefix, dense prefixes become hotspots. Mitigations: replicate hot cells, cache them in Redis, and/or use the quadtree's natural load-balancing (bounded leaf size). Reads dominate, so aggressive caching of popular city-center cells absorbs most traffic.


7) Bottlenecks & Follow-ups


    

  • Dense-cell hotspots — quadtree adaptive cells, Redis caching, replication.
    • 

  • Boundary misses — always include neighbor cells + exact-distance post-filter.
    • 

  • Place updates / moving entities (e.g. nearby drivers in Uber) — for moving objects, re-index on cell change; high write churn favors in-memory quadtrees per region.
    • 

  • Ranking beyond distance — blend rating, popularity, personalization → heavier scoring service.
    • 

  • Follow-ups: routing/ETA (separate graph + Dijkstra/contraction hierarchies), real-time traffic, multi-region replication, S2/H3 hierarchical indexing.
    • 



← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Parking Lot
Requirements & clarifying questions


Before coding, pin down scope. Ask: How many entry/exit gates? Multiple floors? What vehicle types (motorcycle, car, truck/bus)? Is the spot-to-vehicle mapping strict (a truck needs a large spot) or flexible (smaller vehicles can fit larger spots)? How is fare computed — flat, hourly, or tiered by vehicle type? Payment methods (cash, card, UPI)? Do we need to find the nearest free spot, or any free spot? Single building or multiple lots? Concurrency — many cars entering at once?


A reasonable in-scope set: multi-floor lot, three vehicle/spot sizes, hourly pricing that varies by vehicle type, ticket issued at entry and paid at exit, and an algorithm to find the best available spot. Out of scope: reservations, license-plate recognition, distributed multi-lot.


Core entities / classes


    

  • Vehicle (abstract): holds licensePlate and a VehicleType. Subclasses Car, Motorcycle, Truck.
    • 

  • ParkingSpot (abstract): spotId, SpotType, isFree, the parked Vehicle. canFit(vehicle) decides eligibility.
    • 

  • ParkingFloor: owns a collection of spots, exposes getFreeSpot(type), and tracks per-type availability counts for fast lookup.
    • 

  • ParkingLot (Singleton): aggregates floors, gates, and orchestrates entry/exit.
    • 

  • Ticket: ticketId, spot, entryTime, vehicle.
    • 

  • EntryGate / ExitGate: front doors that issue and close tickets.
    • 

  • ParkingStrategy (interface): picks a spot — NearestFirst, RandomFree, etc.
    • 

  • PricingStrategy (interface): computes fare from ticket and exit time.
    • 

  • Payment: amount, status, PaymentStrategy (cash/card).
    • 



Relationships


ParkingLot composes ParkingFloors (floors don't outlive the lot); each ParkingFloor composes ParkingSpots. A ParkingSpot associates with a Vehicle when occupied (the vehicle lives independently). Ticket associates a Vehicle with a ParkingSpot. Car/Truck/Motorcycle inherit from Vehicle; concrete spots inherit from ParkingSpot. ParkingLot uses a ParkingStrategy and PricingStrategy (association via interface), enabling runtime swap.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Singleton for ParkingLot — one physical lot, one global coordinator for gate operations and counts.
    • 

  • Strategy for spot selection and pricing — pricing rules and "which spot" policy change often and independently; isolating them behind interfaces lets us swap without touching ParkingLot.
    • 

  • Factory (VehicleFactory / SpotFactory) to centralize object creation and keep gates ignorant of concrete classes.
    • 

  • Observer (optional) for a display board that subscribes to availability changes.
    • 



Strategy is the workhorse here: pricing is a textbook example of behavior that varies (weekend surge, EV discount) and shouldn't be baked into entities.


Key code


enum class VehicleType { MOTORCYCLE, CAR, TRUCK };
enum class SpotType   { SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE };

class Vehicle {
public:
    Vehicle(std::string plate, VehicleType type);
    VehicleType getType() const;
    std::string getPlate() const;
protected:
    std::string licensePlate;
    VehicleType type;
};

class ParkingSpot {
public:
    virtual bool canFit(const Vehicle& vehicle) const = 0;
    bool isFree() const;
    void assign(std::shared_ptr<Vehicle> vehicle);
    void release();
    SpotType getType() const;
protected:
    std::string spotId;
    SpotType type;
    std::shared_ptr<Vehicle> parked;
};

class PricingStrategy {
public:
    virtual double computeFare(const Ticket& ticket,
                               std::time_t exitTime) const = 0;
    virtual ~PricingStrategy() = default;
};

class ParkingStrategy {
public:
    virtual std::shared_ptr<ParkingSpot>
        findSpot(std::vector<ParkingFloor>& floors,
                 const Vehicle& vehicle) = 0;
    virtual ~ParkingStrategy() = default;
};

class ParkingLot {
public:
    static ParkingLot& instance();
    Ticket parkVehicle(std::shared_ptr<Vehicle> vehicle);
    double unparkVehicle(const std::string& ticketId);
    void setPricing(std::unique_ptr<PricingStrategy> strategy);
private:
    ParkingLot() = default;
    std::vector<ParkingFloor> floors;
    std::unique_ptr<ParkingStrategy> spotPicker;
    std::unique_ptr<PricingStrategy> pricing;
    std::unordered_map<std::string, Ticket> activeTickets;
    std::mutex lotMutex;
};




Extensibility & SOLID


The design honors Open/Closed: a new VehicleType::BUS plus a BusSpot and updated factory adds support without editing existing classes; a weekend SurgePricingStrategy slots in via setPricing. Single Responsibility is respected — pricing, spot selection, and lifecycle (lot) are separate. Dependency Inversion holds because ParkingLot depends on the PricingStrategy/ParkingStrategy abstractions, not concretes. Liskov holds since any ParkingSpot subtype is interchangeable through canFit. For concurrency, lotMutex (or per-floor locks for finer granularity) guards the find-and-assign critical section so two cars never grab the same spot. To scale reads, maintain atomic per-type free counters so the display board and availability checks avoid scanning. Multiple lots would generalize the Singleton into a registry keyed by lot id.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Elevator System
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: How many elevators and floors? Are there separate hall calls (up/down buttons in the lobby) and car calls (floor buttons inside)? What scheduling goal — minimize wait time, minimize travel, or fairness? Do we support express/zoned elevators, capacity limits, door sensors, emergency/maintenance modes? Is this single-threaded simulation or real concurrent control? Should idle elevators "park" at a home floor?


In scope: N elevators serving M floors; hall and car calls; a dispatcher that assigns the best car using the classic SCAN/LOOK (elevator) algorithm — keep moving in one direction servicing requests, then reverse. Each car tracks direction and a sorted set of target floors. Out of scope: physics of acceleration, mechanical faults.


Core entities / classes


    

  • Elevator (ElevatorCar): id, currentFloor, Direction (UP/DOWN/IDLE), ElevatorState, upStops and downStops (sorted sets), capacity. Methods addStop, step() (advance one floor), openDoor.
    • 

  • Request: a hall call (floor + desired direction) or a car call (target floor). Modeled as HallRequest / CarRequest.
    • 

  • ElevatorState (interface): Idle, Moving, DoorsOpen, Maintenance — encapsulates per-state behavior and legal transitions.
    • 

  • Dispatcher / ElevatorController: receives requests, chooses an elevator via a SchedulingStrategy, and drives the simulation clock.
    • 

  • SchedulingStrategy (interface): assign(elevators, request) returns the chosen car.
    • 

  • Button / Panel: HallPanel per floor, CarPanel per elevator — generate requests (Command-style).
    • 

  • Display: observer of a car's floor/direction.
    • 



Relationships


ElevatorController composes the Elevator fleet and uses a SchedulingStrategy (association via interface). Each Elevator has-a current ElevatorState (composition; state objects are swapped as it transitions). Panels create Request objects and hand them to the controller. Display observes Elevator. Concrete states inherit from ElevatorState; concrete strategies inherit from SchedulingStrategy.


Design patterns applied


    

  • State for elevator behavior — an elevator behaves very differently when Idle vs Moving vs DoorsOpen vs Maintenance, and transitions are rule-bound. State pattern removes giant if (state == ...) switches and localizes transition logic.
    • 

  • Strategy for scheduling — "nearest car," "least busy," or "zoned" dispatch policies are interchangeable; the choice is a tuning knob, not a structural change.
    • 

  • Command for button presses — each press is a request object that can be queued, logged, and replayed.
    • 

  • Observer for displays/indicators reacting to car movement.
    • 

  • Singleton (optional) for the controller as the single coordination point.
    • 



Key code


enum class Direction { UP, DOWN, IDLE };

class ElevatorState {
public:
    virtual void onStep(Elevator& car) = 0;
    virtual void onArrive(Elevator& car) = 0;
    virtual std::string name() const = 0;
    virtual ~ElevatorState() = default;
};

class Elevator {
public:
    void addStop(int floor, Direction dir);
    void step();
    void setState(std::unique_ptr<ElevatorState> next);
    int getFloor() const;
    Direction getDirection() const;
private:
    int id;
    int currentFloor;
    Direction direction;
    std::unique_ptr<ElevatorState> state;
    std::set<int> upStops;
    std::set<int, std::greater<int>> downStops;
};

class SchedulingStrategy {
public:
    virtual Elevator& assign(std::vector<Elevator>& fleet,
                             const Request& request) = 0;
    virtual ~SchedulingStrategy() = default;
};

class ElevatorController {
public:
    void submitHallCall(int floor, Direction dir);
    void submitCarCall(int elevatorId, int floor);
    void tick();
private:
    std::vector<Elevator> fleet;
    std::unique_ptr<SchedulingStrategy> scheduler;
};




Extensibility & SOLID


New scheduling policies (energy-saving zoning, VIP priority) implement SchedulingStrategy without touching Elevator or ElevatorController — Open/Closed and Dependency Inversion via the strategy interface. New behaviors like EmergencyState or FireRecallState plug into the State machine without editing other states — each state owns only its own transitions (Single Responsibility). Direction-aware upStops/downStops sorted sets make the SCAN algorithm O(log n) per insert and give the next target in O(1) at the set boundary. For real concurrency, the controller's request queue is a thread-safe producer/consumer: panels (producers) push commands, the dispatch loop (consumer) pops and assigns, with each elevator advanced on a tick. The Command objects make the queue trivially loggable and testable, and capacity/door-sensor checks live inside the relevant state, keeping the fleet logic clean.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Movie Ticket Booking (BookMyShow)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: Scope of catalog (cities → cinemas → screens → shows)? Seat selection by the user, or auto-assign? Crucially — how do we prevent two users booking the same seat? What's the hold/lock timeout while a user pays? Payment integration, refunds, cancellations? Seat categories/pricing (recliner vs normal)? Do we need waitlists? Single server or distributed?


The heart of this question is seat locking and concurrency: when a user picks seats, we must atomically reserve them for a short TTL (say 5–10 minutes) so others see them as unavailable; on payment success they convert to BOOKED, on timeout/failure they auto-release. In scope: catalog model, seat lifecycle, a lock manager with expiry, and idempotent payment confirmation. Out of scope: recommendation, dynamic surge pricing.


Core entities / classes


    

  • City → Cinema → Screen → Show: catalog hierarchy. A Show ties a Movie to a Screen at a startTime and owns the ShowSeats.
    • 

  • Seat: physical seat in a screen (row, number, SeatCategory).
    • 

  • ShowSeat: per-show seat state — AVAILABLE, LOCKED, BOOKED — plus price and the locking user/expiry.
    • 

  • SeatLockProvider (interface): lockSeats(show, seats, user), unlock, validateLock — backed by in-memory map + expiry or Redis with TTL.
    • 

  • Booking: bookingId, user, show, locked seats, BookingStatus (CREATED, CONFIRMED, EXPIRED, CANCELLED).
    • 

  • BookingService: orchestrates select → lock → pay → confirm.
    • 

  • Payment / PaymentStrategy: handles charge, returns success/failure.
    • 

  • User.
    • 



Relationships


Cinema composes Screens; Screen composes physical Seats and composes Shows. Each Show composes ShowSeats (one per physical seat, created at show creation). Booking associates a User with a set of ShowSeats of one Show. BookingService uses a SeatLockProvider and a PaymentStrategy (association via interfaces). ShowSeat references its physical Seat for layout/category.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Strategy for SeatLockProvider and PaymentStrategy — swap an in-memory lock manager for a distributed Redis-backed one without changing booking flow; swap payment gateways freely.
    • 

  • State for ShowSeat / Booking lifecycle — legal transitions (AVAILABLE→LOCKED→BOOKED, or LOCKED→AVAILABLE on expiry) are enforced in one place.
    • 

  • Singleton for the in-memory SeatLockProvider so all threads share one lock table.
    • 

  • Factory to build the ShowSeat set when a show is created.
    • 

  • Observer (optional) to notify a UI/cache when seat availability changes.
    • 



Key code


enum class SeatStatus { AVAILABLE, LOCKED, BOOKED };

class SeatLockProvider {
public:
    virtual void lockSeats(const std::string& showId,
                           const std::vector<std::string>& seatIds,
                           const std::string& userId) = 0;   // throws if any taken
    virtual void unlockSeats(const std::string& showId,
                             const std::vector<std::string>& seatIds,
                             const std::string& userId) = 0;
    virtual bool validateLock(const std::string& showId,
                              const std::string& seatId,
                              const std::string& userId) = 0;
    virtual ~SeatLockProvider() = default;
};

class InMemorySeatLockProvider : public SeatLockProvider {
public:
    void lockSeats(const std::string& showId,
                   const std::vector<std::string>& seatIds,
                   const std::string& userId) override;
private:
    struct Lock { std::string userId; std::time_t expiresAt; };
    std::unordered_map<std::string, Lock> locks;   // key: showId:seatId
    std::mutex mtx;
    int ttlSeconds = 600;
};

class BookingService {
public:
    Booking createBooking(const std::string& userId,
                          const std::string& showId,
                          const std::vector<std::string>& seatIds);
    Booking confirmBooking(const std::string& bookingId,
                           const PaymentRequest& payment);
private:
    std::shared_ptr<SeatLockProvider> lockProvider;
    std::unique_ptr<PaymentStrategy> payment;
    std::unordered_map<std::string, Booking> bookings;
};




Concurrency deep-dive & SOLID


The correctness crux is the check-and-lock atomicity. In lockSeats, acquire mtx, verify every requested seat is free (or held by the same user and unexpired), then write all locks in one critical section — never check-then-lock without the lock held, or two threads both pass the check. Locks carry an expiresAt; a lazy sweep on access (or a background reaper) reclaims expired holds back to AVAILABLE. confirmBooking first calls validateLock to ensure the hold is still valid (the user didn't dawdle past TTL), then charges, then flips seats to BOOKED — all idempotently keyed on bookingId so a retried payment webhook can't double-book.


Distributed scale swaps InMemorySeatLockProvider for a Redis implementation using SET key val NX PX ttl (atomic lock-if-absent with native TTL) — Dependency Inversion means BookingService doesn't change. Open/Closed lets new seat categories, pricing strategies, or a waitlist feature attach without editing the core flow. Single Responsibility: locking, payment, and catalog are distinct classes, so a bug in expiry logic stays contained to the lock provider.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Splitwise (Expense Sharing)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: What split types — equal, exact amounts, percentage, shares? Group expenses vs one-off between two people? Multi-currency? Do we need debt simplification (minimize the number of transactions to settle)? Settle-up flow and partial payments? Activity feed/notifications? Validation rules (splits must sum to the total)?


In scope: users, groups, expenses with pluggable split strategies, a balance sheet tracking who-owes-whom, and a simplify algorithm that reduces a tangle of debts to the minimum cash flow. Out of scope: currency conversion rates feed, receipts OCR.


Core entities / classes


    

  • User: userId, name, and (conceptually) a balance map.
    • 

  • Group: a set of users plus the expenses scoped to them.
    • 

  • Expense: amount, paidBy (who fronted the money), participants, and the chosen SplitStrategy. Produces a list of Splits.
    • 

  • Split: (user, amountOwed) — the resolved share for one participant.
    • 

  • SplitStrategy (interface): computeSplits(amount, participants, metadata) → vector<Split>. Implementations: EqualSplit, ExactSplit, PercentSplit, ShareSplit.
    • 

  • BalanceSheet / ExpenseManager: holds the global owes[from][to] = amount matrix; addExpense mutates it; getBalances(user) reads it.
    • 

  • DebtSimplifier: takes the net balance per user and emits the minimal set of settlement transactions.
    • 



Relationships


Group aggregates Users (users exist independently). Expense composes Splits and uses a SplitStrategy (association via interface); it references the payer and participant Users. ExpenseManager composes the balance matrix and uses a DebtSimplifier. Concrete split classes inherit from SplitStrategy.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Strategy for split computation — the split rule is the canonical varying behavior; EqualSplit vs PercentSplit differ only in how shares are derived. Encapsulating each as a strategy keeps Expense agnostic and validation localized (percentages sum to 100, exacts sum to total).
    • 

  • Factory to instantiate the right SplitStrategy from a split-type enum.
    • 

  • Observer for an activity feed — users subscribe to a group and get notified when an expense touching them is added.
    • 

  • Singleton (optional) for ExpenseManager as the single source of truth for balances.
    • 



Key code


struct Split { std::string userId; double amount; };

class SplitStrategy {
public:
    virtual std::vector<Split> computeSplits(
        double totalAmount,
        const std::vector<std::string>& participants,
        const std::vector<double>& metadata) const = 0;
    virtual ~SplitStrategy() = default;
};

class EqualSplit : public SplitStrategy {
public:
    std::vector<Split> computeSplits(
        double totalAmount,
        const std::vector<std::string>& participants,
        const std::vector<double>& metadata) const override;
};

class ExpenseManager {
public:
    void addExpense(const std::string& paidBy,
                    double amount,
                    const std::vector<std::string>& participants,
                    std::unique_ptr<SplitStrategy> strategy,
                    const std::vector<double>& metadata);
    double getNetBalance(const std::string& userId) const;
    std::vector<std::tuple<std::string,std::string,double>> simplify() const;
private:
    std::unordered_map<std::string,
        std::unordered_map<std::string,double>> owes;  // owes[from][to]
};

class DebtSimplifier {
public:
    std::vector<std::tuple<std::string,std::string,double>>
        minimizeCashFlow(
            const std::unordered_map<std::string,double>& netBalance) const;
};




Balance simplification deep-dive & SOLID


In addExpense, after the strategy returns splits, for each participant other than the payer we do owes[participant][payer] += share, then net out any reciprocal edge so we never store both A→B and B→A. The cleanest representation for settlement is the net balance per user: sum of (received − owed). Users with negative net are debtors, positive are creditors, and the totals always sum to zero.


DebtSimplifier::minimizeCashFlow solves minimum cash flow: repeatedly take the maximum creditor and maximum debtor, settle min(|debtor|, creditor) between them, and recurse. A max-heap implementation pairs the extremes each round; it is greedy and produces at most N−1 transactions, which is optimal for the common case (the exact minimum-transaction problem is NP-hard, but the greedy max-creditor/max-debtor heuristic is the standard interview answer and near-optimal). This keeps settle-up frictionless: instead of everyone paying everyone, the group converges in the fewest transfers.


SOLID: Open/Closed — a new split type (e.g. AdjustmentSplit) implements SplitStrategy and registers in the factory without editing Expense or ExpenseManager. Single Responsibility — splitting, balance bookkeeping, and simplification are three classes. Dependency Inversion — Expense depends on the SplitStrategy abstraction. Multi-currency would add a Money{amount, currency} value type and a conversion service injected into ExpenseManager, leaving the algorithm intact.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Vending Machine (State Pattern)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: Does it accept coins/notes, card, or both? Multiple products with per-slot inventory and price? Does it give change, and what if exact change isn't available? Can the user cancel and get a refund mid-transaction? What happens when a product is sold out or the machine is empty? Single-item or multi-item per session? Maintenance/restock mode?


This is the canonical State pattern question. The machine moves through well-defined states — Idle → HasMoney → Dispensing → (back to Idle) — and the same user action (insert coin, select product, dispense) means different things in different states. In scope: coin insertion, product selection with price checks, dispense, change return, cancel/refund, and a no-stock/insufficient-funds path. Out of scope: networked telemetry, dynamic pricing.


Core entities / classes


    

  • VendingMachine: the context. Holds inventory, current balance inserted, the selected product, and a pointer to the current VendingMachineState. Delegates every action to the state.
    • 

  • VendingMachineState (interface): one method per user action — insertMoney(amount), selectProduct(code), dispense(), cancel(). Each returns/throws appropriately and may transition the machine.
    • 

  • Concrete states: IdleState, HasMoneyState, DispensingState (and optionally OutOfServiceState).
    • 

  • Inventory / Slot: Slot{ product, price, count } indexed by product code; Inventory manages counts and isAvailable.
    • 

  • Product: code, name, price.
    • 

  • CoinDispenser / ChangeManager: tracks coin denominations and computes change.
    • 



Relationships


VendingMachine has-a current VendingMachineState (composition; states are swapped on transition) and composes an Inventory and a ChangeManager. Inventory composes Slots; each Slot references a Product. Concrete states inherit from VendingMachineState and hold a back-reference (association) to the VendingMachine so they can mutate balance/inventory and trigger transitions.


Design patterns applied


    

  • State (primary) — replaces a brittle switch(machineState) inside every method with discrete state classes. Each class implements only the actions legal in that state; illegal actions (e.g. dispense() while Idle) return a clear error from that one place. Adding a state doesn't touch the others.
    • 

  • Strategy (optional) for change-making (GreedyChange vs ExactChangeOnly).
    • 

  • Singleton for the machine instance (one physical unit).
    • 

  • Factory for creating state objects, or simply hold one instance of each state and switch pointers.
    • 



Key code


class VendingMachineState {
public:
    virtual void insertMoney(VendingMachine& m, int amount) = 0;
    virtual void selectProduct(VendingMachine& m, const std::string& code) = 0;
    virtual void dispense(VendingMachine& m) = 0;
    virtual void cancel(VendingMachine& m) = 0;
    virtual std::string name() const = 0;
    virtual ~VendingMachineState() = default;
};

class VendingMachine {
public:
    void setState(std::shared_ptr<VendingMachineState> next);
    void insertMoney(int amount);
    void selectProduct(const std::string& code);
    void dispense();
    void cancel();

    int  getBalance() const;
    void addBalance(int amount);
    void resetBalance();
    Inventory& inventory();
private:
    std::shared_ptr<VendingMachineState> state;
    int balance = 0;
    std::string selectedCode;
    Inventory inv;
};

class HasMoneyState : public VendingMachineState {
public:
    void insertMoney(VendingMachine& m, int amount) override;   // add to balance
    void selectProduct(VendingMachine& m, const std::string& code) override;
    void dispense(VendingMachine& m) override;                  // -> DispensingState
    void cancel(VendingMachine& m) override;                    // refund -> Idle
    std::string name() const override { return "HasMoney"; }
};




Extensibility & SOLID


The flow: in IdleState, insertMoney accrues balance and transitions to HasMoneyState; there selectProduct validates stock and that balance ≥ price, then dispense decrements inventory, returns change via ChangeManager, transitions to DispensingState and back to IdleState. cancel in HasMoneyState refunds and returns to Idle. Each state ignores or errors on actions that don't apply, so the context stays free of conditionals.


SOLID payoffs: Open/Closed — a MaintenanceState (restock, disable vending) or a card-payment AwaitingCardState is a new class; existing states are untouched. Single Responsibility — transition logic for a state lives only in that state; inventory and change-making are their own classes. Liskov — every state is a drop-in VendingMachineState. Dependency Inversion — the machine depends on the state interface, not concrete states. Swapping ChangeManager to ExactChangeOnly (refuse sale if change unavailable) is a Strategy substitution. This decomposition is exactly why the State pattern is the expected answer: behavior that varies by mode becomes polymorphism instead of nested conditionals.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
LRU Cache (Class Design)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: What capacity bound — fixed entry count or memory bytes? Must get and put both be O(1)? Is eviction strictly Least-Recently-Used, or do we want a pluggable policy (LRU/LFU/FIFO)? Thread-safe for concurrent access? Generic key/value types? TTL/expiry on entries? Eviction callbacks (e.g. write-back to a store)?


The classic requirement is a fixed-capacity cache with O(1) get and put and LRU eviction. In scope: generic <Key, Value>, a clean class API, the doubly-linked-list + hash-map design, and a note on thread safety and pluggable eviction. Out of scope: distributed cache, persistence.


Core entities / classes


    

  • Node: a doubly-linked-list node holding key, value, and prev/next pointers. Storing the key in the node lets eviction remove the map entry in O(1).
    • 

  • LRUCache: the public face — get(key), put(key, value). Internally owns a unordered_map<Key, Node*> for O(1) lookup and a doubly-linked list (with sentinel head/tail) ordering nodes from most- to least-recently-used.
    • 

  • EvictionPolicy (optional interface): to generalize beyond LRU — recordAccess(node), evictCandidate(). LRUPolicy, LFUPolicy, FIFOPolicy.
    • 

  • DoublyLinkedList (optional helper): encapsulates addFront, moveToFront, removeLast, hiding pointer surgery from the cache.
    • 



Relationships


LRUCache composes the hash map and the doubly-linked list (their lifetimes are owned by the cache). The map associates keys to Node* that live inside the list — the same node is referenced from both structures, which is the trick enabling O(1) on both paths. If using the policy abstraction, LRUCache uses an EvictionPolicy (association via interface) that manages recency ordering, decoupling "storage" from "which to evict."


Design patterns applied


    

  • Strategy for EvictionPolicy — LRU vs LFU vs FIFO is exactly interchangeable behavior; isolating it lets the same storage core serve any policy. This is the standard way to answer the "now make it LFU" follow-up.
    • 

  • Template Method / Generics — templating on <Key, Value> keeps it type-agnostic.
    • 

  • Facade — LRUCache presents a tiny two-method facade over the map+list internals.
    • 

  • Decorator (optional) — a ThreadSafeCache decorator wraps a base cache with a mutex, or a TTLCache decorator adds expiry, without modifying the core.
    • 



Key code


template <typename Key, typename Value>
class LRUCache {
public:
    explicit LRUCache(std::size_t capacity);
    std::optional<Value> get(const Key& key);
    void put(const Key& key, const Value& value);
    std::size_t size() const;
private:
    struct Node {
        Key key;
        Value value;
        Node* prev;
        Node* next;
    };
    void moveToFront(Node* node);
    void removeNode(Node* node);
    void addFront(Node* node);
    Node* evictLast();

    std::size_t capacity;
    std::unordered_map<Key, Node*> table;
    Node* head;   // sentinel: most-recently-used side
    Node* tail;   // sentinel: least-recently-used side
    std::mutex mtx;
};

template <typename Key, typename Value>
std::optional<Value> LRUCache<Key, Value>::get(const Key& key) {
    std::lock_guard<std::mutex> guard(mtx);
    auto it = table.find(key);
    if (it == table.end()) return std::nullopt;
    moveToFront(it->second);
    return it->second->value;
}




Why O(1) and SOLID


The hash map gives O(1) key→node lookup; the doubly-linked list gives O(1) reordering because, with both prev and next, splicing a node out and re-inserting it at the front touches only constant neighbors — no scan. On get, find the node and moveToFront. On put: if present, update value and move to front; if absent, create a node, addFront, insert into the map, and if over capacity, evictLast — read the key from the tail-adjacent node to erase its map entry, also O(1). Sentinel head/tail nodes eliminate null-checks at the boundaries, removing a whole class of pointer bugs.


Thread safety: a single std::mutex around get/put makes it correct under concurrency at the cost of contention; the Decorator approach keeps the lock-free core testable and wraps synchronization separately. For higher throughput one shards into N independent LRUCache segments keyed by hash(key) % N, each with its own lock.


SOLID: Single Responsibility — the list manages ordering, the map manages lookup, the cache coordinates. Open/Closed via the EvictionPolicy strategy — the "make it LFU" follow-up becomes a new policy class implementing recordAccess/evictCandidate (LFU adds a frequency count and a min-frequency pointer to stay O(1)), with the storage core untouched. Dependency Inversion — the cache depends on the policy abstraction. TTL and metrics attach as decorators, keeping each concern independent and the hot path lean.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Logging Framework (Chain of Responsibility)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: What severity levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL)? Multiple sinks (console, file, remote/HTTP) simultaneously, or routed by level? Configurable minimum level at runtime? Custom formatting/timestamps? Thread-safe for concurrent writers? Async logging to avoid blocking the caller? Log rotation? Should a message stop at the first handler or fan out to all matching ones?


The canonical framing uses Chain of Responsibility: a message enters at the head of a chain of handlers, each responsible for one severity threshold (or sink); a handler either processes the message (if its level matches) and/or passes it to the next. In scope: level-based routing through a handler chain, pluggable formatters and appenders, runtime level config. Out of scope: distributed log aggregation, query/search.


Core entities / classes


    

  • LogLevel (enum): DEBUG < INFO < WARN < ERROR < FATAL, ordered so comparisons gate output.
    • 

  • LogMessage: level, text, timestamp, threadId, optional context map.
    • 

  • LogHandler (abstract): the chain link. Holds a nextHandler and a level threshold. handle(message) decides whether to act and whether/when to forward. Concrete: DebugHandler, InfoHandler, ErrorHandler — or sink-oriented ConsoleHandler, FileHandler, RemoteHandler.
    • 

  • Logger: the client-facing entry point with debug()/info()/warn()/error() convenience methods; builds a LogMessage and pushes it into the chain head.
    • 

  • Formatter (interface): format(message) → string (SimpleFormatter, JsonFormatter).
    • 

  • Appender/Sink (interface): append(formattedText) (ConsoleSink, FileSink, NetworkSink).
    • 



Relationships


Logger composes (owns) the head LogHandler. Each LogHandler has-a next LogHandler (the chain — a self-referential association forming a linked list). A handler uses a Formatter and an Appender (association via interfaces) to render and emit. Concrete handlers inherit from LogHandler; concrete formatters/sinks inherit from their interfaces. Logger creates LogMessages passed by value/reference through the chain.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Chain of Responsibility (primary) — decouples the message producer from the set of handlers. Each handler decides independently whether the message is at or above its level and whether to forward. Adding/reordering handlers (e.g. insert a remote alerting handler for FATAL only) doesn't touch the Logger or other handlers.
    • 

  • Strategy for Formatter and Appender — formatting (plain vs JSON) and destination (file vs network) vary independently of routing.
    • 

  • Singleton for the Logger — one global, lazily-initialized logger is the common convenience.
    • 

  • Decorator (optional) for an AsyncHandler wrapping any handler to enqueue messages onto a background worker.
    • 



Key code


enum class LogLevel { DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL };

struct LogMessage {
    LogLevel level;
    std::string text;
    std::time_t timestamp;
};

class LogHandler {
public:
    explicit LogHandler(LogLevel threshold) : threshold(threshold) {}
    void setNext(std::shared_ptr<LogHandler> next) { this->next = next; }
    void handle(const LogMessage& message) {
        if (message.level >= threshold) write(message);
        if (next) next->handle(message);
    }
    virtual ~LogHandler() = default;
protected:
    virtual void write(const LogMessage& message) = 0;
    LogLevel threshold;
    std::shared_ptr<LogHandler> next;
};

class ConsoleHandler : public LogHandler {
public:
    ConsoleHandler(LogLevel t, std::shared_ptr<Formatter> f);
protected:
    void write(const LogMessage& message) override;
private:
    std::shared_ptr<Formatter> formatter;
};

class Logger {
public:
    static Logger& instance();
    void setChain(std::shared_ptr<LogHandler> head);
    void log(LogLevel level, const std::string& text);
    void info(const std::string& text)  { log(LogLevel::INFO, text); }
    void error(const std::string& text) { log(LogLevel::ERROR, text); }
private:
    std::shared_ptr<LogHandler> chainHead;
    std::mutex mtx;
};




Extensibility & SOLID


The chain is assembled once: e.g. console(DEBUG) → file(INFO) → remoteAlert(ERROR). A single ERROR message flows through all three; only handlers whose threshold it meets actually write. Note the handle template here both processes and forwards (fan-out) — a variant stops at the first matching handler if routing should be exclusive; that policy lives in handle alone.


SOLID payoffs: Open/Closed — a new SlackAlertHandler or MetricsHandler is a new subclass spliced into the chain; nothing existing changes. Single Responsibility — routing (handler), rendering (Formatter), and emission (Appender) are separate, so a file-rotation bug is contained to FileSink. Dependency Inversion — handlers depend on Formatter/Appender abstractions. Liskov — any handler is a valid chain link. Concurrency: guard sinks with a mutex, or wrap the chain head in an AsyncHandler (Decorator) that enqueues LogMessages to a single background consumer thread, keeping the caller non-blocking. Runtime level changes are just mutating a handler's threshold. This separation is exactly why CoR is the expected answer — it turns "which handlers see this message" into composition rather than conditionals.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Notification Service (Observer)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: What channels — email, SMS, push, in-app, webhook? Do users subscribe to event topics, or are notifications targeted directly? Per-user channel preferences (email yes, SMS no)? Delivery guarantees, retries on failure, rate limiting? Templating/personalization? Synchronous or async/queued delivery? Do we need delivery status tracking?


The canonical framing is Observer: a Subject (event source / topic) maintains a list of Observers (subscribers) and, when an event fires, notifies all of them. Each observer routes to a channel. In scope: topic-based subscription, multi-channel fan-out, per-user channel preference filtering, and pluggable channel senders. Out of scope: a full message broker, analytics.


Core entities / classes


    

  • Observer (interface): update(notification) — the subscriber contract. Implemented by per-user subscriptions or per-channel listeners.
    • 

  • Subject / NotificationTopic (interface): subscribe(observer), unsubscribe(observer), notifyAll(notification) — maintains the observer registry for one event type/topic.
    • 

  • NotificationService: the orchestrator. Maps topics to subjects, accepts publish(topic, payload), builds a Notification, and triggers notifyAll.
    • 

  • Notification: recipient, title, body, metadata.
    • 

  • Channel / NotificationSender (interface): send(user, notification). Concrete: EmailSender, SmsSender, PushSender, WebhookSender.
    • 

  • User: holds channelPreferences (which channels are enabled) and contact details.
    • 

  • UserObserver: an Observer bound to a User; on update, it consults the user's preferences and dispatches to the enabled NotificationSenders.
    • 



Relationships


NotificationService composes NotificationTopic/Subject instances (one per topic). Each Subject aggregates Observers (subscribers register/deregister; they live independently). A UserObserver references a User and uses one or more NotificationSenders (association via interface). Concrete senders inherit from NotificationSender; subjects and observers inherit from their interfaces. Notification is a value object passed by reference through notifyAll.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Observer (primary) — decouples event producers from consumers. The subject knows only the Observer interface, so subscribers can be added/removed at runtime without the publisher knowing who listens. This is the textbook fit for "notify everyone interested when X happens."
    • 

  • Strategy for NotificationSender — each channel is an interchangeable delivery strategy; per-user preference selects which strategies fire.
    • 

  • Factory to create senders by channel type.
    • 

  • Singleton for NotificationService as the central hub.
    • 

  • Decorator (optional) for RetryingSender or RateLimitedSender wrapping a base sender.
    • 



Key code


struct Notification {
    std::string recipientId;
    std::string title;
    std::string body;
};

class Observer {
public:
    virtual void update(const Notification& notification) = 0;
    virtual ~Observer() = default;
};

class Subject {
public:
    virtual void subscribe(std::shared_ptr<Observer> observer) = 0;
    virtual void unsubscribe(std::shared_ptr<Observer> observer) = 0;
    virtual void notifyAll(const Notification& notification) = 0;
    virtual ~Subject() = default;
};

class NotificationSender {
public:
    virtual void send(const User& user, const Notification& notification) = 0;
    virtual ~NotificationSender() = default;
};

class UserObserver : public Observer {
public:
    UserObserver(std::shared_ptr<User> user,
                 std::vector<std::shared_ptr<NotificationSender>> senders);
    void update(const Notification& notification) override;  // honors prefs
private:
    std::shared_ptr<User> user;
    std::vector<std::shared_ptr<NotificationSender>> senders;
};

class NotificationService {
public:
    static NotificationService& instance();
    void registerTopic(const std::string& topic,
                       std::shared_ptr<Subject> subject);
    void subscribe(const std::string& topic,
                   std::shared_ptr<Observer> observer);
    void publish(const std::string& topic, const Notification& notification);
private:
    std::unordered_map<std::string, std::shared_ptr<Subject>> topics;
};




Extensibility & SOLID


Flow: a producer calls service.publish("order_shipped", notification); the service finds the topic's Subject and calls notifyAll, which iterates its observers calling update. Each UserObserver.update checks the user's channelPreferences and forwards only to enabled senders — so the same event reaches one user by email+push and another by SMS only.


SOLID payoffs: Open/Closed — a new WhatsAppSender channel is a new NotificationSender subclass plus a factory entry; subjects, observers, and the service are untouched. Likewise a new topic just registers a new Subject. Single Responsibility — subscription management (Subject), preference-based routing (UserObserver), and delivery (Sender) are distinct. Dependency Inversion — the service and observers depend on the Subject/Observer/NotificationSender abstractions, never on concrete channels. Liskov — any sender substitutes for NotificationSender. For reliability, wrap senders in a RetryingSender decorator and push delivery onto a background queue so notifyAll returns fast; rate limiting attaches the same way. This Observer-centric decomposition is why interviewers expect it — runtime subscribe/unsubscribe and fan-out become first-class instead of hard-wired conditionals.


← System Design · LLD · Object-Oriented Design
Rate Limiter (Token Bucket)
Requirements & clarifying questions


Clarify: Per-user, per-API-key, per-IP, or global limits? What algorithm — fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, leaky bucket? Should it allow short bursts (token bucket) or strictly smooth traffic (leaky bucket)? Single-process or distributed (multiple app servers sharing one limit)? What happens on limit breach — reject (HTTP 429), queue, or throttle? Thread-safe under concurrency? Configurable limits per client tier?


The expected focus is the token bucket class: each client has a bucket of capacity C that refills at R tokens/second; a request consumes one token if available, else it's rejected. Token bucket is favored because it permits bursts up to C while bounding the long-run rate to R. In scope: a thread-safe token-bucket class, a per-client registry, a pluggable algorithm interface, and a note on the distributed variant. Out of scope: a full API gateway.


Core entities / classes


    

  • RateLimiter (interface): allowRequest(clientId) → bool. The strategy seam.
    • 

  • TokenBucket: the core — capacity, refillRatePerSec, availableTokens (fractional), lastRefillTimestamp. allow() lazily refills based on elapsed time, then tries to consume one token.
    • 

  • TokenBucketRateLimiter: owns a registry clientId → TokenBucket, creating buckets lazily; delegates allowRequest to the client's bucket.
    • 

  • Other strategies: SlidingWindowRateLimiter, FixedWindowRateLimiter, LeakyBucketRateLimiter — alternative implementations of the same interface.
    • 

  • RateLimiterConfig: per-tier capacity/rate (free vs premium).
    • 

  • Clock (interface): injectable time source for testability (SystemClock, FakeClock).
    • 



Relationships


TokenBucketRateLimiter composes a map of TokenBuckets (buckets are owned by the limiter and created per client). It uses a Clock and a RateLimiterConfig (association via interface/value). TokenBucketRateLimiter implements the RateLimiter interface, as do the other algorithms (inheritance). A web filter/middleware uses a RateLimiter (association) and never knows which algorithm backs it.


Design patterns applied


    

  • Strategy (primary) — the rate-limiting algorithm is the varying behavior; token bucket, leaky bucket, and sliding window are interchangeable behind RateLimiter. Switching from fixed window to token bucket becomes a config change, not a rewrite.
    • 

  • Factory to build the configured limiter (RateLimiterFactory::create(config)).
    • 

  • Singleton for the limiter registry so all request threads share bucket state.
    • 

  • Dependency Injection of Clock — the lazy-refill computation depends entirely on elapsed time, so injecting the clock makes the time-sensitive logic unit-testable without sleeps.
    • 



Key code


class RateLimiter {
public:
    virtual bool allowRequest(const std::string& clientId) = 0;
    virtual ~RateLimiter() = default;
};

class TokenBucket {
public:
    TokenBucket(double capacity, double refillRatePerSec);
    bool allow();
private:
    void refill();
    double capacity;
    double refillRatePerSec;
    double availableTokens;
    std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point lastRefill;
    std::mutex mtx;
};

bool TokenBucket::allow() {
    std::lock_guard<std::mutex> guard(mtx);
    refill();
    if (availableTokens >= 1.0) {
        availableTokens -= 1.0;
        return true;
    }
    return false;
}

void TokenBucket::refill() {
    auto now = std::chrono::steady_clock::now();
    double elapsed = std::chrono::duration<double>(now - lastRefill).count();
    availableTokens = std::min(capacity,
                               availableTokens + elapsed * refillRatePerSec);
    lastRefill = now;
}

class TokenBucketRateLimiter : public RateLimiter {
public:
    TokenBucketRateLimiter(double capacity, double refillRatePerSec);
    bool allowRequest(const std::string& clientId) override;
private:
    double capacity;
    double refillRatePerSec;
    std::unordered_map<std::string, TokenBucket> buckets;
    std::mutex registryMtx;
};




Token-bucket mechanics, concurrency & SOLID


The elegance of token bucket is lazy refill: rather than a background timer adding tokens, refill() computes how many tokens should have accrued since lastRefill (elapsed × rate), caps at capacity, and updates the timestamp — O(1), no timer thread. availableTokens is a double so partial refills accumulate correctly between sub-second requests. Burst handling falls out naturally: a client idle for a while can spend up to capacity tokens at once, then is throttled to the steady refillRatePerSec.


Concurrency: each TokenBucket guards its refill-and-consume with its own mutex (the check-and-decrement must be atomic, or two threads both see the last token). The registry uses a separate lock only for lazy bucket creation, so per-client request paths don't contend with each other. For a distributed limiter, the in-memory bucket is replaced by a Redis-backed implementation — store tokens + timestamp per key and run the refill-and-consume as an atomic Lua script (or use INCR with expiry for a fixed window) — without changing the RateLimiter interface.


SOLID: Open/Closed — adding LeakyBucketRateLimiter or a Redis variant is a new class implementing RateLimiter; callers are untouched (Dependency Inversion — middleware depends on the interface). Single Responsibility — TokenBucket does accounting, the limiter does client routing, the factory does construction. Per-tier limits live in RateLimiterConfig, so a premium tier is data, not code. Injecting Clock keeps the refill math deterministic in tests — the one piece of logic most likely to harbor subtle bugs.
